blurb: Mount Goode is the highest summit in North Cascades National Park at 9,200 feet and the fourth-highest non-volcanic peak in Washington. Located in the remote heart of the North Cascades and invisible from any road, this granite and gneiss peak features multiple alpine rock and ice climbing routes ranging from Class 4 to Grade IV+ 5.10.
hierarchyNote: Peak data verified: Mount Goode, 9,200-9,220 ft, coordinates 48°28'58"N 120°54'39"W, Chelan County, North Cascades, Washington. Hierarchy and location confirmed across multiple primary sources (Wikipedia, SummitPost, Mountain Project, WTA).

routes.corrections: Route name 'Gunsight Pass' could not be verified in primary sources (mountainproject.com, summitpost.org, peakbagger.com). Gunsight is a separate peak range in the North Cascades; no documented climbing route by this name was found on Mount Goode. This may be an error or refer to an undocumented approach.

Route name 'North Ridge' could not be verified in primary sources for Mount Goode in Washington. A 'North Ridge' was found on Mount Goode in Alaska's Chugach Mountains (10,610 ft), first ascent April 11, 2022 by Matt Kogel and Travis Powell. No primary source documentation found for a 'North Ridge' route on the Washington peak.

routes.overview: The earliest climbing route on Mount Goode, likely incorporating the Southeast Ridge as part of the approach. Class 4 terrain with mixed rock, snow, and ice.
routes.beta: The least technical route on Mount Goode, featuring class 4 terrain with extensive snow and loose rock throughout. This line was the first ascent of the peak and remains occasionally used, though less popular than the Northeast Buttress.
routes.approach: Approach from Rainy Pass via the Pacific Crest Trail southbound along Bridge Creek, then branch to North Fork Bridge Creek Trail approximately 10 miles. Cross Goode Glacier and ascend via the Southwest Couloir to the Southeast Ridge portion. Alternative approach via Stehekin.
routes.descent: Rappel descent via the Southwest Couloir with multiple rappel stations. Estimated 5-6 rappels of up to 30 meters each.
routes.descentText: Descend approximately 10 feet on the southwest side of the summit notch to reach rappel anchor stations at the top of the Southwest Couloir. Make multiple rappels (typically 5-6) to 30 meters down the couloir, navigating steep snow and loose scree. Traverse back across Goode Glacier and descend to Bridge Creek via the approach route.
routes.itinerary: Day 1-2: Approach via PCT and Bridge Creek Trail to camping area (approximately 10 miles, 8-10 hours). Day 3: Climb Goode Glacier and ascend Southeast Ridge/Southwest Couloir to summit (8-10 hours). Day 4: Descend to camp and rest. Day 5: Hike out.
routes.bail: If conditions become hazardous, retreat back down the Southeast Ridge/Southwest Couloir. Escape down Goode Glacier to Bridge Creek valley. In case of injury, descent via rappels to glacier base should take 6-8 hours.
routes.turnaround: Route should be abandoned if conditions include unstable snow, active rockfall, or deteriorating weather. Most parties turn around by early afternoon summit time to have daylight for rappel descent.
routes.bestSeason: Late July through August. Earlier in season, excessive snow makes glacier travel difficult; later in season, alpine weather becomes unstable and loose rock hazards increase.
routes.comms: Cell service extremely limited or non-existent in the Bridge Creek drainage and at altitude on the mountain. Satellite messenger or personal locator beacon recommended for emergency communication.
routes.detailedRack: Standard alpine rock rack: single set of cams (0.4-3 inches), limited pitons, approximately 8-10 quick draws. Helmet essential due to loose rock. 60-meter rope recommended.
routes.proNeeds: Minimal protection placements; route is mostly scrambling. A few anchor points needed for belayed sections and rappel stations (typically fixed bolts exist at established stations).
proTips: Getting off the glacier onto the route is often the crux—watch for snow moats that can be difficult to cross
July conditions are optimal with moderate temperatures and stable snow
Consider bringing a lightweight snow shovel for snow bridge crossing
Establish camp away from avalanche paths in Bridge Creek drainage
watchOut: Rockfall from loose granite—constant hazard throughout descent
Crevasses on Goode Glacier, especially late season
Snow moats at glacier terminus create dangerous gaps
Exposure on narrow summit ridge sections
knownHazards: Loose, poor-quality rock throughout upper sections
Avalanche terrain on approach and descent
Rapidly changing alpine weather can create whiteout conditions
Steep snow and ice sections require careful frontpointing

Mixed snow and rock, loose terrain throughout

routes.corrections: Route name 'Southeast Ridge' may refer to the Southwest Couloir/Southeast Ridge first ascent line (also called Southwest Couloir in modern sources). Primary historical sources cite this as the first ascent route (1936), though the modern name emphasizes the Southwest Couloir approach. This route is documented as Class 4 and remains the least technical summit route.

Route name 'Direct North Face' could not be verified in primary sources (mountainproject.com, summitpost.org, peakbagger.com). While the north face is frequently referenced as an imposing feature (6,000+ feet of relief), no documented climbing route specifically called 'Direct North Face' was found. This may be an undocumented or proposed route.

Route name 'West Face' could not be verified in primary sources (mountainproject.com, summitpost.org, peakbagger.com). While some sources reference terrain west of Mount Goode, no documented climbing route specifically called 'West Face' on the Washington peak was found. The three primary documented routes are Northeast Buttress, Southwest Couloir/Southeast Ridge, and Megalodon Ridge.

blurb: Mount Shuksan (9,131 ft) is one of America's most photographed peaks, dominating the North Cascades east of Mount Baker with its iconic pyramid summit. Rising dramatically from the Cascade Range, this metamorphic schist peak offers the full spectrum of alpine climbing from beginner glacier routes to technical mixed climbing and ice ascents. First summited by Asahel Curtis and W. Montelius Price in 1906, the mountain remains a classic training ground for North American mountaineers.
hierarchyNote: CRITICAL: The provided route IDs do not correspond to documented routes in authoritative sources (Mountain Project, SummitPost, NPS). Route wa_shuksan_nisqually_glacier appears to be a data error (Nisqually Glacier is on Mount Rainier, not Shuksan). Routes wa_shuksan_cont_divide, wa_shuksan_crystal_traverse do not appear in any primary source. Only wa_shuksan_north_face and wa_shuksan_summit_pyramid have partial correspondence to documented features. Documented routes per Mountain Project/NPS: Sulphide Glacier (most popular), Fisher Chimneys, Price Glacier, North Face, White Salmon Glacier, Southeast Ridge. Recommend data reconciliation before deployment.

routes.overview: The final 600-foot scramble to Mount Shuksan's summit from the base of the pyramid, featuring excellent quality metamorphic rock and class 3-4 terrain with low 5th class sections on the upper pitches. Standard finish route from glacier approaches.
routes.beta: Most parties use the central gully route, which features bomber class 3 rock with good protection options, progressing through easier 4th class terrain before encountering low class 5 moves on the final three pitches. The Southeast Ridge variant offers more technical climbing. Rock quality is excellent throughout, and the climbing is pleasant and well-protected.
routes.approach: All routes begin from various glacier bases (typically Sulphide Glacier, Fisher Chimneys approach, or Price Glacier), which deposit climbers at approximately 6,800-7,600 feet. The pyramid base sits between the Sulphide Glacier and White Salmon Glacier approach zones.
routes.descent: Rappel the 4th class gully using 60-meter rope to reach all belay stations. Parties often downclimb or rappel back to the glacier base (6,500-6,800 ft), then reverse approach route.
routes.descentText: From the summit, descend via the central gully using rappel anchors already in place. Deploy 60-meter ropes with directional anchors to reach each belay. The gully is class 4 downclimb terrain; many parties rappel the entire descent to avoid the complexity of downclimbing exposed terrain. Once at the gully base (~6,800 ft), reverse the glacier route to high camp.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Trailhead to High Camp (glacier base ~6,500-6,800 ft), 5-6 hours, 3,000-4,000 ft gain. Day 2: High Camp to Summit via glacier approach and pyramid gully/ridge, 5-6 hours, 2,300-2,600 ft gain. Day 3: Descent to high camp, 3-4 hours; return to trailhead, 3-5 hours.
routes.bail: From the summit gully: retreat downclimb/rappel to glacier base at 6,800 ft and reverse glacier route. From upper pitches: use existing belay anchors to lower off route. Avoid pyramid entirely if corniced or iced in late season.
routes.turnaround: Typical turnaround for summit day is mid-afternoon from base camp to ensure safe descent in daylight. Parties not reaching the pyramid base by 1 PM should consider returning.
routes.bestSeason: July through October are ideal months to climb the summit pyramid. Late July to August offers prime conditions with the best balance of stable weather and minimal avalanche hazard. June attempts risk deep snow and crevasse hazards; early summer ice may require ice tools for the gully. October approaches require careful assessment of snow/ice conditions and rime ice on rock.
routes.comms: Cell service unreliable above 6,000 ft; satellite communicator (Garmin InReach, SPOT) recommended. Local radio frequencies for wilderness emergencies can be obtained at Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount. No guaranteed cell coverage in North Cascades; relying on cell phone alone is not recommended.
routes.detailedRack: Single-pitch style trad rack: double length slings for ridge protection, stoppers to 1.5 inches, or climb unroped if experienced. Light alpine rack with minimal pieces; most parties carry doubles on small cams and nuts. Helmet essential due to rockfall hazard from other parties.
routes.proNeeds: Protection is optional for experienced climbers; many parties climb unroped in favorable conditions. If protecting: 1-2 half-ropes or single 60m rope sufficient. Anchors are solid metamorphic schist with excellent gear-placement options throughout. Belay anchors already established in gully.
whatToBring: Warm layers (fleece, hardshell jacket, insulated parka)
Sun protection (glacier glasses, sun hat, high-SPF sunscreen)
proTips: Central gully is standard; well-established boot tracks and belay anchors make route-finding straightforward
High rock quality allows flexible, creative protection options
Route typically snow-free by late July; fall conditions present rime ice hazard
Other parties on the pyramid create rockfall risk; wear helmet and maintain awareness
Descend gully quickly to minimize exposure to afternoon rockfall
Late-season (September-October) may require ice tools and screws for the summit gully
watchOut: Rockfall from climbers above: constant hazard; helmets essential

Corniced summit in winter/spring: avoid pyramid in early season
Exposure on upper pitches; loose rock on some sections
Afternoon rockfall and weather deterioration: start early

Late-season ice on gully may require ice tools
Exposure to steep terrain with run-out potential

climate.forecastZone: Northwest Avalanche Center: West Slopes North zone
climate.typical: Alpine conditions: temperatures range 30-50°F in summer, below 20°F in early/late season. Strong winds common above 8,000 ft. Rime ice forms frequently due to orographic moisture.
climate.spring: Deep snow (6+ ft), high avalanche hazard (NWAC monitoring required). Crevasses heavily concealed. Not recommended; approach is extremely committing.
climate.summer: Ideal conditions: stable weather patterns, minimal avalanche hazard. Temperatures 40-50°F during day, near freezing at night. Morning starts crucial to avoid afternoon thunderstorms. Crevasse rescue gear essential due to visible crevasses.
climate.fall: Unpredictable: early season (Sep) resembles summer; late season (Oct) brings freeze-thaw, rime ice, and increased rockfall. Temperatures drop to 20-30°F overnight. Storm risk increases.
climate.winter: Not recommended. Extreme avalanche hazard, deep snow, severe weather, temperatures well below 0°F, limited daylight. Only experienced mountaineers with specialized equipment should attempt.

routes.overview: A glacier traverse route using the Crystal Glacier as part of the approach to reach the summit pyramid. This involves navigating crevassed glacier terrain and requires glacier travel skills.
routes.beta: The Crystal Glacier is one of the nine named glaciers on Mount Shuksan. Access to the glacier and traverse across it is typically part of more complex approaches that loop around multiple glaciers. Crystal Glacier approaches are less commonly documented than the standard Sulphide or Fisher Chimneys routes.
routes.approach: Approach varies; typically involves reaching the Crystal Glacier from the Hanging Glacier saddle area. The glacier can be reached from Upper Curtis Glacier traverse, with traverses between the Hanging Glacier and Crystal Glacier saddle.
routes.descent: Reverse the glacier route, or link to Sulphide Glacier for descent. Multiple glacier combinations possible depending on party preference and conditions.
routes.bestSeason: Mid-to-late summer (July-September) when crevasses are visible and weather is most stable.
routes.comms: No reliable cell service; satellite communicator essential
knownHazards: Crevasses on Crystal Glacier (may be concealed in early season)
Rockfall from surrounding peaks

routes.overview: Mount Shuksan's most dramatic and technical route. The North Face climbs 45-50 degree snow and ice slopes rising from the White Salmon cirque, topping out onto the shoulder between the Crystal and Hanging Glaciers. This is the iconic view of Shuksan seen on postcards and calendars.
routes.beta: Starting from White Salmon Lodge trailhead, the approach descends to White Salmon Creek and traverses toward White Salmon Glacier. In good snow conditions the route is straightforward; without snow cover, contour around clearcut areas to avoid dense brush. The face climb ascends ±45 degree continuous snow, steepening to 50 degrees approaching the shoulder. Crevasse rescue gear essential. Late-season schrund at base may obstruct access. Expect 5,500 ft of vertical climbing.
routes.approach: Start at White Salmon Lodge trailhead (off SR 542 past Mt. Baker Ski Area). Descend to White Salmon Creek and traverse toward White Salmon Glacier base (approximately 4,500-5,000 ft). Without snow cover, contour around the clearcut on the north side to avoid dense brush, stream crossings, and visibility issues. Approach gain is approximately 1,500 ft to glacier base.
routes.descent: Reverse the climbing route (descend the North Face directly) or traverse White Salmon Glacier for descent. The route description indicates preference for descending White Salmon Glacier, providing exposure to new terrain on return rather than retracing the ascent.
routes.descentText: Most parties descend via White Salmon Glacier rather than reversing the North Face. From the shoulder (8,600 ft), descend the White Salmon Glacier southwest back to the glacier base and reverse the approach route to White Salmon Lodge. This descent typically takes 2-3 hours for experienced parties.
routes.bestSeason: July and August offer the best conditions with stable snow and manageable crevasse hazards. Early July can have soft, post-holing conditions; late August approaches winter-like conditions with rime ice. September and later becomes increasingly dangerous due to freeze-thaw cycles and rime.
routes.comms: No cell service in approach valley; satellite communicator mandatory for safety communications.
routes.detailedRack: Alpine ice climbing gear: crampons, ice axe (potentially two tools for steeper sections), ice screws (8-10 screws recommended for belayed pitches), quickdraws, carabiners, rappel/lowering slings. Crevasse rescue equipment: prusiks, cordelette, pulleys for self-rescue.
routes.proNeeds: Protection on the face varies by snow/ice conditions and chosen line. Early season: primarily snow anchors and pickets. Late season: ice screws and ice anchors essential. Some parties climb unprotected on moderate angle with excellent crampon technique; others belay for security.
whatToBring: Full insulation system (synthetic, no down in wet conditions)
proTips: Timing is critical: early morning start (4-5 AM) to reach summit by early afternoon before conditions deteriorate
Soft snow conditions are dangerous; frozen morning conditions (common in late July/August) provide faster, safer climbing
Route-finding can be obscured by whiteout conditions; mark descent route carefully or rappel with anchors
Constantly assess conditions above; active Hanging Glacier produces large avalanches that sweep the approach valley
Watch the barometer: pressure drops indicate incoming storms; turn back if conditions worsen
watchOut: Active avalanches from Hanging Glacier above: constant hazard; assess debris and visible slides
Late-season rime ice: can make 45° snow feel like steep ice; requires ice tools and screws
Crevasses with weak snow bridges: most critical hazard on glacier sections
Sudden weather changes: severe storms can develop in 30 minutes; monitor sky constantly
Schrund at face base: may be impassable late in season; early season attempts may require bridging or cutting
knownHazards: Large avalanches from Hanging Glacier sweep the approach valley; stay alert to debris and active slides
Late-season rime ice on face; can transform benign 45° snow into WI2-3 climbing
Deep crevasses on glacier approaches; rescue team not available
Exposure: long run-out fall potential on 50° slopes
Sudden whiteout in storms; compass and altimeter essential for navigation

hierarchyNote: ELEVATION DISCREPANCY: Database lists 9,050 ft, but all authoritative sources (Mountain Project, Summit Post, Wikipedia, PeakVisor, Peakbagger) confirm 8,285 feet (2,525 m). blurb: Nooksack Tower (8,285 feet) is an outlier of Mount Shuksan Massif in North Cascades National Park, positioned 0.75 miles northeast of Shuksan's main summit. Composed of loose, poor-quality rock and surrounded by three glaciers (Price, East Nooksack, and West Nooksack), it ranks as one of the most difficult technical mountains in the Cascade Range. Fred Beckey and Clifford Schmidtke made the first ascent in 1946, establishing a route that today sees approximately one ascent per season on average.

routes.fa: Fred Beckey and Clifford Schmidtke, July 5, 1946
routes.overview: The original and standard ascent route on Nooksack Tower via the north face, combining glacier travel, ice couloir climbing, and loose fourth-class rock scrambling on the north arête to the summit.
routes.beta: Start from the bergschrund at approximately 6,500 feet and climb 800 feet up a 50-degree ice couloir on the north face. Work westward into a hidden rock trough and transition to fourth-class rock scrambling (hardest pitch ~5.4) along the north arête for the final 1,200 feet to the summit. All pitches require careful belaying. One safety piton used on first ascent; modern parties use rock protection and ice screws.
routes.approach: From Glacier, WA, drive Mount Baker Highway (Hwy 542) east 13 miles to Nooksack River bridge. Turn left on USFS Road 32 (Nooksack River Rd) and continue to washout at 2.3 miles (park at Ruth Creek, 2,156 ft). Hike abandoned road 2 miles to Nooksack Cirque trailhead (2,550 ft). Ford the Nooksack River (typically on a log; knee-deep to waist-deep depending on season; late spring/summer requires the log crossing). Proceed up forested mountainside east of creek, staying on ridge crest that develops above Price Lake (3,911 ft). Continue up moraine above Price Lake to bivouac area at ~5,900 feet on flat rock with gentle slope leading to Price Glacier. Round the north side of the lower glacier and ascend to the bergschrund at base of the north face ice couloir (~6,500 ft). Total approach time from trailhead: 8-12 hours depending on water levels and fitness.
routes.descent: Descend via 10 or more 60-meter rappels from the summit block. First rappel is off a stack of rocks from the summit block. Include rappel off deadman picket in the upper couloir. Simulclimb remaining couloir in darkness if on 1-day push. Alternatively, some parties have descended the south face route. Exercise extreme caution on first rappel (one climber noted if you fell here you'd end up in Price Lake).
routes.descentText: From the summit, descend directly down the north face via rappels. Set rappel anchors carefully as rock quality is poor. Many parties rappel at night due to the extent of descent required. Descend the north couloir via alternating rappels and simulclimbing. Be prepared for loose rock and poor anchor options. At least 10 sixty-meter rappels needed to reach the glacier. Once on the glacier, down-climb carefully to avoid crevasses. Return via Price Glacier and the approach route (mirror image of ascent). Total descent time: 4-6 hours from summit to camp.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Drive to Ruth Creek trailhead (2,156 ft). Hike 2 miles on abandoned road to Nooksack Cirque trailhead (2,550 ft). Ford Nooksack River. Ascend through forest to moraine above Price Lake (3,911 ft). Continue to bivouac area at ~5,900 feet (8-12 hours total). Day 2: Ascend Price Glacier and traverse to bergschrund (~2-3 hours). Climb north face ice couloir and rock sections to summit (7-8 hours). Return to bivouac via rappels and descent (4-6 hours). Day 3: Descend via approach route to trailhead (6-8 hours).
routes.bail: If turning back, retreat as soon as conditions worsen. The route is serious and exposed; early retreat is preferable to being caught on the descent in darkness. Descent of the couloir can be downclimbed if rappel anchors fail, but extreme care needed. Alternative bailout: do not commit to the summit if weather deteriorates or if you haven't summited by 1-2 PM due to descent time requirements (10+ hours to return to camp). Consider bivying on the glacier if necessary.
routes.turnaround: Plan to reach the bergschrund by 9-10 AM to allow sufficient daylight for the 10+ hour descent sequence. If you haven't topped out by 1-2 PM, seriously consider rappelling from your high point and descending the north face rather than pushing to the summit.
routes.bestSeason: June through September, with August offering the most stable conditions. July-August has the most daylight hours and typically the best weather windows. June can have excessive snow; September brings increased storm frequency. Most ascents occur in July-August during anti-cyclone weather patterns.
routes.comms: Cell coverage is unreliable in the Nooksack Cirque area due to terrain blocking. No cell signal expected in the cirque or on the glacier. Consider satellite communicator (InReach, Garmin) for emergency communication. Some climbers report possible weak signals from Skagit Valley towers at high elevation passes, but this is not reliable.

climate.forecastZone: National Weather Service Bellingham, WA zone
climate.typical: Marine west coast climate; storms originate in Pacific and push northeast. West side of North Cascades receives high precipitation, especially winter. Summer (Jul-Aug) typically clearer with fewer storms.
climate.spring: May-June: significant snowfall possible on approach and higher elevations. Forest muddy from snowmelt. River fords treacherous. Warm afternoons but cold mornings and nights.
climate.summer: July-September: warmest and most stable period. July high avg 75°F in lowlands, significantly cooler at elevation (~30-40°F at 8,000 ft). Afternoon thunderstorms possible. Glaciers most crevassed late summer.
climate.fall: September-October: increasing storm frequency. Early season storms can bring snow to 8,000+ ft. Temperatures dropping. Visibility often poor.
climate.winter: November-February: heavy snowfall west side (upward of 300 inches annually on western slopes). Peak snow accumulation January. Much of route snow-covered; winter ascents rare and require specialized skills.

access.permit: Backcountry Use Permit required (free) for all overnight camping in North Cascades National Park backcountry. Available year-round; reserve via Recreation.gov (60% of permits) or obtain in-person at Glacier Ranger Station (40% for walk-ups) the day of trip.
access.closures: North Cascades National Park is open year-round. Backcountry access may be restricted during emergency closures or severe avalanche conditions (uncommon). pitchDetail.notes: Forest travel from Price Lake moraine to glacier junction; steep but straightforward.
50-degree ice couloir on north face, 800 feet vertical. Rock-hard ice early season, running water/thin ice late season. All pitches belayed.

Rock trough and north arête. Poor quality, loose blocks. Expect rock fall hazard from parties above. Simulation climbing often used. Hardest individual pitch around 5.4 but generally sustained Class 4.
Final approach to summit block. Steeper exposure but shorter pitches.

gear: Rock protection: sets of nuts and cams (size 0.3-2 inches)
routes.detailedRack: Single rope (60m minimum, 70m preferred for rappelling long pitches). Bring nut tool. Protection rack: one set each of nuts (sizes 3-10) and cams (0.3-2 inch). Include redundant smaller cams (0.3, 0.4, 0.5) for thin rock sections. Add 5-6 slings (Metolius or Black Diamond 16/18-inch) and cordelette (7-8mm, 6 feet). For ice: 3-5 ice screws (Sidewinder or Black Crevasse, mix of 10cm and 13cm). Rappel setup: 8-10 carabiners for anchors, several quicklinks for expedited rappel anchor conversions. Two ice axes and crampons per climber.
routes.proNeeds: Rock protection limited by poor quality. Focus on bomber placements where possible; expect many marginal pro options. Common practice is to place protection at least every 30-50 feet on rock pitches, though finding good placements is challenging. Many parties simulclimb less-steep sections and carefully place pro on exposed sections. Ice screws provide primary protection on couloir; use 1-2 per belay.
proTips: Plan a predawn start (3-5 AM from bivouac) to maximize daylight for the long descent.
Scout the bergschrund approach before committing; late season bergschrunds can be large and require traversing.
Bring a deadman picket kit for rappel anchor in the upper couloir; rock anchors there are unreliable.
Simul-climb less technical sections to save time; the descent is the real time crunch.
Use dark rock as landmarks to navigate the north face couloir (it's the biggest gully on the north side).
Consider a 9mm tag line on a biner for rappelling in extreme cases to move faster, though requires skill.
Post-monsoon (late July-August) often has more stable weather; June and September storms more frequent.
Many parties bivy at 5,900 feet the night before and make a dawn push; doing the whole climb in a single push from the road takes 20-24 hours.
watchOut: Nooksack River ford can be dangerously high in early morning melt or after heavy rain; wade in late afternoon if possible, or just after sunrise when flows are lowest.
Bergschrund can be large and difficult to cross; may require rappelling in or down-climbing into, which adds time and complexity.
Loose rock is a constant hazard; wear helmet at all times, check each handhold, and avoid climbing directly below other parties.
Upper couloir has rockfall hazard from natural icefall and sloughing ice as it melts through the day; climb early.
Rappel anchors are often marginal; inspect each carefully before committing weight.
Exposure on north arête is significant; simulate-climbing unroped increases objective hazard.
Crevasses on Price Glacier can be large and hidden; rope up before starting glacier travel, and watch for signs of crevasse fields.
Late-summer crevasses are more open and visible; early-season crevasses are better hidden under snow (both are serious hazards).
knownHazards: Loose, poor-quality rock throughout the route creates constant rockfall hazard and unpredictable pro placements.
50-60 degree north face couloir provides steep ice climbing with avalanche potential during or immediately after storms.
Extensive crevassing on Price Glacier, particularly large crevasse spanning the entire glacier width (~50 feet) reported; late-season ice climbing overhanging bergschrunds rated AI-5.
Exposure on final north arête with 1,000+ foot falls to glacier below if anchors fail.
Route-finding in fog is difficult; whiteouts can trap climbers in complex terrain.
Descent is lengthy (10+ hours) and many rappels create time pressure and rappel-related accidents (anchor failure, rappelling off ends).
Altitude not extreme but altitude gain (6,129 feet) combined with technical climbing and gear carrying creates dehydration and fatigue.
Weather deterioration rapid; afternoon thunderstorms common in July-August; lightning hazard on summit block.
River ford high in early morning and after rain; entrapment on wrong side of river possible.

road.name: Mount Baker Highway (WA-542 to USFS Road 32)
road.status: Open year-round; east of Glacier may close in winter
road.seasonalGate: USFS Road 32 (Nooksack River Road) may have seasonal closures due to snow or maintenance; verify with Glacier Ranger Station or USFS before attempting in winter or spring
road.driveNote: Drive Mount Baker Highway (WA-542) east from I-5 through Bellingham and Glacier. At Glacier, continue east on Hwy 542 approximately 13 miles to Nooksack River bridge. Immediately before bridge, turn left onto USFS Road 32 (Nooksack River Road). Road is rough gravel with potential washouts. Smaller vehicles (sedans) should proceed cautiously; high-clearance vehicle recommended. Road is not plowed in winter. Drive to washout at mile 2.3 and park at Ruth Creek (elevation 2,156 feet). Total drive from Bellingham: ~1.5-2 hours. Road conditions variable; check current status with ranger station.

emergency.rangerStation: Glacier Ranger Station, North Cascades National Park: (360) 599-2714 (located on Hwy 542 between Glacier and Concrete, WA). Open seasonally with extended hours in summer.
emergency.nearestHospital: Skagit Valley Hospital (Mount Vernon, WA): (360) 814-4000. Approximately 45 minutes from the trailhead. United General Medical Center (Sedro-Woolley, WA): (360) 856-6001. Approximately 30 minutes from trailhead.
emergency.notes: Cell coverage unreliable in the Nooksack Cirque area; no coverage expected in cirque or on glacier. Satellite communicator (InReach/Garmin) highly recommended for emergency contact. Helicopter evacuation possible but weather-dependent and extremely expensive (insurance recommended). USFS and NPS are coordinated for search and rescue; report overdue parties through Glacier Ranger Station or County Sheriff.

routes.overview: Alternative ridge approach to Nooksack Tower from the west side, though this route is less frequently documented in modern climbing literature than the north and south faces.
routes.beta: Approach via Dailey Prairie to a ridge of solid golden dunite. Route slowly transitions from exposed hiking through stands of fir trees into sporting alpine scrambling. Follow ridge crest with easy route-finding. Tower crux section requires finding a fixed pin in the vertical section; most parties belay this section. Descent down north slope requires ice axe and crampons.
routes.descent: Descent of north slope with ice axe and crampons required. Helmets always recommended.

routes.fa: Charles Bell, Ed Cooper, David Hiser, and Mike Swayne; 1961 (III 5.7 line of least resistance)
routes.overview: The North Buttress climbs the right (west) of two ribs in the north face, offering 2,500 feet of predominantly Class 3-4 terrain with mixed rock and snow. A direct and sustained alpine rock climb with crux near the top of a chimney midway up the face.
routes.beta: This is the north face of Mount Terror, approaching via McMillan Cirque. The route mostly Class 3-4 with crux near top of chimney midway up face. Snow field adjacent to north face rarely sees sun and gets icy; axe and crampons mandatory. The first ascent party (1961) climbed a line of least resistance to the right of the crest at III 5.7. A 1984 variant called the Stoddard Buttress follows a more direct, cleaner line right on the crest at III 5.8.
routes.approach: Approach via Goodell Creek trailhead (turn north across Highway 20 from Newhalem campground). Hike old forestry road for 4.5 miles at 600 ft elevation. Continue via Terror Creek trail to Terror Basin. From Crescent Creek Camp, traverse into McMillan Cirque. Approximately 6,000 feet elevation gain to base camp.
routes.descent: Descend via West Ridge to Blob-Terror notch, then descend gully to Crescent Creek Cirque via loose downclimbing and rappels.
routes.bestSeason: July through September, with August being optimal. July is good but buggier. September requires care due to fall snow and shorter daylight. By late August, most seasonal snow is gone.

routes.overview: The East Ridge is a Class 5.6 alpine rock route, described as probably the least popular route on Mount Terror. Four pitches ascend the ridge with mixed rock and scrambling to the summit.
routes.beta: Ascend approximately 4 pitches with Class 5.6 crux climbing. The route traverses to the south face over loose rock before wrapping up again for pitches with 5th class rock mixed in. Roughly 2 pitches of low 5th class climbing with decent rock.
routes.approach: Either hike up Terror Creek trail to saddle near Chopping Block, or cross the Barrier from McMillan Spire and Inspiration Peak Basecamp area. From ridge walk east away from Chopping Block, then drop down into valley traversing scree and talus to base of East Ridge. Approximately 1 hour to base of climb from ridge crest.

routes.fa: William Degenhardt and Herbert Strandberg; 1932 (peak first ascent; this route is most likely their descent/ascent line)
routes.overview: The West Ridge is the standard and least technically difficult route on Mount Terror. It combines low 5th class rock climbing with Class 3-4 scrambling on a long, exposed ridge, gaining the summit from the west side.
routes.beta: This is the 'easiest' route to summit Terror, least technical but still requiring considerable physical exertion. The route involves ascending a steep, loose gully to a notch, then scrambling/climbing the west ridge with Class 3-4 terrain to the summit. Established belay/rappel anchors spaced ~25 meters apart exist on lower section.
routes.approach: From Crescent Creek Camp (6+ hours approach from trailhead), traverse to base of Terror Couloir or approach the west-side notch via the hideously steep choss gully. Total approach approximately 6,000 feet elevation gain from Goodell Creek trailhead.
routes.descent: Reverse via the west ridge, downclimbing Class 3-4, with optional rappels at established anchors.
routes.descentText: For return, downclimb the summit tower or rappel a couple spots, then hike back to first notch. Loose rock makes downclimbing less pleasant than climbing; rappel anchors exist on summit block, at first pitch, and throughout the gully for safer descent.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Goodell Creek trailhead to Crescent Creek Camp (6,000 ft gain, 2 days for most parties). Day 2: Camp to summit and return. Total 2-3 days minimum.
routes.bail: If conditions deteriorate high on ridge, traverse west to Blob-Terror notch and descend gully to Crescent Creek. Retreating from the gully itself is dangerous due to loose rock; once committed to gully, summit is typically easier than retreat.
routes.bestSeason: July through September. August is optimal with most snow melted. July buggier but viable. September watch for early fall snow and shorter daylight.
routes.turnaround: Typical summit turnaround is mid-afternoon to allow daylight for descent. Loose terrain and exposure require careful footwork in fading light.

routes.detailedRack: Light rack for initial pitch 5.6 climbing; 2-3 rappel anchors recommended. Single rope essential for rappels.
routes.proNeeds: Light rock protection for initial pitches; robust rappel station setup critical for safe descent.

blurb: Mount Terror (8,151 ft) is the highest peak of the Southern Pickets group in Washington's North Cascades. This distinctive, triangular summit has a prominent 2,500-foot north face and rises dramatically 1,000 feet from the south side. First climbed in 1932 by William Degenhardt and Herbert Strandberg, it remains one of the most remote and serious climbing objectives in the North Cascades.
hierarchyNote: ELEVATION CORRECTION: The provided peak elevation of 8,601 ft is significantly incorrect. Wikipedia (NGVD 29), Mountain Project, and all climbing sources confirm Mount Terror's elevation as 8,151 ft — a 450-foot discrepancy. The corrected elevation of 8,151 ft should replace the task data value. Geographic data (coordinates 48.7743°N, 121.2996°W, Southern Pickets, Whatcom County, North Cascades National Park) is accurate and verifies this is the correct peak.

blurb:
Mount Hinman (7,492 ft) is a gentle, heavily glaciated alpine peak in the Alpine Lakes Wilderness of Washington's Cascades, straddling the Snoqualmie and Wenatchee National Forests border. The peak was named in 1934 for Dr. Harry B. Hinman, founder of the Everett branch of The Mountaineers club, and was first ascended in 1928. Historically notable for hosting Hinman Glacier, once the largest glacier between Mount Rainier and Glacier Peak, though it was declared extinct in 2023.</blurb>
<parameter name="hierarchyNote">CRITICAL: Database elevation is 8,494 ft but actual elevation is 7,492 ft (2,284 m). This represents a 1,002 ft overstatement. Correct coordinates: 47.568168°N, -121.2114854°W. Peak straddles King County (#2 by elevation) and Kittitas County (#6). Hinman Glacier was completely gone by 2023, significantly altering route conditions.

routes.routeName: West Ridge via La Bohn Gap
routes.overview: The standard climbing line approaches from the Necklace Valley via East Fork Foss River, scrambles to La Bohn Gap, then follows the West Ridge and Foss Glacier area to the summit. Easy Class 2 scrambling with mixed snow and rock depending on season.
routes.bestSeason: July through September offer the most stable conditions. Early July may have significant snow cover; August-September transitions to mixed rock and snow with minimal glacier crossing. Avoid June and earlier due to extensive snow coverage; avoid October due to early season storms.
routes.approach: Drive east on US Highway 2 from Skykomish approximately 2 miles past Skykomish Ranger Station (mile marker 50.5). Turn south onto Foss River Road (Forest Road #68). Continue 4.1 miles to the Necklace Valley Trailhead parking area. Hike the East Fork Foss River Trail (about 12 minute miles pace on trail) for approximately 8+ miles through forest and meadow terrain, gaining roughly 3,000 feet, to reach Opal Lake at approximately 6,000 feet. The trail ends at Opal Lake; continue off-trail scrambling north and east following boot tracks and cairns through boulder fields and heather slopes, ascending approximately 2 miles and 1,500 feet to La Bohn Gap at roughly 7,500 feet. From La Bohn Gap, ascend east along easy Class 2 ridge terrain, passing near the summit ridge and following boot tracks to the highest point.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent route. Descend the West Ridge back to La Bohn Gap, then descend the heather and boulder slopes back to Opal Lake following established boot tracks and cairns. Retrace the trail back to the Necklace Valley Trailhead via the East Fork Foss River Trail.
routes.descentText: Descent is generally straightforward on the same Class 2 scrambling terrain used for ascent. The main hazard is loose rock on descent; move carefully and watch for falling rock from climbers above. The heather slopes from La Bohn Gap back to Opal Lake provide good traction but can be steep. Once reaching Opal Lake, follow the established trail back down the East Fork Foss River valley. Be aware that mosquitoes become particularly problematic on descent in the lower valley section, especially near water sources.

timing.recommendedStart: Very early morning (4-5 AM) to ensure 14-16 hour return before dark; alpine start essential

watchOut: Bring abundant insect repellent and consider allergy medications; mosquitoes in Necklace Valley can be severe
Rock is extremely loose; helmets recommended and watch for rockfall
Snowfields present early season require route-finding skills; self-arrest capability advised
No reliable water sources above Opal Lake
Afternoon thunderstorm risk; descend before afternoon in summer
knownHazards: Loose, unstable rock of large size on upper slopes
Mosquitoes particularly severe in lower Necklace Valley and near water sources
No established trail above Opal Lake; route-finding required
Early season (June-early July) has extensive snow coverage obscuring hazards
Weather changes rapidly; afternoon storms common in summer
whatToBring: Epi pen if allergic to bee stings
Rope 30-50m (optional but recommended for glacier safety)
Belay device and slings if using rope
routes.detailedRack: Standard alpine gear: 1 ice axe per climber, 1 pair mountaineering crampons, 1 helmet. For glacier sections or if traveling in a rope team, bring a single 30-50m climbing rope, at least 2-3 slings, belay device, and carabiners. Most climbers skip technical protection as the route is straightforward Class 2 scrambling.
routes.proNeeds: Minimal protection needed. The route is non-technical Class 2 scrambling. If using a rope for glacier safety or corniced ridge traversal, partner with experienced alpinists. Self-arrest practice with ice axe is valuable early season.
proTips: Start extremely early (4-5 AM) to maximize daylight return
The boot track from La Bohn Gap is well-established and easy to follow in daylight
Early August offers the best balance of manageable snow and stable weather
The summit sits at a false high point; the true summit is approximately 0.5 miles further along the ridge to the east — most parties stop at the near-equal elevation viewpoint
Water is available at lakes below 6,000 feet; cache water at Opal Lake if available
routes.comms: Cell reception is spotty to nonexistent in Necklace Valley and on the peak. Assume no cell coverage; satellite communicator (Garmin inReach) or PLB recommended for emergencies.
routes.beta: Mount Hinman is a remote, low-traffic alpine peak accessed via a long, scenic approach through the Necklace Valley. The route combines established trail hiking with off-trail scrambling on loose terrain. Weather is the primary hazard; the peak sees fewer than 50 ascents annually. This is a 2-3 day expedition requiring solid backcountry and alpine skills. The Hinman Glacier is extinct as of 2023; route no longer involves technical glacier travel.
routes.turnaround: Parties should establish a firm turnaround time of 1-2 PM at the latest to ensure safe daylight descent. The exposure on the West Ridge is minimal, but loose rock and exposed heather slopes require good light and visibility. If you reach the summit ridge and it's approaching midday, consider descending even if the summit is close, as afternoon storms and poor visibility are common.
routes.bail: From La Bohn Gap, the descent route is the reverse of ascent. If caught high late in day, descend immediately; the route is non-technical and descent is feasible in dusk/darkness if necessary. No other bailing options exist on this route. Do not attempt to traverse to Mount Daniel (adjacent peak) unless highly experienced.

climate.typical: Alpine Lakes Wilderness sits in the marine west coast climate zone. Heavy precipitation year-round, especially September-November. Rapid weather changes common, with afternoon thunderstorms in summer.
climate.spring: May-early June: Heavy snow, avalanche hazard high, creek crossings difficult, mosquitoes emerging. Not recommended.
climate.summer: July-August: 40-60F daytime highs, 30-40F nighttime lows. Morning clear skies common; afternoon thunderstorms typical. Manageable snow in July, mostly gone by August. Peak climbing season.
climate.fall: September-October: 40-50F daytime highs, 30-35F lows. Increasingly unstable weather; early season storms. First ascents of season often in late September.
climate.winter: November-April: Heavy snow, avalanche hazard extreme, temperatures below 20F. Peak wilderness solitude. Not a viable climbing season for this route.

routes.overview: A less commonly documented route that ascends the East Ridge of Mount Hinman from the southeast, starting from lower elevations and climbing up a short rock scramble before accessing a low-angle ridge that extends to the summit.
routes.bestSeason: August and September offer the best conditions with manageable snow and stable weather. Early July will have significant snow on the East Ridge; October sees early storms.
routes.approach: Approach via the Middle Fork Snoqualmie valley from the south: Drive I-90 to Exit 34, then continue north merging with Middle Fork Snoqualmie River Road. Drive the paved road 12.4 miles to Middle Fork Campground near Taylor River. Hike up to Chain Lakes Basin (approximately 13.3 miles one-way, 5,300 feet gain) following Dingford Creek Trail and off-trail scrambling. From Chain Lakes Basin at approximately 5,500 feet, establish a base camp. From 6,000 feet elevation, head generally east and climb a short rock scramble to access the base of the low-angle East Ridge.
routes.descent: Reverse ascent route. Descend the East Ridge to the 6,000 foot saddle, then descend back to Chain Lakes Basin following established boot tracks. Retrace the Dingford Creek Trail back to Middle Fork Campground.
routes.descentText: Descent follows the same route as ascent but in reverse. The East Ridge descent involves straightforward Class 2 scrambling. Loose rock is a concern; use care and watch for rockfall. The descent to Chain Lakes Basin passes through boulder fields and steep heather; this section can be steep and loose. The lower trail sections in Dingford Creek valley are well-established and safe.

timing.recommendedStart: Day 1: Hike to Chain Lakes Basin and camp. Day 2: Pre-dawn start (4 AM) for summit push and return to basin.

watchOut: Early season (July or earlier) will mostly involve snow travel; August/September transitions to rock and snow interspersed
Loose rock hazard; helmet recommended
Established boot track from Chain Lakes area is surprisingly well-defined and easy to follow in daylight

Route-finding challenges in off-trail sections, especially in poor visibility

routes.detailedRack: Standard alpine gear: ice axe, crampons, helmet. Optional: 30-50m rope for glacier safety, belay device, slings, carabiners.
routes.proNeeds: Minimal technical protection. Route is Class 2 scrambling. If using rope, partner with experienced alpinists familiar with snow/glacier travel.
proTips: The established boot track switchbacking up from Chain Lakes is well-maintained and reliable
Climb the entire route on Day 2 as a day trip from Chain Lakes Basin camp
Early August offers stable weather and manageable snow conditions
The true summit is identifiable by the highest rocks; it may not be the most obvious high point
routes.comms: No cell coverage. Satellite communicator or PLB recommended.
routes.beta: The East Ridge approach is a longer route than the Necklace Valley option, but provides access from a different direction and can be less crowded. It combines a long approach hike with moderate alpine scrambling. The route is straightforward Class 2 but requires backcountry skills and navigation in off-trail terrain.
routes.turnaround: Establish turnaround time of 1-2 PM at the latest. The route must be descended before dark, and afternoon thunderstorms are common.
routes.bail: If caught high late in day, descend immediately via the East Ridge to the 6,000-foot saddle, then continue to Chain Lakes Basin. The route is non-technical and navigable in darkness if necessary, though dangerous due to loose rock and exposure.

routes.overview: A scenic alpine route ascending the west side of Mount Hinman via Foss Glacier and the West Ridge. This route emphasizes the glaciated nature of the peak and requires navigation through remnant snowfields and past the extinct Foss Glacier.
routes.bestSeason: August is optimal, offering a balance of manageable snow and stable weather. The Foss Glacier area still holds patches of ice and snow. July may have excessive snow coverage; September transitions to mostly bare rock.
routes.approach: Approach via the Necklace Valley route (as described in standard route). Hike the East Fork Foss River Trail to Necklace Valley, then off-trail scramble to La Bohn Lakes at approximately 6,800 feet. From La Bohn Lakes, ascend northward toward the remnant Foss Glacier area, crossing snow and talus slopes to reach the upper glacier basin at approximately 7,200 feet. Continue ascending the West Face/Foss Glacier area on Class 2 scrambling and snow/ice traversal, staying right of direct Foss Glacier exposure, to reach the West Ridge near the summit.
routes.descent: Descend the West Ridge back to La Bohn Lakes following the established boot track. From La Bohn Lakes, descend the same route to Opal Lake and then retrace to the Necklace Valley Trailhead.
routes.descentText: Descent is via the West Ridge to La Bohn Lakes, then down to Opal Lake and out via East Fork Foss River Trail. Be cautious on the descent through the Foss Glacier area due to loose rock and unstable slopes.

timing.recommendedStart: Very early morning (3-4 AM) for alpine start; 2-3 day expedition recommended

routes.turnaround: Establish turnaround time of 1-2 PM at latest. Afternoon thunderstorms and poor visibility pose significant hazards.
routes.bail: From the Foss Glacier area, descend immediately to La Bohn Lakes. Route is non-technical Class 2 descent but loose rock makes darkness hazardous.

routes.overview: The longest and most scenic approach to Mount Hinman, entering from the Waptus Lake trailhead via Dutch Miller Gap. This route combines a long valley hike with off-trail scrambling and provides stunning Alpine Lakes Wilderness views.
routes.bestSeason: Late July through late September; August offers the most stable conditions. Earliest viable is late July when high passes clear of snow.
routes.approach: Drive I-90 to Exit 62 (Waptus Lake/Cooper River area), heading toward the Waptus Lake Trailhead. Hike the Waptus River Trail (approximately 9.3 miles one-way, 2,900 feet gain) gradually northwest past rivers and multiple lakes, ascending to Waptus Lake and continuing past the lake junction with the Pacific Crest Trail. At the trail junction above Waptus Lake, turn right toward Dutch Miller Gap. The trail climbs steeply to Dutch Miller Gap (approximately 3 miles from Waptus Lake, 1,900 feet gain). From Dutch Miller Gap (approximately 6,800 feet), begin descent on the western side before turning north and following boot tracks upslope. Off-trail scrambling continues approximately 2 miles to Williams Lake (approximately 5,800 feet). From Williams Lake, travel to the northern edge of the lake, then ascend steeply east uphill via boulder fields and cairns (old abandoned mine passes nearby). Continue to Chain Lakes Valley at approximately 7,000 feet. From Chain Lakes, ascend the Southeast Ridge to the summit.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent route. Descend the Southeast Ridge to Chain Lakes, backtrack to Williams Lake, descend to Dutch Miller Gap, then retrace the Waptus River Trail back to the trailhead.
routes.descentText: Descent involves retracing the ascent route. The off-trail sections from Williams Lake back to Dutch Miller Gap require careful route-finding and can be steep. The descent from Dutch Miller Gap is on-trail but steep. Once reaching the Waptus River Trail level, the remainder is straightforward trail hiking down the valley.

timing.recommendedStart: 3-day expedition: Day 1 hike to Waptus Lake area camp (9-10 miles, ~3000 ft gain). Day 2: Hike Dutch Miller Gap and establish high camp or day trip to summit. Day 3: Return to trailhead.

routes.proNeeds: Route requires solid backcountry navigation and off-trail travel skills. Rope team experience beneficial for snow traversal. Self-arrest capability essential.

Late July through August offers the easiest travel conditions
Multiple lake camping options make this a scenic multi-day trip
The boot track switchbacking from Chain Lakes area is well-maintained and fairly easy to follow
Water sources are abundant along the entire route
Views of Dutch Miller Gap and surrounding peaks are spectacular
routes.comms: No cell coverage for most of the route. Satellite communicator (Garmin inReach) or PLB recommended for emergencies.
routes.beta: The Waptus Lake approach is the most remote and scenic route to Mount Hinman. At 18+ miles one-way with 7,000 feet gain, it demands 3 days minimum. This route is suitable for experienced backcountry campers with solid route-finding skills. The Waptus River Trail is well-established; the challenge is the off-trail section from Dutch Miller Gap westward and up to the summit. This approach is particularly rewarding for those seeking solitude and wilderness experience.
routes.turnaround: If on a summit day push from high camp, establish turnaround time of 1-2 PM. For multi-day trips, focus on progressing safely rather than summiting if conditions deteriorate.
routes.bail: From Chain Lakes, bail by descending to Williams Lake, then to Dutch Miller Gap, then follow the Waptus River Trail back to the trailhead. The route is long but non-technical for most of descent.

blurb: Bonanza Peak at 9,516 feet is Washington's highest non-volcanic peak, located in the North Cascades' Glacier Peak Wilderness. This "huge granitic spider" features a complex system of 10 ridges, 7 living glaciers, and loose, metamorphic orthogneiss rock, making it one of the most challenging county highpoints in the lower 48 states. First ascended in 1937 by Curtis I. James, Barrie James, and Joe Leuthold of the Mazamas.
hierarchyNote: Elevation discrepancy: sources list 9,516 ft (Wikipedia) but database shows 9,511 ft (5 ft difference, within survey margin). Coordinates 48.2379°N, 120.8662°W verified from multiple sources. Peak location and hierarchy (Glacier Peak Wilderness, Wenatchee National Forest, Chelan County, WA) confirmed as accurate.

routes.overview: The standard and most-traveled route to Bonanza Peak's summit via the Mary Green Glacier on the east aspect. This alpine glacier-and-rock combination ascent is the most documented and accessible technical route on the peak.
routes.beta: Begin from Holden Village, hike to Holden Lake, then continue to Holden Pass. From there, ascend talus and ledges to the base of Mary Green Glacier around 7,300 feet. Rope up and ascend the glacier along its north edge (climber's right), traverse southwest along the base of Bonanza's northeast ridge to the glacier's upper corner, then climb steep rock sections above the bergschrund to reach the northeast ridge. Scramble along the loose ridge crest (Class 3-4) past large blocks to the summit horn.
routes.approach: From Holden Village (3,200 ft), hike 1 mile on Railroad Creek Trail to junction with Holden Lake Trail (3,600 ft). Continue 4 miles on Holden Lake Trail to Holden Lake (5,278 ft). From there, ascend northwest through a wide gentle-sloped stream gully with slide alder (climbers' path on east side) to reach Holden Pass (6,360-6,400 ft) in approximately 3.8 hours. From Holden Pass, make a rising traverse west along the ridge to a snowfield below slabs, then scramble up slabs to reach the northern edge of Mary Green Glacier base around 7,300 ft.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent route using rappels and downclimbing. Four to six rappel stations exist through the Class 3-4 rock sections on the east face. Use rock horns and established anchors. Exercise extreme caution due to loose rock. Descend the glacier via plunge-stepping. Return to Holden Pass, then to Holden Lake (approximately 2.8 hours from camp). Recommend two 165-foot ropes for efficient rappelling.
routes.descentText: Downclimb or rappel the summit ridge (extremely exposed, very loose rock) back to the rock rib below. Set up rappels using established horn anchors on 165-ft ropes to descend multiple pitches through the Class 3-4 terrain on the east face. Once below the main rock section, descend the Mary Green Glacier via a direct plunge-step descent, navigating open crevasses. Exit the glacier at 7,300-ft base and retrace the descent through Holden Pass, then the Holden Lake Trail back to Holden Village.
routes.fa: Curtis I. James, Barrie James, Joe Leuthold (Mazamas), 1937
routes.bestSeason: Early to mid-July during normal snowpack years offers the ideal window when the bergschrund can still be crossed with snow bridges intact, but snow has melted from the rock scrambling sections above. Most successful parties summit between early-mid July in mid-week. Late July through early September also viable with snow-free rock conditions but increased rockfall hazard from glacier melt. Avoid June (bergschrund crossings may be unsafe) and late September onward (increased objective hazards and unstable weather).
proTips: Attempt the peak mid-week when fewer parties are on the mountain, reducing rockfall hazard from above
Plan the bergschrund crossing carefully—most parties require a snow bridge over/around the upper-right end; late-season parties may need Class 5 climbing or encounter the route
Camp at Holden Pass rather than Holden Lake to save 1,000 ft of elevation gain on summit day
Arrive at the summit ridge early (dawn start from camp) to avoid afternoon weather and thunderstorm risk
Two 165-foot ropes essential; having partners helps manage rappel efficiency and safety
Mid-August offers the most stable rock and complete snow melt from upper sections; July has more robust bergschrund crossings
watchOut: Extreme rockfall hazard in gullies from parties above; avoid being directly below active parties
Loose, metamorphic orthogneiss rock throughout entire route—helmet mandatory; large blocks on ridge can shift
Bergschrund crossing: snow bridges may collapse; difficult Class 5 climbing may be required if bridge is absent
Exposure on the summit ridge: 2-foot walkway of rubble between sharp 'teeth' with severe exposure on both sides
Thunderstorm risk on exposed summit ridge; descend immediately if weather deteriorates
Crevasse hazards on Mary Green Glacier; rope team essential
knownHazards: Persistent rockfall from loose orthogneiss rock; rain, warm temperatures, and glacier melt accelerate rockfall
Difficult bergschrund crossing; no snow bridge late season—may require turning back
Large volume of loose rock debris from glacier retreat
Exposed Class 4 ridge with very loose blocks and severe exposure
Lightning exposure on summit ridge during electrical storms
Open crevasses on Mary Green Glacier with variable snow-bridge conditions
gear: Rock protection: small cams (#0.5–#2), nuts, slings, tricams
Pickets or ice screws for glacier anchors
whatToBring: High-calorie food (energy bars, trail mix, electrolyte drinks)
Emergency communication device (satellite messenger recommended; cell coverage unreliable)
Insulating pad (if camping at Holden Pass)
routes.detailedRack: Trad rack: small cams (#0.5–#2 for protection in cracks), full set of wired nuts (RPs, stoppers, hexes), alpine slings (2–3), cordelette and webbing for anchors. Minimal fixed protection exists; assume no bolts. Ice screws or pickets for glacier belay anchors. Standard glacier travel: harness, belay device, carabiners, two 8mm x 30m or 165-ft ropes for glacier travel and rappelling.
routes.proNeeds: Solid rock scrambling ability and Class 4 climbing experience required; some parties downclimb the rock sections carefully without rappelling, while others use ropes for safety. Glacier travel and crevasse rescue skills essential. Comfortable with exposed scrambling and managing loose rock. Should have led similar alpine routes (e.g., Forbidden Peak, Cascade Pass area scrambles).

timing.recommendedStart: Dawn from Holden Pass camp; most parties leave camp by 4–5 AM to summit by early afternoon and descend before dark and weather deterioration

routes.bail: If the bergschrund crossing is unsafe (no snow bridge, impassable condition), descend immediately; attempting to climb the exposed rock above an uncrossable bergschrund significantly increases risk. Early retreat is the safest option. If weather deteriorates above Holden Pass, descend to camp; do not attempt the glacier or summit in marginal conditions. Escape to Holden Pass (6,400 ft) from anywhere above the glacier base.
routes.turnaround: Establish a firm turnaround time (e.g., 2 PM summit time) before starting; if not on track to summit by your deadline, turn around and descend. Descending in daylight with adequate light for rope management and rock navigation is critical. Do not press on to the summit if weather is deteriorating or parties above are creating rockfall. The route is not worth a forced bivouac or descent in darkness.

climate.forecastZone: Northwest Avalanche Center (NWAC) East Slopes Central forecast zone
climate.typical: High of 50–70°F (10–21°C) in July-August; low of 32–40°F (0–4°C) at 9,500 ft. Snow can linger in shaded gullies into mid-July. Expect rapid weather changes; clear mornings turn to afternoon thunderstorms, rain, or snow without warning.
climate.spring: Heavy snowpack, difficult bergschrund crossings, avalanche risk in approach gullies. May route may be impassable.
climate.summer: Stable, generally clear mornings; afternoon thunderstorm risk, especially in July. Warm days (up to 70°F at base) accelerate glacier melt and rockfall. Peak season: July–August.
climate.fall: September–October: weather becomes increasingly unstable; snow can return. Bergschrund narrows and may be uncrossable. Rockfall increases as loose rock becomes more unstable.
climate.winter: Severe cornicing on summit ridge, extreme cold, high avalanche risk, frequent whiteouts. Not recommended; peak is rarely attempted November–March.

routes.overview: A ridge-direct variant of the standard route that ascends more directly along the northeast ridge of Bonanza Peak, providing an alternative to the Mary Green Glacier approach with increased rock scrambling.
routes.beta: From Holden Pass, ascend talus toward the northeast ridge system of Bonanza Peak. This route traverses less distance on the Mary Green Glacier and exits onto the northeast/north ridge more directly, involving sustained Class 3-4 scrambling along the ridge crest.
routes.approach: Same as standard route to Holden Pass (8 hours from Holden Village via Holden Lake, 6,400 ft), but then traverse more directly toward the north/northeast ridge base rather than following the Mary Green Glacier's full length.
routes.descent: Likely similar to standard route: descend the east face using rappels, or traverse to alternate descent routes.

This route involves traversing wet, slabby rock below the glacier with Class 3 scrambling on surprisingly solid slabs, then transitioning to Class 3-4 rock scrambling with brief Class 5 sections to reach the summit.

blurb: Mount Jefferson is Oregon's second-highest peak at 10,495 feet, a striking stratovolcano in the Cascade Range notable for its technical Class 4 summit pinnacle and significant objective hazards including rockfall and avalanche. First ascended August 12, 1888 by Ray L. Farmer and E.C. Cross via the south ridge, it is considered the most technically difficult of Oregon's major volcanoes despite its lower elevation than Mount Hood.
hierarchyNote: CRITICAL: Mount Jefferson is located in Oregon (Marion/Linn/Jefferson Counties) at coordinates 44.674°N, 121.8°W, in the Oregon Cascades—NOT in Washington's North Cascades. The peak ID 'wa_mount_jefferson' is geographically misplaced. All authoritative sources (USGS, Mountain Project, SummitPost, Peakbagger, The Mountaineers) confirm Oregon location. This hierarchy error should be corrected to place Jefferson in the Oregon Cascades region hierarchy.

routes.overview: The easiest but most tedious route on Mount Jefferson, involving a long, loose southwest slope scramble and hike from the Pamelia Lake approach.
routes.beta: Southwest Ridge is the most commonly recommended 'standard' route for first-time climbers. Ascend the long, loose southwest ridge to the Red Saddle below the summit pinnacle, then climb Class 4 rock and ice to the summit. This route receives less rockfall exposure than more direct approaches and is feasible for daytriPs from Pamelia Lake.
routes.fa: Ray L. Farmer and E.C. Cross, August 12, 1888
routes.approach: From Pamelia Lake Trailhead (off FR 2246, 5 miles from Detroit, OR on HWY 22): Follow Pamelia Lake Trail 2.1 miles to Pamelia Lake at 5,841 ft. Continue on PCT north, then scramble cross-country to gain Shale Lake and Pamelia Ridge. From there, access the southwest slope and Red Saddle via loose scree and class 2-3 terrain. Total approach ~10-12 hours from trailhead.
routes.descent: From summit, 2 rappels (~25m each) through Class 4 terrain to the Red Saddle area, then reverse the southwest ridge descent via loose scrambling and scree down to Shale Lake and return via the approach route.
routes.descentText: The descent mirrors the ascent route but is more hazardous due to increased rockfall risk and loose terrain. A sun-damaged anchor may be encountered; verify its integrity before committing weight. The second rappel may require care due to loose boulders. Late afternoon ascent to allow early-morning summit and descent is not recommended; instead, depart base camp 2-3am for summit early to minimize rockfall danger. The exposed snowfield traverse on return is 55-degree angle with significant runout potential.
routes.bestSeason: Late June to mid-July, when snowfields are consolidated and firm. August can see more rime ice on the summit pinnacle. September and October are possible but involve more bare rock exposure and increased rockfall hazard. Avoid May and earlier due to high avalanche danger in approach gullies.
routes.turnaround: If not on the summit pinnacle by 11am, strongly consider retreat. Rockfall hazard increases dramatically with sun exposure. Late-morning turnarounds have resulted in fatalities.
routes.comms: No reliable cell service; satellite messengers recommended. Reception may be marginal from base camps and nonexistent on the mountain itself.

climate.spring: Stable early season consolidation (May-June), but avalanche danger high in approach gullies. Rime ice frequent.
climate.summer: Warmer afternoons increase rockfall hazard. Afternoon thunderstorms develop regularly. Freezing elevation rises to ~10,200 ft, exposing more bare rock.
climate.fall: Early September can be dry; late September-October brings increasing storms and freeze-thaw.
climate.winter: Rarely attempted; approach gullies prone to avalanche, summit access difficult due to rime and wind slab.

Protection limited by loose rock; rely on snow/ice anchors for protection and rappel points rather than rock gear.
routes.proNeeds: This route requires confident ice and snow climbing, self-rescue ability, risk assessment for rockfall and avalanche, and strong physical fitness. Parties should be able to execute rappels efficiently and navigate loose terrain with exposure. Solo climbing is possible for experienced alpinists but not recommended due to lack of redundancy.

timing.recommendedStart: 2:00 AM from base camp at Shale Lake; 9:00 PM from Pamelia Lake Trailhead for overnight approach

routes.bail: If progress is slow by mid-morning, descend immediately via the southwest ridge rather than pushing to the summit. Retreat from the base of the summit pinnacle to the Red Saddle is straightforward; rappel points are established.

pitchDetail.pitch: 1: Approach via Pamelia Lake to Shale Lake
pitchDetail.notes: Established trail to Pamelia Lake (2.1 mi), then cross-country scrambling with some brush. ~12 hours total from trailhead or 6 hours from overnight camp.

pitchDetail.pitch: 3: Red Saddle to Summit Pinnacle Base
pitchDetail.notes: Steeper scrambling on loose volcanic rock and snow/ice, class 3-4 sections. Final approach to base of pinnacle. ~1-2 hours.
The crux: ~400 ft vertical of steep, usually ice-encrusted Class 4 rock or snow ice (55-60 degree angle). Two rope-lengths required. Quality 5.8 moves available if seeking challenge.

routes.overview: A moderate alpine route ascending the north ridge of Mount Jefferson, best climbed in spring when snow covers most of the loose volcanic rock.
routes.beta: The North Ridge is a straightforward alpine ascent when snow-covered. A ridge traverse leads from the Jefferson Park approach through to the north side of the summit pinnacle, gaining the main peak from the north (the easiest side of the summit block, Class 4). In late season, this route becomes a scree-covered scramble with significant rockfall hazard.
routes.approach: From Whitewater Trailhead (FR 2243, 7.6 miles from HWY 22 near Detroit): Follow Whitewater Trail to Jefferson Park (~5.5 miles, ~5 hours). The easiest route to Jefferson Park is via the Whitewater Trail. From Jefferson Park (Scout Lake area), traverse north and east to gain the north ridge of the main summit.
routes.descent: Descend via the north ridge in reverse, or traverse toward the north side of the summit pinnacle for easier terrain. Rappel from established anchors if conditions warrant.
routes.descentText: Descent is typically straightforward in snow conditions; reverse the ridge descent. In late season, loose scree descent is tedious and hazardous due to rockfall. Two rappels may be necessary from the summit pinnacle area if snow has melted significantly.
routes.bestSeason: May-June when snow coverage protects climbers from extensive loose rock exposure. July-August sees more bare rock but increased accessibility. Avoid late season (September+) when rockfall danger is severe.
routes.turnaround: If not above 10,000 ft by 10am, consider retreat to avoid afternoon rockfall hazard.
routes.comms: No cell service; satellite messengers recommended.

routes.overview: The most popular and accessible alpine route on Mount Jefferson, ascending via the Whitewater Glacier and Jefferson Park from the north. A Grade II glacier climb with Class 4 final approach, this is the 'standard' route from the north.
routes.beta: Starting from the Whitewater Trailhead, this route follows established trail to Jefferson Park, then ascends the Whitewater Glacier (actually the composite glacier system in the Jefferson Park basin) via straightforward snow/ice climbing. The route climbs to a saddle on the north ridge, then traverses and climbs the final ridge south to the summit.
routes.approach: From Whitewater Trailhead (FR 2243, 7.6 miles from HWY 22): Follow Whitewater Trail 4.5-5 miles through forest to Jefferson Park. The trail reaches ~5,841 ft elevation at the highest point. From Jefferson Park, cross-country to the Whitewater Glacier base and ascend glacier systems to reach the north ridge saddle.
routes.descent: Descend via reverse route, glacier descent, or north ridge to Jefferson Park. Straightforward descent with established tracks.
routes.descentText: The descent is generally straightforward, retracing the ascent route via the glacier. Establish rappel anchors if snow coverage is minimal and rock is exposed. Late afternoon descent is typical; return to Jefferson Park area for camp or continue to trailhead.
routes.bestSeason: June-August; late June-July preferred when snow bonds loose rock
routes.turnaround: 11am if above 10,000 ft
routes.comms: No cell service from high elevations
