blurb: Dome Peak is a high, massive, glaciated mountain in the Glacier Peak Wilderness of Washington's North Cascades, composed of quartz diorite rock with two main summits connected by a narrow ridge. The northeast peak was first climbed by George Freed and Eric Larson on August 1, 1936, making it a remote but notable destination popular on the Ptarmigan Traverse. The peak features Chickamin Glacier to the north, Dome Glacier to the west, and a rock face dropping to the southeast.
hierarchyNote: ELEVATION CONFLICT: User data lists 8,386 ft, but Wikipedia and multiple reliable sources (including Mountain Project, SummitPost references, and peakery.com) consistently confirm 8,920 ft. The 8,386 ft figure appears to be incorrect; all authoritative sources reference 8,920 ft. Coordinates confirmed: 48.3017912, -121.0317721 (48°18′12.3″N 121°01′46.2″W).

routes.overview: The most commonly climbed route to Dome Peak's northeast summit, starting from the Downey Creek trailhead and ascending via Bachelor Creek to Itswoot Ridge base camp, then traversing to Dome Glacier for the ascent. Features glacier travel, crevasse crossing, and exposed rock climbing on the summit ridge.
routes.beta: The standard approach via Downey Creek/Bachelor Creek is the closest and most direct route to Dome Peak. From high camp on Itswoot Ridge at 6,200 ft, traverse east across snow and talus to Dome Glacier, then ascend the glacier through crevasses to gain access to the snow slopes and ultimately the exposed summit ridge.
routes.fa: George Freed and Eric Larson on August 1, 1936
routes.rockGrade: 5.5 crack on summit ridge traverse (low Class 5 overall)
routes.approach: From Darrington, take SR-530 north to Suiattle River Road (FR-26). Drive 19.5 miles to Downey Creek Campground trailhead (1,450 ft, 48.25875, -121.22311). Take Downey Creek Trail 6.5 miles to Bachelor Creek at 2,400 ft. Continue past Cub Lake (5,300 ft) following the ridge trail upward. Camp on Itswoot Ridge at 6,200 ft after approximately 9-11 hours of hiking. From camp, traverse east across snow and talus to reach Dome Glacier at its base.
routes.descent: Reverse route via Dome Glacier back to Itswoot Ridge, then retrace Downey Creek Trail. Use rappels if necessary on the final snow/rock section to facilitate group safety on the descent.
routes.descentText: Descend via the same route ascending. From the summit, reverse the rock ridge traverse (Class 5 exposure), then descend 300 ft of snow slope to the notch at 8,560 ft. Descend the Dome Glacier, carefully navigating crevasses by reversing the ascent route. Be particularly cautious crossing the bergschrund on descent. From the base of the glacier, retrace the snow/talus traverse back to Itswoot Ridge, then follow the trail descending steeply through the avalanche gullies and over the 5,900 ft pass to reach Cub Lake. Descend past Cub Lake to Bachelor Creek, then continue down the Downey Creek Trail. Expect 6-8 hours from summit to high camp, and an additional 5-6 hours from camp to trailhead.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Drive to Downey Creek Campground trailhead (elevation 1,450 ft). Hike 13.6 miles (9-11 hours) via Bachelor Creek and Itswoot Ridge to establish high camp at 6,200 ft. Day 2: Summit day. Depart base camp at dawn and ascend Dome Glacier through crevasse fields to the summit ridge. Traverse the exposed ridge (5.5 crack crux) to reach the northeast summit at 8,920 ft (5-8 hours). Return to base camp (5-7 hours). Day 3: Descend from high camp via Itswoot Ridge, Cub Lake, and Bachelor Creek back to the trailhead (5-6 hours).
routes.bail: From Itswoot Ridge camp, retrace the approach trail back to the trailhead. From the glacier, if conditions deteriorate or crevasses become impassable, descend carefully to the base of the glacier and retrace the snow/talus traverse back to high camp. The key bail point is Itswoot Ridge; if weather or avalanche danger is high, do not venture onto the glacier.
routes.turnaround: Turn around by 11 AM from the summit ridge to ensure safe return to high camp before dark. If not on the summit by early afternoon, descent timing becomes increasingly risky. All parties should adhere to established turnaround times regardless of proximity to summit.
routes.bestSeason: July through September is the optimal climbing window, with August typically offering the best weather and most stable snow conditions. June may still have excessive snow and avalanche hazard. October approaches begin to see increasing precipitation and early season snow. Peak climbing season is mid-July through mid-August when snow and glacier conditions are most stable and days are longest.
routes.comms: No cell phone coverage is reliably available in the Glacier Peak Wilderness. Satellite messengers or emergency communication devices are essential. Parties should plan accordingly for emergencies given the remote nature of this peak.
routes.detailedRack: Standard alpine rack: 12-15 ice screws (13-17cm), small to medium carabiners (4-6), 2-3 rappel anchors, 60m dynamic rope for glacier travel and summit ridge traversal. Carry at least one 17cm aluminum ice screw for main anchors. Include prussik cord (3m minimum) for self-rescue if partner falls in crevasse.
routes.proNeeds: All parties must be comfortable with glacier travel including crevasse rescue, self-rescue, and self-arrest. Rope skills are mandatory due to the exposed summit ridge and crevasse fields. Prior experience on glaciated peaks such as Mt. Rainier is strongly recommended.
proTips: The famous rock feature 'Cheval' on the summit leads between the two peaks and is the exposed crux of the otherwise technically easy approach
Travel roped on the glacier at all times due to hidden crevasses and the bergschrund
The summit ridge is sharp and exposed; some parties fix ropes for both ascent and descent safety
Itswoot Ridge provides the best camp location; do not camp higher if possible as the additional 900 feet saves little summit day time
Travel in early morning hours on summit day to minimize rockfall and cornice hazard
watchOut: The bergschrund has deep parallel crevasses up to 4 feet across that can be concealed by snow bridges
Cornices may overhang the ridge line on windy days—stay on the south side of the ridge crest
Rockfall occurs from exposed peaks above; stay alert and wear helmet at all times
Altitude sickness is uncommon at 8,920 ft but monitor for early symptoms
The exposed rock ridge requires focus and experience; do not attempt if you lack comfort with Class 5 climbing
knownHazards: Crevasses and bergschrund: The bergschrund at the base of the snow face is particularly hazardous, with deep parallel crevasses generally not more than 4 feet across but frighteningly deep.
Exposure: The summit ridge is sharp and exposed, requiring careful rope management and potentially fixed lines for safety.
Cornices: Overhanging snow cornices can develop on the ridge crest and are subject to sudden collapse.
Weather: Afternoon storms are common; early starts are essential to minimize exposure.
Altitude exposure: The 8,920 ft elevation and remote location mean rescue is extremely difficult if accidents occur.

pitchDetail.notes: Dome Glacier lower section: 35-degree snow and glacier travel with crevasses. Rope up and establish safe spacing. Navigate around crevasse fields trending generally east-northeast.

Summit ridge traverse: Sharp, exposed rock ridge staying on south side of crest. Low Class 5 climbing with 5.5 crack crux just before the northeast summit. Approximately 500 ft of elevation gain over exposed terrain. Rock quality is excellent but exposure is significant.

road.name: Suiattle River Road (Forest Road 26)
road.seasonalGate: Gate typically opens in June after snow melt; confirm current status with Darrington Ranger District
road.driveNote: From Darrington on SR-530, drive north and turn east onto FR-26 (Suiattle River Road). Drive approximately 19.5 miles to Downey Creek Campground. Road is narrow, winding, and may be partially rough; high-clearance vehicles recommended after heavy rains.

climate.typical: High-altitude alpine climate with cool temperatures year-round. Summer highs 40-60F at summit, winter lows well below zero. Average annual precipitation 100+ inches.
climate.spring: May-June: Lingering snow at higher elevations, avalanche hazard from wet loose slides, increased water crossings
climate.summer: July-September: Stable snow and glacier conditions, daytime highs 50-65F at high elevations, typically dry mid-day (afternoon thunderstorms possible)
climate.fall: September-October: Cooling temperatures, increasing precipitation, early season snow possible above 7,000 ft by late September
climate.winter: November-April: Heavy snow accumulation 200-300+ inches at higher elevations, unstable avalanche conditions, peak passes often blocked

access.landManager: Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest, Darrington Ranger District

access.permit: No permit required for day hiking; backcountry camping allowed throughout Glacier Peak Wilderness with no reservation system

access.closures: Road may be closed seasonally (typically November-May depending on snow); check with Darrington Ranger District (360) 436-1155 for current conditions

timing.recommendedStart: 5:00-6:00 AM from high camp for optimal summit timing and weather avoidance

emergency.rangerStation: Darrington Ranger Station: (360) 436-1155; located at 1405 Emens Avenue North, Darrington, WA 98241
emergency.nearestHospital: Providence Regional Medical Center Everett (Level II Trauma Center), 1700 13th Street, Everett, WA (approximately 60+ miles); Cascade Valley Hospital in Arlington (approximately 40 miles) provides Level IV Emergency Department
emergency.notes: Rescue from Dome Peak is extremely difficult and time-consuming due to remote wilderness location. Helicopter rescue is rare due to weather and terrain. All parties must be self-sufficient and capable of self-rescue. Satellite communication is essential given complete lack of cell service.

routes.overview: An alternative route approach that gains the summit via the Chickamin Glacier on the northern side of the peak. This variant traverses beneath the north face to reach the col between Dome and Chickamin Glaciers, then ascends to the northeast summit.
routes.beta: This route approaches Dome Peak from the north via the Chickamin Glacier. After establishing high camp at Itswoot Ridge, traverse northeast toward the Chickamin Glacier side of the peak. The route involves traversing around the northern approaches to Dome Peak to gain the col between the two major glaciers.
routes.fa: George Freed and Eric Larson on August 1, 1936
routes.approach: From Itswoot Ridge high camp (6,200 ft), traverse north and east across snow and talus to access the base of the Chickamin Glacier. Ascend the eastern side of the Chickamin Glacier, navigating crevasses to reach the col at 8,450 ft between Dome and Chickamin Glaciers.
routes.descent: Descend via the same route, reversing the glacier traverse. Use care crossing the bergschrund on descent.
routes.descentText: Reverse the ascent route down the Chickamin Glacier, carefully managing crevasse hazards and rope team spacing. From the col, descend back to the glacier base and retrace the snow/talus traverse back to Itswoot Ridge high camp. Expect 6-7 hours from summit to high camp.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Drive to Downey Creek Campground trailhead and hike 13.6 miles to establish high camp at Itswoot Ridge (6,200 ft) in 9-11 hours. Day 2: From high camp, traverse to Chickamin Glacier base and ascend via the glacier to the Dome/Chickamin col, then to the northeast summit at 8,920 ft. Return to high camp. Day 3: Descend via Itswoot Ridge to trailhead.
routes.bail: If conditions deteriorate on the glacier or crevasses become impassable, descend carefully to the base of the Chickamin Glacier and retrace back to high camp. The exposure on this route is less than the standard route; retreat is more straightforward.
routes.turnaround: Turn around by 1 PM from the col to ensure sufficient time for descent. The Chickamin Glacier approach requires careful evaluation of conditions due to potential icefall hazard from seracs above the route.
routes.bestSeason: July through August, when Chickamin Glacier conditions are most stable and snowfall is minimal
routes.comms: No cell phone coverage in the Glacier Peak Wilderness. Satellite communication essential.
routes.detailedRack: Glacier travel rack: 10-12 ice screws, medium to large carabiners (4-5), 60m rope, rappel anchors. The Chickamin Glacier approach requires solid anchor placement due to softer snow conditions on the north-facing glacier.
routes.proNeeds: Solid glacier travel and crevasse rescue skills required. Experience navigating north-facing glaciers with potentially softer snow conditions is beneficial. Icefall hazard awareness is critical.
proTips: The Chickamin Glacier is typically less crevassed than Dome Glacier on the western side
Watch for icefall hazard from seracs above the glacier—travel through this section quickly and early
This route sees fewer parties and may have more post-holing in snow; adjust pacing expectations
Navigation can be challenging in poor visibility on the northern approach; carry a compass and altimeter
watchOut: Icefall from seracs above the route is a serious hazard—be alert for falling ice
Crevasses on Chickamin Glacier can be well-concealed; rope up and maintain spacing
North-facing slopes retain snow longer; avalanche hazard may be higher earlier in the season
The col between glaciers can be wind-scoured and icy—use caution on final ascent
knownHazards: Icefall: Seracs and ice cliffs above the Chickamin Glacier pose falling ice hazard
Crevasses: The glacier contains numerous crevasses, some concealed by snow bridges
Avalanche potential: The north-facing slopes above the glacier can slough wet avalanches in afternoon sun
Exposure: The final ascent from the col has exposure but less vertical rock than the standard route

pitchDetail.notes: Chickamin Glacier: Ascend the eastern margin of the glacier, navigating moderate crevasses. Angle is generally 25-32 degrees. Watch for icefall hazard. Gain the col at 8,450 ft at the junction between Chickamin and Dome Glaciers.

routes.overview: An alpine approach ascending the east ridge of Dome Peak, combining snow slopes, mixed terrain, and rock climbing to gain the northeast summit. This route involves significant exposure and technical mixed climbing.
routes.beta: The east ridge route ascends toward the col between Dome and Chickamin Glaciers via snow slopes on the eastern side, then transitions to rock and mixed terrain for the final ascent. This route requires comfort with alpine rock climbing and mixed terrain.
routes.fa: George Freed and Eric Larson on August 1, 1936
routes.approach: From Itswoot Ridge high camp (6,200 ft), traverse east across snow and talus to reach the base of the east ridge. Ascend snow slopes trending toward the col at 8,450 ft, then transition to rock and mixed terrain for the upper ridge section.
routes.descent: Reverse route or descend via the standard Dome Glacier route if conditions permit
routes.descentText: Either reverse the ascent route down the east ridge (Class 4-5 exposed terrain) or, if feasible and party agrees, descend via the standard Dome Glacier route. If descending via the east ridge, use rappels as needed on exposed sections. Expect 6-8 hours from summit to high camp.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Hike from trailhead to Itswoot Ridge high camp (6,200 ft) in 9-11 hours. Day 2: Ascend the east ridge to the northeast summit at 8,920 ft via Class 4-5 mixed terrain (6-8 hours). Descend to high camp (6-8 hours). Day 3: Descend to trailhead.
routes.bail: If conditions are poor or party is uncomfortable with exposure, retreat to high camp via the ascent route before committing to the upper ridge.
routes.turnaround: Turn around by noon from the col if not making good progress on the rock section. The mixed terrain sections on the east ridge are exposed and require careful movement.
routes.bestSeason: July through early August when rock is dry and snow bridges are stable
routes.comms: No cell phone coverage. Satellite communication essential.
routes.detailedRack: Alpine mixed rack: 8-10 ice screws, 2-3 sets of cams (0.5-2 inch), small nuts, 6-8 carabiners, 60m rope, rappel anchors. Bring small to medium rock protection for the Class 4-5 rock sections.
routes.proNeeds: Solid mixed terrain climbing experience required. Comfort with alpine rock climbing on exposed terrain is essential. Crevasse rescue and self-rescue proficiency mandatory.
proTips: This route is less traveled than the standard route; navigation may be more challenging
The rock quality is excellent on the east ridge; solid protection placement is usually possible
Start very early to ensure daylight for the technical sections
Have a clear turnaround time and stick to it regardless of proximity to summit
watchOut: The east ridge is significantly more exposed than the standard route—commitment level is high
Rockfall is a potential hazard from the slopes above—wear helmet and move efficiently
Mixed terrain with ice and rock transitions requires careful foot and hand placement
Weather deterioration is particularly dangerous on exposed ridge terrain
knownHazards: Exposure: The east ridge is significantly more exposed than other routes to Dome Peak
Mixed terrain hazard: Transitions between ice/snow and rock require technical skill
Rockfall: Slopes above the ridge may produce rockfall especially in afternoon conditions
Weather: The ridge is vulnerable to wind and rapid weather changes

pitchDetail.notes: Snow slopes and talus leading to the base of the east ridge. Ascend Class 3-4 terrain with mixed snow and rock, gaining the lower ridge section.
Upper east ridge: Class 4-5 rock climbing with some ice axe placements. Mixed terrain with exposure. Approximately 300-400 ft of climbing to the northeast summit.

routes.overview: The Ptarmigan Traverse approach to Dome Peak, starting from the Cascade Pass side and traversing south through remote alpine terrain via the Dana Glacier to reach Dome Peak. This is a committing multi-day alpine traverse that requires advanced route-finding skills.
routes.beta: This route approaches Dome Peak as the southern terminus of the famous Ptarmigan Traverse. Starting from White Rock Lakes or Cascade Pass, traverse south across multiple glaciers including Dana Glacier, eventually reaching the col between Dome Peak and One-Eyed Bull. From there, ascend the Dome Glacier to the summit.
routes.fa: George Freed and Eric Larson on August 1, 1936 (for the peak)
routes.approach: Access via the Ptarmigan Traverse from Cascade Pass (north) or White Rock Lakes (south). This is a remote, unmarked, multi-day alpine traverse. From White Rock Lakes, traverse low below One Eyed Bull and gain access to the eastern half of the Dana Glacier. Ascend the gently-angled glacier to the Dome/Dana Glaciers Col at 7,470 ft, then traverse to the base of Dome Glacier.
routes.descent: Reverse route via the Ptarmigan Traverse, or if descending to the Downey Creek side, retrace via the standard Dome Glacier/Downey Creek route
routes.descentText: Most parties descend via the standard Downey Creek approach rather than reversing the entire Ptarmigan Traverse due to time constraints. If reverting to the traverse, retrace via Dana Glacier back to the Dome/Dana col, then navigate the complex glacial terrain of the traverse heading north or south depending on entry point.
routes.itinerary: This is a 5-7 day expedition typically. From White Rock Lakes base camp, spend day 1 traversing to Dana Glacier. Day 2: Cross Dana Glacier to the Dome Peak col. Day 3: Ascend Dome Peak summit, descend to high camp, then either continue the Ptarmigan Traverse or descend via Downey Creek (requires additional day). Days 4-7: Traverse exit or descend approach.
routes.bail: If weather deteriorates or travel slows, descend to lower elevations via Dana Glacier to established camps. The Ptarmigan Traverse route is more committing than the Downey Creek approach due to remoteness.
routes.turnaround: Turn around by mid-day from the summit to ensure safe descent to camp before dark
routes.bestSeason: July through August only. June has too much snow; September brings increased storm risk.
routes.comms: No cell phone coverage. Satellite communication absolutely essential on this remote traverse.
routes.detailedRack: Complete glacier and alpine rescue rack: 12-15 ice screws, medium carabiners (6-8), 60m rope, rappel anchors, prussik cord, crevasse rescue gear. Bring redundant navigation tools given unmarked nature of the traverse.
routes.proNeeds: Expert-level glacier travel and crevasse rescue proficiency required. Route-finding skills in complex alpine terrain are essential. Extensive high-altitude mountaineering experience is mandatory.
proTips: This is one of Washington's premier alpine traverses; start with detailed trip reports and consider hiring a guide
The western half of Dana Glacier has fewer crevasses than the eastern half; monitor for changes
Stay well-roped on all glacier sections due to hidden crevasses and altitude
Clear early to maximize daylight for the Dome Peak section
watchOut: The Ptarmigan Traverse is unmarked and navigation is complex; poor visibility can lead to navigation errors
Hidden crevasses are common on all glacier sections
Altitude exposure (8,920+ ft) can slow travel unexpectedly
Weather is the primary limiting factor; turn back immediately if weather deteriorates
This route is more exposed to avalanche hazard than the Downey Creek approach in certain snow conditions
knownHazards: Crevasses: Multiple crevasse fields on Dana Glacier and throughout the traverse
Navigation: The route is unmarked and complex; poor visibility significantly increases difficulty
Avalanche hazard: Greater exposure than direct routes to Dome Peak
Weather: The high-altitude traverse is extremely weather-dependent
Altitude exposure: Sustained high altitude over multiple days increases AMS risk

pitchDetail.notes: Dana Glacier sections: Ascend generally low-angle glacier terrain (25-30 degrees) with crevasses. Eastern section is longer and requires careful route-finding.

road.driveNote: Access via either the north (Cascade River Road near Marblemount) or south (White Rock Lakes approach) depending on traverse direction. Confirm road status before attempting.

emergency.county: Skagit County (southern sections) / Whatcom County (northern sections)

emergency.notes: This is an extremely remote location. Rescue is nearly impossible. All parties must be fully self-sufficient with satellite communication. This is one of Washington's most committing alpine routes.

routes.overview: The standard and most popular route to Colonial Peak, combining glacier travel with Class 3-4 rock scrambling on the west ridge. Ascends the Colonial Glacier to a snow finger, then scrambles the final ridge to the summit.
routes.fa: First ascent of peak: July 31, 1931 by William Degenhardt and Herbert Strandberg (Seattle Mountaineers). Route established as standard mountaineering line.
routes.bestSeason: July through September. June has significant snow coverage requiring winter climbing skills. After mid-July, Colonial Glacier becomes heavily crevassed; route-finding becomes critical. August offers the best conditions with lower snow but good weather stability. September sees frequent afternoon thunderstorms.
routes.approach: Park at Pyramid Lake Trailhead on Highway 20, 6.5 miles east of Newhalem at elevation 1,150 feet. Hike the Pyramid Lake Trail 2 miles gaining 1,500 feet to Pyramid Lake (2,650 feet). Continue off-trail up Pyramid Ridge following a climbers' trail, traversing scree and boulders over mixed terrain. Establish camp at approximately 5,400-6,100 feet near Colonial Glacier base. Navigation challenges exist on Pyramid Ridge; route-finding knowledge essential.
routes.descent: Descend the West Ridge and snow finger, reversing the ascent route. Thread through crevasses on Colonial Glacier using established cairn markers. Descend Pyramid Ridge to Pyramid Lake, then follow the trail back to the trailhead.
routes.descentText: From the summit, descend southeast back to the notch west of the summit pyramid. Carefully navigate the snow finger descending 150 feet, then negotiate loose rock scrambling (Class 3-4) downward. Traverse talus and high firnfield, threading crevasses on the Colonial Glacier (after mid-July, cross to eastern rim of basin following rock ribs and cairns to avoid heavy crevassing). Descend off-trail through Pyramid Ridge, navigating cliff bands and benchmarks back to Pyramid Lake, then hike the trail back to the trailhead.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Start 8 AM, hike Pyramid Lake Trail to Pyramid Lake (2 hours), continue to base camp near Colonial Glacier (5-6 hours), establish camp. Day 2: Alpine start 3 AM from camp, ascend Colonial Glacier (2 hours), climb West Ridge and summit (1-2 hours), summit by 7-8 AM, descend to camp (2 hours). Day 3: Descend to trailhead, depart 10 AM, arrive at cars by 3-4 PM (5-6 hours).
routes.bail: From low camp, descend Pyramid Ridge trail system back to Pyramid Lake and the marked trail to the trailhead. From upper glacier, retreat down the glacier threading crevasses and descend Pyramid Ridge. In bad weather above camp, descent is straightforward but route-finding critical; stay to climber's left on glacier to avoid cliffs and maximize visible cairn markers.
routes.turnaround: Turn around by 10 AM from high camp if summit is not reached, allowing safe descent in daylight. Do not attempt ice/snow sections after 2 PM due to afternoon thunderstorm risk and poor visibility. In early season (June), evaluate snow conditions on the approach carefully; if conditions require full winter climbing skills beyond the party's ability, retreat immediately.
routes.beta: This is a straightforward alpine scramble that requires glacier travel experience and crevasse rescue skills. The route is snow-dependent; conditions vary dramatically year-to-year and even week-to-week. The upper scramble features loose, blocky rock typical of North Cascades metamorphic terrain. Parties must be comfortable with basic navigation and route-finding. The notch section contains at least one Class 4 move. Water sources available at higher elevations; stream crossings possible near Pyramid Lake.
routes.proNeeds: Glacier travel protection: rope, harnesses, prussiks for crevasse rescue. Scrambling protection minimal; consider a short 30-50 meter rope for exposed Class 4 section near notch.
proTips: Travel on the glacier early in the day (before 10 AM) to avoid thermal-induced crevasse collapse and rockfall hazard.
After mid-July, avoid the direct glacier approach below Paul Bunyan's Stump due to heavy crevassing; instead traverse east around the basin rim following rock ribs and established cairns.
Early-season ascents (June-early July) with straightforward crevasse crossings (finger-width) reward early alpine starts and faster movement.
The descent takes nearly as long as the ascent; plan for a full day summit push and descent back to camp.
In marginal visibility, cairns and dirt bands mark the safe passages through crevasse zones; study cairn locations before committing.
Descending the upper snow finger requires careful footwork; loose rock hazard increases significantly in descent.
watchOut: Crevasses open on Colonial Glacier from mid-July onward; some sections become impassable mid-season despite appearing benign early-season.
Class 4 exposed scrambling near the notch with loose rock; falls are serious and potentially fatal. Move carefully with three-point contact.
Rockfall hazard on north-facing slopes during and after rain; afternoon thunderstorms are common and bring rockfall danger.
Route-finding can be obscured by snow in early season or by low clouds; establish reliable landmarks and cairn markers before descending.
The upper snow finger is steep (40+ degrees); slips are likely to result in long slides into crevassed terrain below.
knownHazards: Colonial Glacier crevasses, especially mid-season (July-August) and lower elevation snowfields in spring.
Loose, blocky metamorphic rock on upper scramble prone to rockfall, particularly on north-facing aspects.
Class 3-4 exposed scrambling on the upper ridge with significant exposure to falls.
Route-finding challenges on Pyramid Ridge approach and during descent; whiteout conditions extremely dangerous.
Afternoon thunderstorms with lightning risk above 6,000 feet; most common July-August.
whatToBring: Ice axe for self-arrest practice and glacier travel
Harness and carabiners for glacier travel
Helmet (essential for rockfall and scrambling exposure)
Sleeping bag rated to at least 20°F
Stove and fuel for melting snow or boiling water
Map (USGS 7.5 minute North Cascades topographic) and compass
First aid kit with blister treatment
routes.detailedRack: Rope: 50-meter dynamic single rope. Protection: 2-3 sets of locking carabiners, 2 harnesses, 3-4 slings, 3-4 prussik cords (for crevasse rescue), ice axe per person, crampons per person. Belay: anchor bolts or natural rock anchors may be used; most parties move together and simulclimb. Rescue: practice crevasse rescue technique before attempting glacier travel; 2-person rope teams minimum. Scramble: minimal protection; short-rope teams with one person on belay for Class 4 section.

timing.recommendedStart: 3:00 AM from high camp for alpine start; 8:00 AM from Pyramid Lake trailhead on approach day

road.name: North Cascades Highway (WA-20)
road.status: Open seasonally; typically closed in winter
road.seasonalGate: Mid-November to mid-April (variable by year)
road.driveNote: Paved highway. From Newhalem, drive east 6.5 miles on Highway 20 to Pyramid Lake Trailhead pullout on north side of road. Ample parking (approximately 6 cars) at the designated pullout area.

climate.forecastZone: North Cascades High Country (National Weather Service Zone WAZ567 or similar)
climate.typical: Maritime west coast climate with heavy precipitation. Temperatures drop 3.5-4°F per 1,000 feet elevation gain. Alpine zone experiences frequent afternoon thunderstorms July-August. Wind accelerates over ridges.
climate.spring: May-June: Significant snowpack. High avalanche danger on steep slopes. Night temperatures 10-20°F; daytime 30-40°F. Afternoon thunderstorms increasing by June.
climate.summer: July-August: Best weather window. Day temperatures 40-60°F at glacier elevations, near freezing at night. Afternoon thunderstorms frequent and intense. Crevasse hazard increases dramatically as glacier ablates.
climate.fall: September: Storm frequency increases. Temperatures 30-50°F. Early season snow possible by late September. Shorter daylight limits climb windows.
climate.winter: December-March: Heavy snow. Avalanche danger significant. Temperatures 0-20°F. Route-finding extremely difficult; most climbers avoid this season except for ice climbing specialists.

access.landManager: National Park Service (North Cascades National Park) / US Forest Service (Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest)
access.fees: Recreation.gov backcountry permit: $6.00 reservation fee + $10.00 per person per night
access.permit: Backcountry camping permit required year-round for overnight stays. Obtain at Wilderness Information Center in Marbelmount or online via Recreation.gov.
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass ($5 per day or $30 annual) required at Pyramid Lake Trailhead for day-use or trailhead parking.
access.closures: North Cascades Highway (WA-20) typically closed mid-November through mid-April; check WSDOT website for current status. Backcountry permits may be restricted during ranger-led management periods.

emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades Visitor Center (Marbelmount): (360) 873-2500; Wilderness Information Center: (360) 854-7245
emergency.nearestHospital: Skagit Valley Hospital (Mount Vernon): ~45 miles south; St. Joseph Hospital (Bellingham): ~50 miles west
emergency.notes: Cell service is unreliable in the North Cascades backcountry. Satellite communicator (Garmin InReach, PLB) highly recommended. Report any injury or emergency to park rangers immediately upon descent; helicopters can reach high elevations in good weather.

routes.comms: Cell coverage: Very limited to absent in the backcountry. Verizon spotty around Diablo Lake area but unreliable above 5,000 feet. Satellite communicator (Garmin InReach or Delorme) strongly recommended for multi-day trips. Two-way radios (FRS/GMRS) useful for party communication on glacier but don't reach outside help.
routes.corrections: The peak elevation in the database is listed as 8,804 feet, but authoritative sources (Wikipedia, US Geological Survey data via multiple mapping services, SummitPost, Peakbagger, WTA, and Mountain Forecast) confirm Colonial Peak's true elevation is 7,771 feet (2,369 meters). This represents a 1,033-foot overestimate in the current database record and must be corrected.

routes.overview: A serious alpine ice climb on the north face of Colonial Peak. This is one of the few established ice routes on the peak, featuring mixed rock and ice climbing with significant vertical terrain and exposure. A rare and demanding alpine objective suited to experienced mountaineers.
routes.fa: First ascent: 1986 by Mark Twight, Mark Bebie, John Stoddard, and Monte Westlund
routes.bestSeason: December through March, with optimal conditions in late winter (February-March) when maritime snow provides good consolidation. Early April may still offer climbable conditions but with increasing avalanche danger.
routes.approach: Park at Pyramid Lake Trailhead on Highway 20, 6.5 miles east of Newhalem at 1,150 feet elevation. Hike the Pyramid Lake Trail 2 miles (1,500 feet gain) to Pyramid Lake. Continue off-trail, ascending Pyramid Ridge or taking alternative approaches through snow-covered terrain to establish camp at approximately 5,400-6,000 feet near the base of the north face. Winter conditions require navigation skills and avalanche awareness.
routes.descent: Descend the north face reversing the ascent line, or exit via the West Ridge route descending to base camp. Return to Pyramid Lake trail and descend to the trailhead.
routes.descentText: From the summit, rappel or downclimb the established descent route on the north face (typically 4-6 rappels with 30-40 meter ropes depending on variation used). Thread through rocky sections carefully, protecting against rockfall. Descend the lower snowfields to the base of the north face. Return to base camp and reverse the approach hike back to Pyramid Lake and the marked trail to the trailhead.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Approach the peak from Pyramid Lake Trailhead via Pyramid Ridge, establish camp at 5,400-6,000 feet. Day 2: Alpine start 4 AM, ascend north face (4-6 hours climbing), summit by 10-11 AM, begin descent and rappel to upper glacier, return to camp (3-4 hours). Day 3: Descend to trailhead.
routes.bail: From any point on the face, retreat by carefully descending steeper terrain or rappelling. The lower portions of the face offer easier descent lines. Exit via West Ridge if upper face becomes too dangerous. Early retreat essential if weather deteriorates; afternoon storms pose serious avalanche and rockfall hazards.
routes.turnaround: Turn around by 11 AM if summit has not been reached, allowing time for safe rappel descent before dark. Do not commit to exposed climbing after 1 PM due to deteriorating weather and avalanche risk.
routes.beta: This is a steep, committing alpine ice route with significant technical difficulty. The route features multiple ice pitches (up to 60 degrees) interspersed with mixed rock and snow scrambling. Approximately 4,500 feet of crampons-required terrain from the glacier basin to the summit, with the face proper being 1,500 feet of steeper climbing. Two significant 'fat ice pitches' provide the crux sections. Upper rock sections require bare-handed scrambling on exposed steep terrain. The route has seen only a handful of ascents since first ascent in 1986. Movement must be continuous; time management essential.
routes.proNeeds: Extensive protection required: 2x ice screws (3-4 per pitch recommended), rock protection (nuts, hexes, small cams for mixed sections), 60-80 meter rope (dynamic preferred), harnesses, carabiners, belay devices. Most parties use ice screws for belays; natural rock anchors rare in upper face sections.
proTips: The route can be climbed car-to-car in a day if conditions are perfect and the party is extremely efficient, but a bivy at 6,000+ feet is recommended for safer climbing pace.
Scout the approach route in daylight if possible; snow-covered terrain conceals crevasses and route hazards.
The two 'fat ice pitches' in mid-face provide the most aesthetic climbing; excellent conditions in spring-like Cascade winter weather.
Establish rappel anchors carefully on descent; loose rock is common on the upper face and can trap or damage gear.
Carry extra slings and cordelette for gear recovery and anchor building; protection may not be bomber on lower sections.
The upper rock sections are steep and exposed; leadership requires confident mixed climbing ability.
watchOut: Avalanche hazard on lower face and approach slopes, especially after new snow or rapid temperature changes.
Rockfall from upper bands; wear helmet at all times. Upper rock is loose and blocky.
Corniced ridges near summit can collapse; approach final rocks with caution.
Crevasses and bergschrunds at lower glacier; rope team always.
Weather deterioration extremely rapid; afternoon storms create avalanche danger and whiteout conditions.
The route is rarely climbed; few established fixed anchors and minimal track; route-finding challenging in whiteout.
knownHazards: Avalanche danger on approach slopes and face itself, particularly after new snow or in warm afternoon conditions.
Rockfall on steep upper rock sections; loose metamorphic rock prone to failure.
Exposure and ice fall hazard on steep ice pitches; proper protection and solid climbing technique essential.
Crevasse hazard on glacier approach; route-finding difficult in snow-covered terrain.
Cornices and bergschrunds at higher elevations.
Rapid weather change with afternoon thunderstorms and whiteout conditions.
whatToBring: Two ice axes per person (one main, one backup or modular)
Crampons rated for steep ice, with antibot attachments
Ice screws: 12-15 total (mix of 10cm and 13cm), placed in rack for quick access
Rock protection: 2 sets nuts, 1 set hexes, 1 small cam set (0.3-1.0 inch range) for mixed sections
Two harnesses (sport climbing harness acceptable but alpine preferred)
Belay device: ATC or equivalent; consider two for managing rope systems
Full insulation layers: polypropylene thermals, fleece mid-layer, down jacket
Mountaineering boots rated for steep snow/ice and crampons
Balaclava, insulated gloves with good dexterity for crampon adjustment
Helmet with good ventilation (alpine climbing helmet)
Bivy sack or ultralight emergency shelter
Sleeping bag rated to 0°F or lower for winter conditions
Sleeping pad for insulation (critical in winter)
Lightweight stove rated for cold, plus fuel (white gas preferred for extreme cold)
USGS topographic map (North Cascades area) and compass
First aid kit including blister treatment
routes.detailedRack: Ice climbing: 12-15 ice screws in quickdraw rack (10cm and 13cm mix), ice axes (two per person for mixed terrain traverses). Rock protection: 2 sets nuts, 1 set hexes, 1 small cam set (for belay anchors and protection on mixed sections). Anchors: natural rock anchors rare; rely on ice screws and rock gear for belay setup. Rope: 60-80 meter single rope (9.8-10.2mm recommended); dynamic preferred for safety. Belay setup typically uses ice screw anchors with natural rock backup if available. Rappel setup requires solid anchors; consider leaving slings if bomber anchors unavailable. Most parties plan 4-6 rappels on descent with 30-40 meter rope lengths.

timing.recommendedStart: 3:00-4:00 AM from high camp for alpine start

climate.forecastZone: North Cascades High Country (National Weather Service Zone WAZ567)
climate.typical: Alpine maritime climate with heavy winter precipitation. Winter temperatures at glacier level typically -5°F to +20°F; summit -10°F to +10°F. Wind frequently 20-40 mph with gusts to 60+ mph in storms. Temperature inversions possible.
climate.spring: March-April: Increasingly unstable weather. Day/night cycles cause rapid snowpack bonding and avalanche cycles. Daytime temperatures above freezing at lower elevations, below freezing at altitude.
climate.summer: Not applicable for this route; route is winter/spring only.
climate.fall: Not applicable for this route.
climate.winter: December-March: Heavy snow, low temperatures (-5°F to +15°F), high wind. January-February typically most stable. Late February-March offers spring-like snow conditions (wet afternoons, refrozen mornings) ideal for climbing. Visibility highly variable; whiteouts common.

routes.overview: A less-documented alpine route on the east-facing aspect of Colonial Peak. This route represents an alternative climbing objective on the peak but has limited available information in published climbing sources, suggesting it is rarely ascended and poorly established.

routes.beta: This route is not well-documented in standard climbing guidebooks or trip reports. The east-facing aspect of Colonial Peak is steep and exposed. Without clear documented information about this route, potential climbers should exercise extreme caution and conduct independent research using detailed topographic maps and consultation with local climbing clubs (such as Cascade Climbers forum or Seattle Mountaineers). Alternative established routes (West Ridge, Watusi Rodeo) are recommended for most parties.

Contact local mountaineering clubs (Seattle Mountaineers, Cascade Climbers forum) for firsthand knowledge of this objective.

blurb:
Colonial Peak is a striking 7,771-foot summit in the North Cascades of Washington, notable for its extreme steepness rising 6,500 feet above Thunder Arm of Diablo Lake in under 3 miles. First ascended in 1931 by Seattle Mountaineers William Degenhardt and Herbert Strandberg, the peak offers multiple alpine climbing routes ranging from glacier scrambling on the standard West Ridge to technical ice routes on the north face (Watusi Rodeo, AI 4). Located within North Cascades National Park above the scenic Pyramid Lake Trailhead, Colonial Peak attracts mountaineers seeking high-elevation challenges requiring crevasse rescue skills, rock scrambling experience, and self-sufficiency in remote terrain.",
    "hierarchyNote": "CRITICAL ELEVATION DISCREPANCY: The database lists Colonial Peak at 8,804 feet, but all authoritative sources (Wikipedia, USGS via Peakvisor, SummitPost, Peakbagger, WTA, Mountain Forecast) confirm the elevation is 7,771 feet (2,369 meters). This represents a 1,033-foot overestimate that must be corrected in the database. The peak's geographic location, first ascent history, and route documentation are accurate; only the elevation figure requires correction.",

Mount Challenger (8,236 ft) stands as the northern anchor of the Picket Range in North Cascades National Park, the most rugged and remote mountain wilderness in the lower 48 states. This heavily glaciated granite peak features crevassed Challenger Glacier, exposed rock climbing to 5.7, and is one of the most challenging alpine objectives in Washington.
hierarchyNote: Elevation discrepancy found: Wikipedia and some sources cite 8,207 feet, while Peakbagger and input data use 8,236 feet (29-foot difference). Used input value of 8,236 feet. Minor variants in coordinates from different sources (48.8358°N to 48.8375°N) - using mid-range values.

routes.overview: The most common route to Mount Challenger's summit, ascending the Challenger Glacier on the north slopes. Features glacier travel with crevasse navigation, bergschrund crossing, and a short technical rock pitch.
routes.beta: Approach via Hannegan Pass or Big Beaver Creek to reach the Challenger Glacier arm at ~6,800 feet. From there, traverse the glacier avoiding crevasses, cross the bergschrund, and climb the northeast ridge. Final pitch is exposed 5.5-5.6 rock on solid quartz diorite.
routes.approach: From Hannegan Campground trailhead (Highway 542, 1.3 miles on Ruth Creek Road), hike Hannegan Pass Trail 4.2 miles to Hannegan Pass (4,106 ft). Descend 2,500 feet on PNW Trail to Chilliwack River. Cross river and ascend brushy Easy Ridge trail to reach The Impasse area. Navigate around gorge and ascend to Perfect Pass (6,300 ft) where a good bivy site exists. From Perfect Pass, traverse Challenger Glacier northeast to the rock.
routes.descent: Descend the same route in reverse. Short rappels may be required to reach the glacier from the summit rocks depending on conditions.
routes.descentText: From summit, downclimb 15 feet of 5.0 climbing to the small snow arête, then traverse back down the steep snowslope above the saddle. Descend northeast ridge back to Challenger Glacier, navigating around crevasses to reach Perfect Pass bivy. Return via Easy Ridge and Hannegan Pass trail.
routes.fa: Phil Dickert, Jack Hossack, George MacGowan (September 7, 1936)
routes.bestSeason: July and August offer the most stable weather and best snow conditions. Early June may have excessive snow and crevasse hazards; late September risks early storms.
knownHazards: Heavily crevassed Challenger Glacier with multiple bergschrunds
Avalanche danger on steep approaches, especially in spring and early season
Loose, exposed rock on final pitch requires care; rockfall from above
Wet, icy conditions common, requiring crampons and ice tools
Bergschrund may be impassable late season or after heavy snow
Exposed 5th class rock with limited protection placement
Extreme remoteness with no cell service; emergency response 4+ hours minimum
proTips: Establish good anchor point before the bergschrund for retreat. If it's impassable, turn around.
Travel glacier roped for all party members to prevent solo crevasse falls.
The final rock pitch can be wet and icy - test holds carefully and use helmets.
Perfect Pass (6,300 ft) makes an excellent high camp; water available from snow patches.
Route finding on approach is non-trivial. Carry a GPS and know the terrain - fog or darkness can force unplanned bivy.
watchOut: Moat (crevasse gap) immediately before summit rocks can be 5+ feet wide and difficult to cross - may be impassable late in season
Fixed protection on final pitch exists but inspect carefully before trusting old bolts
Approach requires 3-4 days of travel; do not attempt as day climb from road
Weather can change rapidly; stable forecast is essential
River crossings on approach may be difficult in high water (early summer)
routes.comms: No cellular service in the Pickets. Satellite communicator (Garmin inReach, Spot) strongly recommended for emergency contact.
routes.detailedRack: Climbing quickdraws (6-8), small to medium cams (0.5-3 inches), 4-5 slings, 2-3 locking carabiners. Fixed protection exists on final pitch but bring backup gear.
routes.proNeeds: Protection is sparse on the rock pitch. Rely heavily on fixed bolts on final pitch, supplemented with small cams in cracks. Most protection is placed for belaying at the base of pitch rather than on it.
whatToBring: Water bottles or bladder (3+ liters capacity)

timing.recommendedStart: Start from Hannegan Campground day 1 at dawn; reach Perfect Pass by day 3; summit day 4.

routes.turnaround: Do not push past 2pm on summit day - allow sufficient daylight for descent and unexpected delays. If bergschrund is impassable or weather worsens, retreat immediately.
routes.bail: From Perfect Pass, retreat via same route back to Hannegan Pass and trail (6-8 hours). From glacier, if unable to climb final pitch, descend glacier and retreat via Whatcom Pass or Big Beaver route.
routes.itinerary: Day 1 (Hannegan Campground to Copper Creek Camp): Hike Hannegan Pass Trail to Hannegan Pass, descend to Chilliwack River crossing, reach Copper Creek Camp (~8 miles, 5,500 ft gain-loss). Day 2 (Copper Creek to Easy Ridge/High Camp): Ascend Easy Ridge to Perfect Pass area (~8 miles, 4,000 ft gain). Day 3 (Perfect Pass to Challenger Glacier): Traverse to Challenger Glacier, establish high camp near glacier arm (~2 miles, 1,000 ft gain). Day 4 (Summit): Climb Challenger Glacier to summit and return to high camp (5-6 hours climbing). Day 5 (Return to Hannegan): Retrace route to Hannegan Campground (15+ hours).

climate.typical: Marine west coast climate with high precipitation. Wet, heavy snow falls frequently in winter and spring. Dry, stable weather periods in July-August.
climate.spring: High avalanche danger. Snow can be deep and unstable. Frequent wet snow conditions. Plan for 3-4 additional days vs. summer timing.
climate.summer: Generally dry and cool (35-50F). High pressure systems bring clear skies. Crevasse danger higher due to snow bridge weakening. Occasional afternoon thunderstorms.
climate.fall: Increasing cloud cover and precipitation. Early storms possible by late September. Good climbing window until late September if forecast is stable.
climate.winter: Extremely heavy snowfall and avalanche danger. Park gates closed. Peak not typically climbed November-May.

routes.overview: Alternative approach to Mount Challenger using Wiley Ridge from the Big Beaver Creek side. Avoids some of the Hannegan Pass approach distance but involves significant bushwhacking.
routes.beta: Access via Ross Lake water taxi to Big Beaver Campground. Hike Big Beaver Trail to approximately mile 7-8, then bushwhack south-southwest up Wiley Ridge. Ridge approach leads to Challenger Glacier arm at ~6,800 feet. Ascend glacier to summit as per standard route.
routes.approach: From Big Beaver Campground (accessible via Ross Lake water taxi or hiking Little Beaver Trail), hike Big Beaver Trail north for 7-8 miles. Leave trail and bushwhack steeply upslope to locate and ascend Wiley Ridge. Navigate off-trail terrain for approximately 2-3 miles to reach Challenger Glacier arm at ~6,800 feet.
routes.descent: Reverse route or descend via Challenger Glacier standard route.
routes.descentText: From summit, descend Challenger Glacier and navigate back down Wiley Ridge through dense forest to Big Beaver Trail. Retrace to Big Beaver Campground and water taxi out.
routes.bestSeason: July-August offer clearest routes and best visibility for navigation through dense forest.

blurb: Mount Fury (8,301 ft) is the second-highest peak in the Northern Picket Range within North Cascades National Park, Whatcom County, Washington, featuring two summits of equal height with small glaciers on its slopes. The peak is renowned for its technical alpine/mixed climbing routes—particularly the SE Glacier approach via the East Peak (easier, Ptarmigan Club FA 1930s) and the North Buttress (IV 5.8 alpine mixed). It's one of Washington's most remote and committing 4-5 day mountaineering objectives, characterized by lengthy approaches through wilderness, exposed scrambling, glacier travel, and complex route-finding.
hierarchyNote: Elevation discrepancy: database lists 8,268 ft; authoritative sources (Wikipedia, PeakVisor, Alpinist) report 8,301 ft (2,530 m). Peak has two distinct summits (East Peak ~8,280 ft, West Peak ~8,320 ft, nearly equal height). West Peak is USGS-labeled official summit but less frequently climbed.

routes.overview: The standard approach to Mount Fury's East Peak via glacier and snow climbing from Luna Basin. Most popular and accessible of the Fury routes for competent alpinists with mixed mountaineering skills.
routes.beta: Multi-day expedition from Ross Lake via Big Beaver Trail and Access Creek approach. Requires full mountaineering kit including ice axe and crampons for glacier/snow travel. Class 3-4 terrain with steep snow/glacier slopes (20-40 degrees). Popular with guided parties. Takes approximately 4 days round-trip.
routes.approach: Day 1: Drive SR 20 to Ross Dam Trailhead parking (13.4 miles east of Newhalem). 20-minute hike to dock. Water taxi across Ross Lake to Big Beaver Campground ($40-60/person). Hike Big Beaver Trail approximately 9.4 miles to Luna Camp (trail maintained by NPS, generally pleasant hiking). Day 2: Off-trail from Luna Camp. Cross Big Beaver Creek, ascend north side of Access Creek through forest and slide alder. Follow cairns and ascending terrain through gully system to 6,100 ft notch. Traverse exposed heather slopes to Luna Peak high camp at 7,200 ft. Day 3: From high camp, navigate to SE glacier base via snow slopes and talus. Ascend right side of glacier with possible bergschrund crossing to reach upper glacier.
routes.descent: Down SE Glacier via same route, reversing approach carefully. Use downclimbing on moderate snow and glacier terrain. Final day: return via Luna Camp trail back to Big Beaver.
routes.descentText: Descend the SE Glacier on the same line used for ascent. Continue down to Luna Col and traverse back across heather and talus to the high camp. From high camp, descend through Access Creek basin following cairns and markers back to Big Beaver Creek crossing. Follow flagged/cairn route through forest to Big Beaver Trail and hike to campground for water taxi return to trailhead.
routes.fa: Ptarmigan Climbing Club, 1930s (East Peak first ascent; original FA party registry preserved in summit register)
routes.season: mid-July to late August, possibly September with stable weather

timing.recommendedStart: Early morning (3-5 AM) to avoid afternoon weather

routes.detailedRack: Full alpine rack recommended for crevasse rescue capability: 60m dynamic rope, harness, belay device, locking carabiners (3), standard carabiners (5-6), pickets/deadman for glacier anchors, avalanche safety kit (beacon, probe, shovel).
whatToBring: Stove and fuel (extra due to elevation/cold)
Sunscreen and lip balm with SPF
proTips: Start extremely early on summit day (3-5 AM) to maximize daylight hours and avoid afternoon weather deterioration
Bergschrund crossing is often the crux finding the best crossing point; scout carefully or ask recent ascent parties
Camp selection: Luna Col or 150 ft below on east side offers the best vantage and wind protection
In early season expect deep snow from Luna Camp onward; snowshoes or skis may expedite approach
Bushwhacking through wet slide alder and devil's club on approach is relentless; timing after dry weather helps
watchOut: Bergschrund: Late season opens significantly; gaping crevasses below have turned around many parties
Avalanche terrain: Ascent and especially descent pass through slopes that can release with new snow or rapid warming
Crevasses: Glacier is heavily crevassed; do not travel solo on glacier—rope team mandatory
Route-finding: Approach in early season may be completely snow-covered; poor visibility + whiteout makes navigation extremely difficult
knownHazards: Bergschrund crossing just below summit (climbers report gaping crevasse blocking ascent in certain years)
Avalanche-prone slopes on approach (20-40 degree terrain with rapid melt cycles and rockfall from above)
Heavy crevasse field on SE Glacier (multiple crevasses require probing, bridging, or rappel lines)
Exposed mixed terrain with loose rock on upper ridge sections
Rapidly changeable alpine weather with fog/whiteout obscuring route-finding
routes.turnaround: Establish a firm turnaround time (e.g., 2:00 PM from summit) independent of how close you are to the peak. The descent is technical and time-consuming; descending in darkness or deteriorating weather on unfamiliar terrain is life-threatening.
routes.bestSeason: Mid-July through late August represents the optimal climbing window. During this period, main snow patches and glaciers are well-consolidated for reliable travel, trail access is clear below Luna Camp, and temperatures are warmest.
routes.comms: No cell service anywhere on approach or peak. Satellite communicators (inReach, SPOT) are strongly recommended given remote location and 40+ km from emergency services.

routes.overview: Classic alpine mixed route featuring steep rock, ice, and snow on the north aspect of the East Peak. Grade IV 5.8 technical climb with multiple pitches of rock and complex route-finding. One of the more demanding routes on the Picket Range.
routes.beta: Multi-pitch alpine rock and mixed climb with sections of 5.5, 5.8, and 5.9 free climbing, snow arête traverses, and 50+ degree ice. Approximately 20+ pitches of technical/moderate terrain plus scrambling. Route-finding crucial.
routes.approach: Start as per SE Glacier route to Luna Col high camp. From Luna Col/Luna Lake area, navigate west to the base of the North Buttress on an avalanche-fed glacier. Follow the glacier, avoiding crevasses and bergschrund, to the foot of the North Buttress proper at approximately 6,900-7,000 ft. Ascend snow ramps on the west side of the buttress to reach the first rock pitches.
routes.descent: Descend typically via same route or linked descent involving rappels and downclimbing. In good conditions, some parties descend into glacier basin and traverse back to high camp. Plan for 6-8 rappels (50m cord recommended).
routes.descentText: From the upper snow arête, downclimb or rappel Class 3-4 rock and snow. Descend to the second major snow arête and downclimb or rappel further. Traverse lower and negotiate the steep ice/snow sections downward, staying left of crevasses. Continue descent to glacier base and traverse back to high camp as per ascent approach.
routes.fa: Likely ascended in the mid-20th century by Ptarmigan Club or early Mountaineers; exact FA party unknown
routes.season: mid-July to late August (brief window; late season rockfall and slush)

timing.recommendedStart: 3:00 AM from high camp to maximize daylight

gear: Rock protection: 0.5-2.0 inch cams x2 of each size; stoppers x8-12
routes.detailedRack: Full alpine rock rack essential: 60-70m rope (double or 1-2 twin), rock protection to 2 inches (cams size 0.4-2.0, stoppers), quickdraws (8-10), carabiners (10+), locking carabiners (3-4), belay device, pickets for glacier anchors (2-3), avalanche rescue kit.
whatToBring: First aid kit (comprehensive for remote care)
Climbing-specific tools: off-width chimney pads, rock boots
proTips: The North Buttress route-finding is complex; study existing trip reports and photos beforehand
Pitches 1-3 (initial rock) are often the crux in route-finding; clear/cairned line is not obvious
Snow/ice conditions vary dramatically year-to-year; recent ascent reports (within 1-2 weeks) are critical
Simul-climbing sections are common to make time, but assess rock quality and exposure carefully before simul-ing
Bring extra Spectra cord (7-8mm) or webbing for anchor building; fixed anchors on this route are sparse
watchOut: Rock quality highly variable: 'raging choss' reported in sections; use caution placing protection
Extensive crevasse field at glacier base; do not travel solo on glacier
Rockfall hazard from above, especially during warming cycles; wear helmet and move quickly through exposed terrain
Weather window is short; afternoon thunderstorms can develop rapidly
Complex route-finding: whitish/patchy snow can make the line indistinct
knownHazards: Variable rock quality reported (granodiorite with occasional choss sections)
Crevassed glacier requiring rope team travel and probing
Steep ice/snow sections (50+ degrees) requiring cramponing or step-kicking
Exposed rock scrambling on upper ridge sections with thousands of feet of exposure
Multiple rappel descents with inadequate fixed anchors
routes.turnaround: Establish a turnaround time (e.g., 2:00-3:00 PM from the base of buttress), independent of summit progress. If turnaround time is reached and weather is deteriorating, retreat immediately.
routes.bestSeason: Mid-July to late August, with July offering more stable weather windows and August ice conditions hardening.
routes.comms: No cell service. Satellite communicator essential given remoteness and complexity.

routes.overview: Direct ascent of the East Ridge with a notable rock climbing crux. This route avoids the steep lower glacier of the SE Glacier approach, instead climbing Class 4 scrambling with a 20-foot Class 5.4 rock step. Good option for mid-late summer when glacier travel is minimal. Exposed ridge with significant exposure on both sides.
routes.beta: Ascend talus and scree slopes to reach the east ridge proper. Follow the ridge with Class 3-4 scrambling (loose rock common). A distinctive 20-foot vertical rock step (Class 5.4) guards the upper ridge. Above the rock step, continue Class 3-4 scrambling to summit. Total route involves significant exposure with thousands of feet of drop on either side.
routes.approach: Same initial approach as SE Glacier route via Big Beaver Trail and Access Creek to Luna Basin high camp area. From the basin at 6,600 ft elevation, make an ascending traverse northwest to a notch at 7,050 ft just east of Point 7,850. This notch is the key landmark for the Direct East Ridge line.
routes.descent: Downclimb the rock step (5.4, downclimbing is on manageable terrain) or rappel if less comfortable with downclimbing. Reverse the ridge via Class 3-4 downclimbing back to the notch and descend the approach terrain (talus, scree, heather) back to high camp.
routes.descentText: From the summit area, downclimb Class 3-4 terrain along the east ridge. At the rock step, carefully downclimb the 5.4 grade or set up rappel with natural anchor/slings if preferred. Continue downclimbing Class 3-4 over loose rock to the notch at 7,050 ft. Descend talus and scree slopes back toward high camp. Total descent time: 6-8 hours.
routes.season: Mid-late summer (July-August, especially August-early September) when glacier is melted back and rock is dry

timing.recommendedStart: Early morning, 4:00-5:00 AM from camp

gear: Climbing shoes (optional but helpful for 5.4 crux)
25-30m rope (for rappel option; can be avoided if comfortable downclimbing)
routes.detailedRack: Minimal: climbing shoes (optional), harness, 25-30m cord or rope (for emergency rappel/anchor), helmet. Route is mostly scrambling with one short rock pitch; no significant protection rack needed unless parties want to place gear on the 5.4 step.
proTips: Route-finding is straightforward compared to other Fury routes; ascending traverse to the notch at 7,050 ft is the key landmark
The 20-foot 5.4 rock step is often the crux and the most exposed section; good rock quality in most conditions
Late summer/early September: rock dries out nicely, making the 5.4 step more climbable than in wet conditions
Avoid crampons on upper ridge; sticky climbing shoes recommended for the rock step
watchOut: East Ridge: the exposure is substantial; loose footing can be catastrophic
Rock quality: while generally good, some sections are loose; test holds carefully
Exposure on 'sidewalk in the sky' narrow ledge section: not technical but very exposed
Route-finding on descent: in clouds or poor visibility, retracing the route can be difficult
knownHazards: Sustained Class 4 scrambling with loose rock requiring careful foot placement
Vertical rock step (5.4) with significant exposure
Thousands of feet of exposure on both sides of narrow ridge
Loose rock throughout route (typical Picket Range quality)
Weather exposure on upper ridge; no shelter options
Early season snow/ice on rock makes climbing difficult
routes.turnaround: Turnaround time of 1:00-2:00 PM from the rock step crux, independent of summit distance. If weather is deteriorating or you haven't reached the crux by 1:00 PM, descend.
routes.bestSeason: Mid-late August is optimal when rock has dried, glacier has melted back completely, and ice on the ridge is minimal.
routes.comms: No cell service; satellite communicator recommended

routes.overview: Alternative approach or descent route utilizing the south side of Mount Fury. Less documented than standard SE Glacier approach but used in some multiday traverse sequences and as a descent option from either peak. Primarily described as heather traverses and scree slopes on the south face.
routes.beta: South-side terrain involves descending and traversing heather slopes toward the southeast glacier at approximately 6,700 ft elevation. Route often used in conjunction with Outrigger Peak (adjacent to Fury) in multiday Picket Range traverses. Less commonly ascended as primary route; more often used as descent or alternative egress.
routes.approach: Access via same Luna Basin approach as SE Glacier route. From high camp area, navigate south and southwest across heather slopes at high elevation (7,000-7,500 ft range). Traverse toward south face of the peak, descending into the broad basin south of Mount Fury.
routes.descent: Descend south side heather slopes and scree terrain back to lower glacial basins or approach a different peak in traverse sequence. Route-finding is generally straightforward (traversing at constant elevation across heather), but exposed terrain (thousands of feet exposure) requires care.
routes.descentText: From the peak area, descend south and southwest across heather slopes, losing elevation gradually toward the 6,700-6,800 ft basins. Avoid cliffs and use established heather trails when possible. Some parties cross to Outrigger Pass area (between Fury and Outrigger Peak) to continue traverses. Descent is steep but not technical; allow 4-6 hours for descent to basin.
routes.season: Mid-July to late August, possibly early September

timing.recommendedStart: Early morning from camp

gear: Ice axe (for early season snow if present)
routes.detailedRack: Minimal alpine rack; route is primarily scrambling on heather. Ice axe and crampons useful if residual snow on upper elevation sections, but not always necessary.
proTips: South side heather slopes are generally less stable than rock/glacier; test footing carefully
This route works well as part of a larger Pickets traverse (e.g., linking Challenger, Fury, Outrigger)
Heather terrain can be slick when wet; avoid traversing after rain
Cairn markers may exist from prior parties; follow them when visible to stay on the most stable ground
watchOut: Heather slopes can be unstable and slick, especially when wet
Significant exposure: thousands of feet of drop on either side of narrow heather ridges
Route-finding via heather traverses can be indistinct in clouds or poor visibility
Rock outcrops mixed with heather can have loose blocks; test handholds carefully
knownHazards: Unstable heather slopes (loose plant material can shift underfoot)
Exposed high-elevation terrain with significant drops
Route-finding challenges in poor visibility
Loose rock blocks mixed with heather
Weather exposure; afternoon thunderstorms
Early season snow/ice hazard
routes.turnaround: If weather deteriorates or route-finding becomes unclear, retreat immediately. Exposed heather terrain is dangerous in clouds or rain.
routes.bestSeason: Mid-late August optimal; July can have residual snow; early September possible with stable weather
routes.comms: No cell service; satellite communicator recommended

routes.overview: The classic alpine rock route on Ingalls Peak's North Peak, featuring excellent peridotite rock and moderate climbing with consistent protection. This Grade II 5.4 is the most popular route on the peak and an excellent introduction to alpine rock climbing.
routes.beta: A four-pitch rock climb on the South Ridge of the North Peak, following a direct line up good-quality rock with well-defined crack systems. The climbing is mostly moderate 5.3-5.4 with occasional 5.6 moves depending on route variation. Route-finding at the base is straightforward, ascending directly up from the col between North and South peaks.
routes.fa: Keith Lankin and Ken Solberg, May 30, 1941
routes.approach: From Esmerelda Trailhead parking lot, take trail #1394 for a short distance, then branch right onto Longs Pass Trail #1229, then left onto Ingalls Way Trail #1390 toward Ingalls Pass. Ascend to Ingalls Pass (6,500 ft, approximately 3 miles), then descend into Headlight Basin and traverse east to Ingalls Lake (6,466 ft). From the lake, ascend talus and slabs up the left (south) side, passing through Class 2-3 terrain to reach the notch (saddle) between North and South Ingalls peaks. The South Ridge begins at this col.
routes.descent: Scramble northwest down a loose gravel ridge to a minor saddle. Descend and traverse left to a tree at the top of the gully used on ascent, then rappel down the gully (3 total rappels with 60m rope) and friction slab to the talus base. From there, reverse the approach to Ingalls Lake and descend via Ingalls Way Trail.
routes.descentText: The descent from the summit follows three rappel stations. From the summit, rappel using three bolts down to the ledge atop Pitch 2 (ensure rope is long enough to avoid entanglement in cracks). Rappel again to the top of Pitch 1, then make a final rappel to reach the talus field below. Be cautious of rope-eating cracks at the belay anchors. Once off the rock, descend the Class 3 gully and talus back to Ingalls Lake, following cairns as needed.
routes.turnaround: With quality rock and consistent protection, objective hazard risk is low. Reverse descent by climbing back down the ascent route, with proper rappel setup to minimize time on steep terrain. In case of emergency, establish a bivy at high camp or descend to Ingalls Lake for helicopter evacuation access.
routes.bestSeason: June through October, with optimal conditions in June (peak climbing season, 62% of ascents) and October (25% of ascents) when alpine larches display golden color. Early season (June) may have snow in the couloirs and require ice axe; August brings dry rock and hot conditions; September-October offers clear conditions with minimal snow and excellent weather stability.
watchOut: Loose rock hazard: Much loose rock on upper pitches and ridge; wear helmets and avoid dislodging material
Rockfall hazard: At approximately 6,900 ft elevation, increased rockfall risk requires helmets and awareness of parties above
Wedging cracks: Ropes can wedge in cracks during rappel descent, particularly between Pitch 2 and Pitch 3 anchors
Route-finding: Exposed sections with loose gravel require careful foot placement and awareness of drop-offs
knownHazards: Loose rock on ridges and final summit approach, particularly on the descent
Exposure along ridge traverse with 500+ ft of exposure on some sections
Rope management during three-rappel descent; single-rope raps can be too short
Cracks below top anchor that can trap ropes during rappel
proTips: Bring webbing for potential anchor replacement at rap stations
Use a double-rope rappel setup with 60m rope to reach each station
Establish a clear anchor system before beginning descent to minimize rope issues
Start early; many parties on weekends create delays and route-finding congestion
The left crack system on Pitch 2 is 5.4 and well-protected; the middle crack (5.6) is cleaner but requires stronger climbing
routes.detailedRack: Standard trad rack to 2 inches (small cams BD 0.5-2, full nut set, hex set). Bring doubles of smaller sizes for potential crack trends. 5 quickdraws and double-length slings for transitions. A 60-meter rope is mandatory for the three-rappel descent. Webbing (1-inch) is recommended for anchor reinforcement.
routes.proNeeds: Natural-sized cracks throughout the route accept a standard trad rack. Minimal bolts at belay stations (3 bolts at summit, 2 at Pitch 3 anchor). Protection is consistently available with proper placement; climbers should be comfortable with crack protection and anchor construction.
whatToBring: Rock climbing shoes (approach in approach shoes)

timing.recommendedStart: 4:00 AM to 5:00 AM from trailhead

pitchDetail.notes: Low-angle slab from the dirt, easy ground, class 4-5 terrain leading to 40-foot section of 5th class climbing with an open book section featuring well-worn crack
Full 60-meter pitch on left-side cracks with traverses. Sustained 5.4 climbing to bolted belay ledge (two bolts). Multiple crack options with left at 5.4 and middle at 5.6
Short pitch with mantling move through open book section; exits to easier terrain with final summit slab
Short final section with serpentine slabs to summit; bolted anchor (three bolts) at top

routes.bail: From Pitch 2 ledge, reverse down Pitch 1 or rappel to re-attempt with fresh rope. From higher pitches, rappel directly down the South Ridge using existing anchor bolts, then traverse to Ingalls Lake for evacuation. The route can be aborted at the Ingalls Col/North-South Peak saddle to descend via South Peak (Class 2-3) without committing to technical climbing.

routes.overview: A scenic Class 2-3 scramble ascent of South Ingalls Peak (7,640 ft) via the North Ridge from Ingalls Col, featuring peridotite rock and straightforward scrambling with excellent views of Mount Stuart and surrounding alpine terrain. This route is less technical than the North Peak's rock climbs but requires careful foot placement on exposed terrain.
routes.beta: A Class 2-3 scramble ascent of South Ingalls Peak from the col between North and South peaks. The route follows the North Ridge/east-facing slope from Ingalls Col, ascending chunky peridotite rock with several variations. Most climbing is Class 2 with occasional exposed Class 3 sections. Route-finding is straightforward with cairns marking the way.
routes.approach: Follow the South Ridge approach to the North-South Peak saddle/col (7,100 ft). From this point, the North Ridge ascent begins; scramble northeastward up the chunky Class 2-3 rock, following the ridge crest or just to the right (east) of it. The terrain alternates between stable peridotite and exposed sections. Continue scrambling for approximately 1,600 vertical feet to reach South Ingalls Peak (7,640 ft).
routes.descent: Descend the same route via the North Ridge, carefully managing exposed sections. Reverse scrambling down the ridge to Ingalls Col, then retrace the approach descent to Ingalls Lake and out via trail. Take care on slippery serpentinite bands; stable peridotite offers much better footing.
routes.descentText: Descend via the North Ridge return, retracing your ascent line down the ridge. Exposed Class 3 sections require deliberate foot placement and attention to anchor points. The glassy serpentinite surfaces found on shear zones are particularly slippery and require care. Once at Ingalls Col, reverse down the approach talus and slabs to Ingalls Lake.
routes.turnaround: A straightforward descent via the same route. In case of emergency or inclement weather, retreat to the saddle and make your way down to Ingalls Lake for evacuation. The exposure is generally moderate; rappel anchors are not necessary for this Class 2-3 scramble.
routes.bestSeason: June through October, with June and October offering the most stable weather (peak larch viewing in October). Snow can linger on north-facing slopes into July, requiring careful navigation.
watchOut: Exposed terrain: The North Ridge is exposed with several hundred feet of exposure; slip hazard on certain sections
Serpentinite bands: Glassy, slippery surfaces on altered rock create insecure footing; stick to peridotite when possible
Loose rock: Peridotite weathering produces loose blocks; avoid kicking down material on those below
Route-finding: Early season (June) snow can obscure the ridge line; cairns provide guidance
knownHazards: Exposure along the ridge with up to 500+ feet of drop on sides
Slippery serpentinite bands with 'glassy surface' require extreme care
Peridotite block fall from weathering; wear helmets for upper sections
Early season snow bridges that can collapse into hidden crevasses (unlikely but possible)
proTips: The peridotite offers 'extremely fun scrambling' compared to slippery serpentinite
Use cairn cairns to navigate if snow is present; the ridge line is clear but route-finding matters
Early morning ascent minimizes exposure to afternoon thunderstorms and loosened rock
Bivy in Headlight Basin to avoid early morning scrambling in cold temps
gear: Helmet (rockfall hazard from above and loose rock)
Approach shoes with good grip (Approach rocks require secure footing)
Minimal climbing gear needed (Class 3 scramble only)
60m rope for emergency rappel if needed (optional but recommended)
routes.detailedRack: No technical climbing protection required; Class 2-3 scramble only. A light rope (60m optional) could provide emergency self-rescue capability on exposed sections, though most parties do not carry it.
routes.proNeeds: No artificial protection required. Route-finding and careful foot placement manage objective hazard.

pitchDetail.notes: Continuous scrambling up the North Ridge on peridotite and serpentinite. Peridotite sections offer solid, fun scrambling; serpentinite bands are slippery and require care
