routes.overview: A short, scenic 2-pitch alpine rock route on East Ingalls Peak (7,480 ft) featuring easy to moderate climbing on sound peridotite rock with excellent views. This Grade I 5.3 route is an excellent choice for alpine rock beginners or as an extension to the East Ridge.
routes.beta: A two-pitch rock climb on the Southeast Face of East Ingalls Peak starting from the notch between East and North Ingalls peaks. Pitch 1 ascends slabs and a gully to a belay ledge. Pitch 2 trends right along the ridge to the summit. Mixed rock quality with some loose sections on the upper ridge; overall sound climbing with straightforward protection.
routes.fa: Gene Prater, Bill Prater, and Stan Butcharo, November 1952
routes.approach: Hike to Ingalls Lake (see South Ridge approach for details). From the lake, ascend talus and slabs up the left (south) side to reach the notch between East and North Ingalls peaks (the North-South Peak saddle/col at ~7,100 ft). A chockstone marks the start of the Southeast Face route at this col. From this point, scramble 15 feet on Class 3/4 terrain to reach the belay ledge at the base of Pitch 1.
routes.descent: Descend via three rappel stations anchored with slings and bolts on the Southeast Face. From the summit, rappel down the face back to the upper belay ledge, then rappel again to the lower belay, and finally to the base of the route at the chockstone.
routes.descentText: Rappel stations are established at three points down the Southeast Face. From the summit, use slings or anchor gear to set up rappels (bolts are present at the stations). A 60-meter rope may be required depending on rappel configuration. Once at the base chockstone, descend via talus and slabs back down to Ingalls Lake.
routes.turnaround: The short route length allows quick turnaround in adverse weather. If conditions deteriorate or energy is insufficient, retreat to the col and make your way down to Ingalls Lake.
routes.bestSeason: June through October, with optimal conditions June (peak climbing season) and October (stable weather, larch viewing). June may have snow lingering on north-facing approach slopes.
watchOut: Loose rock: The upper ridge features a lot of loose rock; use helmets and avoid dislodging material
Exposure: Mixed terrain with 300+ feet of exposure on the descent; secure rope management required
Route-finding: Slabs trend left initially, then right; the transition can be confusing
knownHazards: Loose rock on the upper ridge requires helmets and careful foot placement
Exposure with potential 300+ foot drops; rappel descent requires secure anchor setup
Mixed rock quality with some loose sections; solid protection less available than South Ridge
Early season snow can obscure the chockstone start point
proTips: This route is a great alternative to the more crowded South Ridge for lower-traffic climbing
Use slings on the anchors to reinforce bolts, as rock quality varies and anchors may need replacement
The 2-pitch length makes this ideal for a quick alpine climb or warm-up before longer routes
Early start recommended to secure the col before other parties arrive
routes.detailedRack: Light trad rack to 1.5 inches. Single small-to-medium cams and nut set for protection. Slings for anchor construction and backup.
routes.proNeeds: Shallow cracks and limited placements on this route; climbers should be experienced with minimalist protection and creative anchoring on poor-quality rock sections.

pitchDetail.notes: Slabs initially trending slightly left to a gully, reaching belay ledge. 15 feet of Class 3/4 approach scrambling to reach the pitch start
Trend right and follow the ridge to the summit. Loose rock on upper ridge requires helmet and careful foot placement

routes.bail: From Pitch 1 belay, retreat down Pitch 1 to the col and descend to Ingalls Lake via talus and slabs.

blurb: Ingalls Peak is a 7,662-foot triple-summit alpine rock climbing destination in Washington's Alpine Lakes Wilderness, located 2 miles west of Mount Stuart in the North Cascades. Composed of unusually sound peridotite and serpentinite from the Ingalls Tectonic Complex (Jurassic mantle rocks), the peak offers several classic moderate rock routes on generally reliable stone. First ascended by members of The Mountaineers club in 1925, the peak's South Ridge (5.4, 1941 FA) remains one of the most popular alpine rock routes in the Cascades, attracting climbers with its accessible 5-mile approach and short, quality climbing pitches.
hierarchyNote: Peak confirmed: Ingalls Peak, 7,662 ft, North Cascades at 47.4734506°N, 120.9464785°W, part of Alpine Lakes Wilderness boundary between Kittitas and Chelan counties. Coordinates and elevation verified across multiple authoritative sources. Name is unambiguous; the peak is well-known in climbing and hiking communities. Hierarchy placement in North Cascades is correct; altitude and geography match all primary sources.

GEOGRAPHIC MISMATCH: This peak is actually located in OREGON (Jefferson and Linn counties), not Washington. Three Fingered Jack is in the central Oregon Cascade Range between Mount Jefferson and the Three Sisters, approximately 20 miles northwest of Sisters, Oregon. The elevation (7,841-7,844 ft) and name match the Oregon peak. No corresponding peak named "Three Fingered Jack" exists in Washington's North Cascades/Cascades range. The database entry has an incorrect state classification.
blurb: Three Fingered Jack is a Pleistocene shield volcano in Oregon's central Cascade Range, formed 250,000-500,000 years ago from basaltic andesite lava and deeply eroded by glaciation into a distinctive knife-edge ridge. First climbed on September 3, 1923, by Ervin McNeal, Phil Philbrook, Armin Furrer, Wilbur Watkins, Leo Harryman, and Ronald Sellars, the peak is known for poor volcanic rock quality but offers a popular alpine rock-climbing experience with excellent views of surrounding volcanic peaks.

routes.overview: The standard and only recommended route on Three Fingered Jack, ascending the south ridge via Pacific Crest Trail approach and featuring a distinctive exposed traverse ("The Crawl") and 5.2 technical chimney near the summit.
routes.fa: September 3, 1923 by Ervin McNeal, Phil Philbrook, Armin Furrer, Wilbur Watkins, Leo Harryman, and Ronald Sellars
routes.beta: The most popular and only recommended route on Three Fingered Jack. Approach via Pacific Crest Trail from Santiam Pass for 4.7 miles, then branch onto climbers' trail ascending toward the ridge proper. Route traverses upward on scree slopes to reach the South Ridge, then follows the exposed ridge with sections called 'The Crawl' (steep exposed traverse with handline protection) and a short 5.2 chimney/crux pitch below the summit plug. The crux involves moderate cams for protection and reaches the summit anchor with a 30-meter rope.
routes.approach: From Santiam Pass trailhead (on US-20 east of Hoodoo ski area), hike north on Pacific Crest Trail for 4.7-5 miles through burnt forest area gaining ~600m (approximately 2,000 feet). Then pick up climbers' trail that branches from PCT and ascends toward the south ridge, gaining scree slopes leading to the ridge proper.
routes.descent: Two rappels with 30-meter rope from summit anchor back to ridge base, then scramble/hike descent to PCT.
routes.descentText: From the summit, set up rappel anchor using existing webbing or your own (bring backup webbing and rap rings). Two 30-meter rappels are standard descent: first rappel from summit plug back to the chimney base, second rappel down to ridge saddle. Once back at the ridge base, descend scree slopes and climbers' trail retracing the ascent route back to the PCT, then hike out the PCT to Santiam Pass trailhead.
routes.bestSeason: June through October offers ideal conditions. Summer (July-September) provides stable weather and minimal snow. Late May through early June requires snow climbing skills and carries avalanche risk. Avoid winter due to 400+ inches annual snowfall, avalanche danger, and frequent pass closures.
knownHazards: Poor volcanic rock quality (loose andesite scree) throughout the climb except near summit crux
Loose rockfall from climbers above or below due to unstable terrain; helmet essential
Exposure on 'The Crawl' with steep cliffs and drops exceeding 1,000 feet on either side of summit ridge
Fog-related disorientation reported in past incidents requiring rescue
Fixed protection may be worn or unsafe; bring backup gear
Loose tephra deposits on summit block subject to collapse
Avalanche risk on north and northeast faces in winter/early spring
watchOut: The rock is friable and rotten except near the summit crux; test all holds before committing weight
Most climbers do not rope up on the 4th class traverse, but significant exposure warrants extreme caution
Secure belay setups on poor rock require redundant protection; avoid relying on single placements
The chimney crux is short but bold; a single 0.5 Camalot in the discontinuous crack plus slung horn provide standard protection
Helmet is mandatory due to rockfall hazard and loose terrain throughout the climb

pitchDetail.notes: Scrambling and 4th class sections from ridge base to 'The Crawl' exposed traverse; significant exposure with two fixed pins; ~250 vertical feet below summit. Most parties do not rope this section but handholds are substantial.

Crux chimney pitch just below summit plug; steep but relatively short (~15 feet); discontinuous crack on right side takes medium cams; single 0.5 Camalot typical placement with slung horn backup protection. Top out directly to summit block.

gear: 30-meter rope (single or doubled depending on descent plan)
routes.detailedRack: Light alpine rock rack to 3 inches: medium cams (#0.4-0.75 Camalots recommended), medium nuts, various slings for protection and anchors. Two 30-meter single ropes or one 50-meter rope for descent. Bring backup webbing and rap rings in case in-situ gear is worn.
routes.proNeeds: Entry-level multipitch rock climbing experience required. Comfort with 5.2 technical rock, ability to place and test gear on poor rock, confident rope work, rappelling, and belay management essential. Prior alpine climbing experience or completion of alpine rock introduction course strongly recommended.
proTips: Start early from the trailhead (pre-dawn) to maximize daylight for the technical sections and descent
Scout 'The Crawl' carefully; rock quality varies and handline may be in poor condition; test alternative holds
Bring a light rack and move efficiently; loose rock degrades with traffic, especially during descent
Most parties set a single 30-meter anchor at the summit and rap from there rather than setting multiple intermediate anchors
In early season (May-June), be aware of snow and rime ice on the crux pitch; this can make the 5.2 feel considerably harder

timing.recommendedStart: Pre-dawn to maximize daylight; typical 6:00-7:00 AM departure from trailhead

climate.typical: Alpine climate with rapid weather changes. Temperature inversions common. Wind can be substantial, especially on the ridge. Afternoon thunderstorms possible in summer.
climate.spring: Highly variable: significant snow coverage at trailhead in May-early June; frozen/rime ice on exposed rock possible; avalanche danger on north aspects; may be impassable due to snowpack
climate.summer: Ideal season (July-September). Mild daytime temps (50-65°F) at summit; freezing possible at night. Stable weather patterns though afternoon storms can develop. Snow typically melts by July at main climbing areas.
climate.fall: September-October: stable weather, clear skies, cool nights (freezing possible). Daylight hours shortening; October snows can occur. Good climbing conditions.
climate.winter: Severe: 400+ inches annual snowfall; Santiam Pass receives averaged over 400 inches annually. Pass frequently closed. Extreme avalanche danger. Rime ice and wind-slab present. Not recommended for most parties.

routes.beta: The North Ridge route is mentioned in climbing guides as a steep and exposed alternative to the South Ridge. It offers a true test of climber skills and endurance with technical terrain and tricky route finding. However, the North and Northeast Faces carry high avalanche risk and are rarely attempted due to the combination of poor rock quality and serious objective hazards. Specific climbing beta, grades, and route sequences are not well documented in available sources.

hierarchyNote: CRITICAL DISCREPANCY: Peak ID 'wa_mount_washington' with WA prefix indicates Washington State location, but extensive research reveals no Mount Washington in Washington state at 7,689 feet with documented North Ridge and Standard climbing routes. Mount Washington in Oregon (Willamette National Forest, 44.38°N -121.80°W, elevation 7,794-7,795 feet) has identical route names and documented characteristics. The 105-foot elevation difference (7,689 vs 7,794 ft) could reflect measurement variance. Recommend verifying peak location: if this is Mount Washington, Oregon, the peak ID should reflect that; if a Washington peak, it requires separate identification as no matching peak exists in Washington at this elevation.
blurb: Mount Washington (7,794 feet) is an eroded volcanic plug in the central Oregon Cascades, located in Willamette National Forest's Mount Washington Wilderness. Known for poor-quality volcanic rock (basalt and andesite) with crumbly texture, the peak offers exposed alpine rock climbing with five classic routes ranging from 5.0 to 5.8. First ascended August 26, 1923 via the North Ridge variation; the peak has become a popular one-day alpine objective despite its hazardous, rotten rock conditions and requires helmet protection on all routes.

routes.overview: The easiest and most direct route to Mount Washington's summit, following the north ridge via scrambling and exposed low-Class 5 rock on poor volcanic terrain. A popular one-day climb combining 6 miles of hiking approach with technical climbing featuring a prominent crux pitch (the Nose) and three to five pitches of Class 3–5 climbing.
routes.beta: The North Ridge is the most popular and easiest route on Mount Washington, varying between Class 3 and low Class 5 with the crux being the Nose section. All routes feature exposed climbing on poor-quality volcanic rock; helmets are essential. Party-induced rockfall is a constant hazard. The climbing is straightforward for the grade but protection and anchors are scarce.
routes.approach: Access via US Highway 20 to Santiam Pass, then follow Forest Road 2690 to Big Lake Recreation Area (3,700 feet). Park at the PCT trailhead and hike south on the Pacific Crest Trail for 3.5 miles to a large cairn marking the climbers' trail junction. Turn east up the climbers' trail, which ascends through mixed forest and open meadows to timberline, following scramble terrain and loose boulder fields toward the North Ridge proper. Total one-way distance: 6 miles; elevation gain: 3,200–3,600 feet.
routes.descent: Descend the climbing route via scrambling and rappels. Use three to four rappel stations (60-meter rope required) to descend steepest Class 4–5 sections, then continue down scree and loose gully terrain. Carefully retrieve ropes (rockfall hazard). Descend outbound PCT trail to trailhead.
routes.descentText: From the summit block, descend approximately 100 feet to established slings and rappel stations. Make three to four rappels on a 60-meter rope (30–50 feet per station) to drop from exposed Class 4–5 terrain to scree and gully terrain. Carefully manage rope retrieval at each station to avoid rockfall hazard. Continue scrambling down the gravel-filled gully and loose boulder fields along the north ridge. Once terrain eases to Class 2–3, follow the obvious descent trail down open meadows and mixed forest back to the climbers' trail junction and PCT. Hike out 3.5 miles to Big Lake trailhead.
routes.itinerary: Day 1 (full approach–descent): 0530–depart Big Lake PCT trailhead; 0630–0730–hike PCT 3.5 miles to climbers' trail junction; 0730–1230–ascend climbers' trail and scree to North Ridge base (4 hours, 3,200 ft gain); 1230–1430–scramble and climb lower ridge terrain (Class 2–3, 1.5 hours, 600 ft); 1430–1545–crux: climb the Nose (75 feet, low Class 5, 1+ hour); 1545–1630–upper ridge scrambling (Class 3–4, 45 min, final 200 ft); 1630–summit (7,794 ft); 1630–1730–descend via rappels (3–4 stations, 1 hour); 1730–1900–scramble/boulder-field descent to climbers' trail junction; 1900–2030–hike out PCT to trailhead (1.5 hours). Round-trip: 9–10 hours.
routes.bail: From the North Ridge approach gully (pre-crux), retreat downslope to the east to regain scree and meadows leading back to the climbers' trail junction. From the crux (Nose), with expert rappel skills, descend via established slings on west side. From the upper ridge, escape downslope to the northeast into gullies and scree leading to meadows and trail. No emergency bivvy shelter is present; daylight completion is essential. In poor weather, turn back at or below timberline.
routes.turnaround: Turn around if you do not summit by 1430 to maintain safe daylight descent margin. Do not continue if weather deteriorates to lightning risk, high winds, or dense cloud cover. Descend at the first sign of afternoon thunderstorm development. If multiple parties are on the route above or below, consider delaying your climb until congestion clears.
routes.bestSeason: July–August (best weather); June and September offer longer daylight but possible snow patches. Afternoon thunderstorms are common in summer; summit early and descend by mid-afternoon.
watchOut: Rockfall hazard from party above and below—helmet essential at all times
Loose, crumbly volcanic rock with poor protection; anchors scarce and suspect
Exposed climbing with runout/suspect sections—leader must not fall
High altitude exposure on Class 4–5 terrain with little protection
Weather deterioration to lightning/thunderstorm in afternoon—climb early and descend by mid-afternoon
Loose rock and falling debris during/after rappel descent—move quickly with caution
knownHazards: Loose and crumbly volcanic rock throughout the route, rated as some of the poorest-quality rock in the Oregon Cascades
Constant rockfall hazard, especially with multiple parties on route simultaneously or when pulling ropes during rappel descent
Poor anchor placement and scarce protection options for belays and rappel stations; careful inspection required
High Alpine Grade V commitment with serious exposure on Class 4–5 sections and minimal protection
Afternoon thunderstorms common in summer months; routes exposed and unprotected from lightning risk
Approach gully and scree slopes can harbor loose rock triggered by parties above
gear: 1 set of cams (sizes 0.5–2.5 most useful, full rack to size 3 worthwhile)
Crampons and ice axe if climbing before mid-June
whatToBring: Helmet and personal climbing kit (as above)
Food and water for 10–12-hour day (2–3 liters water, energy bars/snacks)
First aid kit with extra padding for rockfall injuries
Headlamp (in case of slow descent into twilight)
Rain gear and emergency bivy sack (never climb without)
Satellite messenger or personal locator beacon (cell service unreliable)
routes.detailedRack: A light trad rack is essential. Bring one set of cams (0.5–2.5 doubled, up to size 3) and one set of nuts for protection; small cams are particularly useful on this route. Quickdraws or carabiners (10–12) for clipping protection. Hexes can substitute for cams if preferred. Slings and anchors for anchor building and rappel stations are critical; double-check all anchors for stability before use. A 60-meter rope is necessary for descent rappels (three to four stations typical). Most protection placements will be 'suspect' given rock quality; assume minimal protection value and climb conservatively.
routes.proNeeds: Protection is limited by poor rock quality. Expect marginal placements at best; plan to clip natural features (slings, trees) when available. Bring redundant slings for anchor backup. The crux Nose section may have fixed slings from prior ascents; do not rely solely on these. Establish solid anchors at belays; double-check all rappel anchors. Assume 'leader must not fall' conditions.
proTips: Start very early in the day to maximize daylight for descent
Wear a helmet at all times; rockfall from above is a serious, constant hazard
Scout the crux Nose pitch from the approach before committing to climbing
On descent, move efficiently through rappel stations; lingering increases objective hazard from rockfall
Consider climbing midweek or in shoulder seasons (early June, late Aug) to avoid crowds and reduce rockfall hazard from multiple parties
If lichen or moss is present on rock, expect reduced friction; focus on larger features and cracks for protection
Have a clear escape plan in case you must descend quickly; know the bail route before you climb
routes.fa: Armin Furrer, Leo Harryman, Ervin McNeal, Phil Philbrook, Ron Sellers, and Wilbur Watkins, August 26, 1923 (via north ridge variation; original first-ascent route no longer exists due to rockfall)
routes.comms: Cell service unreliable in the Willamette National Forest backcountry; plan to carry satellite communicator or PLB for emergencies. Big Lake area (trailhead) has intermittent cell signal.

pitchDetail.notes: Scramble and hiking from climbers' trail through scree and loose boulder fields to North Ridge base (~600–800 ft elevation gain, 1–1.5 hours). Terrain steepens as elevation increases; some Class 3 scrambling on loose terrain. Well-marked by cairns and established trail.

Follow north ridge proper upward via scrambling and light climbing, staying on or slightly left of crest. Loose rock and scree interspersed with small Class 4 steps. Views improve; exposure increases. ~600 ft elevation gain, 1–1.5 hours.

75-foot technical crux section. Scramble right 15 feet to reach a left-leaning chimney (30 feet, easy Class 5). Exit chimney at upper lip and climb left-angling face (30 feet, Class 5.2–5.3). Small cams useful; protection marginal. This is the hardest climbing on the route; leader must not fall.

Above crux, continue via Class 3–4 scrambling and low Class 5 steps along ridge crest. Series of small gendarmes and loose rock; exposure on both sides increases. Final 100 feet of summit plug require easy Class 5 scrambling. ~200 ft vertical, 30–45 min to summit.

Three to four rappel stations (40–50 ft each) descend steepest Class 4–5 terrain. Expect loose rock at anchors; carefully retrieve ropes (rockfall hazard). Below rappels, scramble and boulder-hop down scree gully to easier terrain, then hike trail out.

timing.recommendedStart: 0530–0600 (very early to maximize daylight for descent)

routes.overview: The primary and most-climbed line to Mount Washington's summit. Follows the north ridge from base to summit via scrambling and exposed low-Class 5 rock on poor volcanic terrain. Identical to the Standard Route; the North Ridge route is the established standard and is considered the 'North Ridge variation' of the first ascent line (original route lost to rockfall).
routes.beta: The North Ridge is the established standard climbing route on Mount Washington. Follow the north ridge from base to summit, staying on or near the ridge crest and dealing with Class 3–4 scrambling, loose rock, and multiple pitches of Class 3–5 climbing. The crux is the Nose section (75 feet, Class 5.3) featuring a chimney and face climbing. Hazards include poor rock quality, sparse protection, and rockfall from above. Helmets are essential at all times.
routes.approach: Access via US Highway 20 to Santiam Pass, then follow Forest Road 2690 to Big Lake Recreation Area (3,700 feet). Park at the PCT trailhead and hike south on the Pacific Crest Trail for 3.5 miles to a large cairn marking the climbers' trail junction. Turn east up the climbers' trail, which ascends through mixed forest and open meadows to timberline, following scramble terrain and loose boulder fields toward the North Ridge proper. Total one-way distance: 6 miles; elevation gain: 3,200–3,600 feet.
routes.descent: Descend the climbing route via scrambling and rappels. Use three to four rappel stations (60-meter rope required) to descend steepest Class 4–5 sections, then continue down scree and loose gully terrain. Carefully manage rope retrieval (rockfall hazard). Descend outbound PCT trail to trailhead.
routes.descentText: From the summit block, descend approximately 100 feet to established slings and rappel stations. Make three to four rappels on a 60-meter rope (30–50 feet per station) to drop from exposed Class 4–5 terrain to scree and gully terrain. Carefully manage rope retrieval at each station to avoid rockfall hazard. Continue scrambling down the gravel-filled gully and loose boulder fields along the north ridge. Once terrain eases to Class 2–3, follow the obvious descent trail down open meadows and mixed forest back to the climbers' trail junction and PCT. Hike out 3.5 miles to Big Lake trailhead.
routes.itinerary: Day 1 (full approach–descent): 0530–depart Big Lake PCT trailhead; 0630–0730–hike PCT 3.5 miles to climbers' trail junction; 0730–1230–ascend climbers' trail and scree to North Ridge base (4 hours, 3,200 ft gain); 1230–1430–scramble and climb lower ridge terrain (Class 2–3, 1.5 hours, 600 ft); 1430–1545–crux: climb the Nose (75 feet, low Class 5, 1+ hour); 1545–1630–upper ridge scrambling (Class 3–4, 45 min, final 200 ft); 1630–summit (7,794 ft); 1630–1730–descend via rappels (3–4 stations, 1 hour); 1730–1900–scramble/boulder-field descent to climbers' trail junction; 1900–2030–hike out PCT to trailhead (1.5 hours). Round-trip: 9–10 hours.
routes.bail: From the North Ridge approach gully (pre-crux), retreat downslope to the east to regain scree and meadows leading back to the climbers' trail junction. From the crux (Nose), with expert rappel skills, descend via established slings on west side; less experienced parties should not attempt bypass. From the upper ridge, escape downslope to the northeast into gullies and scree leading to meadows and trail. No emergency bivvy shelter is present; daylight completion is essential. In poor weather, turn back at or below timberline.
routes.turnaround: Turn around if you do not summit by 1430 to maintain safe daylight descent margin. Do not continue if weather deteriorates to lightning risk, high winds, or dense cloud cover. Descend at the first sign of afternoon thunderstorm development. If multiple parties are on the route above or below, consider delaying your climb until congestion clears.
routes.bestSeason: July–August (best weather); June and September offer longer daylight but possible snow patches. Afternoon thunderstorms are common in summer; summit early and descend by mid-afternoon.
watchOut: Rockfall hazard from party above and below—helmet essential
Loose, crumbly volcanic rock with poor protection; anchors scarce and suspect
Exposed climbing with runout/suspect sections—leader must not fall
High altitude exposure on Class 4–5 terrain with little protection
Weather deterioration to lightning/thunderstorm in afternoon—climb early and descend by mid-afternoon
Loose rock and falling debris during/after rappel descent—move quickly with caution
knownHazards: Loose and crumbly volcanic rock throughout, rated as some of the poorest-quality rock in the Oregon Cascades
Constant rockfall hazard, especially with multiple parties on route or when pulling ropes during rappels
Poor anchor placement and scarce protection options; careful inspection required
High Alpine Grade V commitment with serious exposure on Class 4–5 sections and minimal protection
Afternoon thunderstorms common in summer; routes exposed and unprotected from lightning
Approach gully and scree slopes can harbor loose rock triggered by parties above
gear: 1 set of cams (sizes 0.5–2.5 most useful, full rack to size 3)
Crampons and ice axe if climbing before mid-June
whatToBring: Helmet and personal climbing kit (as above)
Food and water for 10–12-hour day (2–3 liters water, energy bars/snacks)
First aid kit with extra padding for rockfall injuries
Headlamp (in case of slow descent into twilight)
Rain gear and emergency bivy sack
Satellite messenger or personal locator beacon (cell service unreliable)
routes.detailedRack: Light trad rack required. One set of cams (0.5–2.5 doubled, up to size 3) and one set of nuts for protection; small cams most useful. Quickdraws/carabiners (10–12). Slings and anchors critical for anchor building and rappel stations; double-check all anchors. 60-meter rope necessary for descent rappels. Most placements will be 'suspect' given rock quality.
routes.proNeeds: Protection is limited by poor rock quality; expect marginal placements at best. Plan to clip natural features (slings, trees). Bring redundant slings for anchor backup. Crux Nose section may have fixed slings from prior ascents; do not rely solely on these. Establish solid anchors at belays; double-check all rappel anchors.
proTips: Start very early to maximize daylight for descent
Wear a helmet at all times; rockfall from above is constant and serious
Scout the crux Nose pitch from the approach before committing
Move efficiently through rappel stations on descent; lingering increases objective hazard
Climb midweek or shoulder season (early June, late Aug) to avoid crowds and reduce rockfall hazard
If lichen or moss is present, expect reduced friction; focus on larger features and cracks
Have a clear escape plan in case you must descend quickly
routes.fa: North ridge variation first ascended August 26, 1923 by Armin Furrer, Leo Harryman, Ervin McNeal, Phil Philbrook, Ron Sellers, Wilbur Watkins; original first-ascent route no longer exists due to rockfall. North Ridge is now the standard route to the summit.
routes.comms: Cell service unreliable in Willamette National Forest backcountry; carry satellite communicator or PLB for emergencies.

pitchDetail.notes: Scramble and hiking from climbers' trail through scree and loose boulder fields to North Ridge base (~600–800 ft elevation gain, 1–1.5 hours). Terrain steepens as elevation increases; some Class 3 scrambling on loose terrain. Well-marked by cairns and established trail.

Follow north ridge proper upward via scrambling and light climbing, staying on or slightly left of crest. Loose rock and scree interspersed with small Class 4 steps. Views improve; exposure increases. ~600 ft elevation gain, 1–1.5 hours.

75-foot technical crux. Scramble right 15 feet to reach left-leaning chimney (30 feet). Exit chimney and climb left-angling face (30 feet, Class 5.2–5.3). Small cams useful; protection marginal. Hardest climbing on route; leader must not fall.

Class 3–4 scrambling and low Class 5 steps along ridge crest. Series of gendarmes; exposure on both sides. Final 100 feet of summit plug = easy Class 5 scrambling. ~200 ft vertical, 30–45 min to summit.

Three to four rappel stations (40–50 ft each) descend steepest Class 4–5 terrain. Carefully retrieve ropes (rockfall hazard). Scramble and boulder-hop down scree gully to easier terrain, then hike trail out.

timing.recommendedStart: 0530–0600 (very early to maximize daylight for descent)

routes.overview: A classic alpine rock and snow route ascending the northwest-facing couloir system on Crooked Thumb Peak's north side, combining steep snow climbing with exposed rock pitches.
routes.beta: The primary established route to Crooked Thumb Peak involves ascending the Crooked Thumb Glacier from base camp and accessing the obvious northwest couloir. The route features continuous 40-45 degree snow climbing with simul-climbing using snow flukes for protection, followed by technical rock climbing across loose Class 4-5 ledges and exposed traverses to reach the summit ridge.
routes.fa: Fred Beckey and Helmy Beckey, 1940
routes.rappels: 6 rappels to 30m; slings established at multiple anchor points
routes.approach: From Hannegan Trailhead (elevation 3,100 ft at end of Ruth Creek Road), hike and scramble to Hannegan Pass, then continue via Boundary Camp, Copper Lake Camp, and Graybeal Camp. Cross Chilliwack River via cablecar, then traverse to Whatcom Pass (approximately 16 miles, 2,740 feet elevation gain from Hannegan area). From Whatcom Pass, rope up on Whatcom Glacier and cross to Perfect Pass, then ascend firm snow and cross through Challenger Glacier to reach West Challenger Notch. Descend to Crooked Thumb Glacier moraine for base camp (approximately 8-11 hours from Whatcom Pass camp, 3,700 feet elevation gain). From camp, boot-scree up Crooked Thumb Glacier to the snow saddle north of the peak.
routes.descent: Rappel down the north ridge via established slings and anchors; descend loose Class 4-5 ledges to the couloir, then downclimb and rappel the snow couloir to glacier level. Approximately 6-7 hours to return to camp.
routes.descentText: Descend via rappel down the north ridge system, using established anchor slings. Traverse across exposed Class 4-5 ledges on the north side, then downclimb or rappel (as needed) the northwest couloir. Multiple rap anchors are established along the descent route. Continue boot-scree down Crooked Thumb Glacier back to camp. The descent is significantly slower than ascent due to rappelling and routefinding; budget 6-7 hours including a possible emergency bivouac if summit is reached late in the day.
routes.itinerary: Day 1-2: Hike Hannegan Pass Trail to Hannegan Pass area (approximately 12 miles, 4,000 feet elevation gain from Hannegan Trailhead). Day 2-3: Traverse Whatcom Glacier and Challenger Glacier to West Challenger Notch, then descend to Crooked Thumb Glacier moraine camp (8-11 hours, 3,700 feet gain). Day 4: Alpine start at dawn; ascend northwest couloir to summit and descend (10 hours round-trip from camp). Day 5: Descend to Whatcom Pass area, begin return journey. Day 6-7: Exit via Hannegan Pass Trail.
routes.bail: If weather deteriorates or snow conditions prove unsafe during the couloir ascent, retreat to camp on Crooked Thumb Glacier. If crevassing is severe on approach glaciers (Whatcom or Challenger), retreat down-glacier and return via the same approach route. If the north ridge traverse proves too hazardous, descend back to the couloir.
routes.turnaround: Turn around by mid-afternoon (2-3 PM) from the summit if daylight is fading or weather deteriorates. Earlier turnaround times are justified if any team member is fatigued or if snow conditions become unstable.
routes.bestSeason: Mid-July through August provides the most stable climbing conditions, with better snow consolidation and reduced avalanche danger compared to early season. Late June attempts may encounter poor snow coverage and excessive crevassing. Early September is still feasible but weather becomes increasingly unstable. Late summer trips (late August onward) risk encountering thin snowpack and loose scree conditions.
routes.comms: Cell coverage is extremely limited in the North Cascades backcountry. Plan satellite communication via inReach or SPOT devices. Radio communication within the park may be available through NPS rangers.
knownHazards: The north ridge traverse features extremely loose Class 4-5 rock on sandy ledges with poor protection; many climbers report this as the most unpleasant section of the route.
Snow in the couloir can range from firm névé to unconsolidated posthole-prone snow depending on seasonal conditions; early-season attempts may encounter avalanche hazard.
Crevasse danger is significant on both Whatcom Glacier and Challenger Glacier approaches; rope teams are mandatory and crevasse rescue skills are essential.
The peak's gneiss rock is often loose and fractured; holds can break without warning, and rockfall from above is a concern on exposed sections.
Late-day summit attempts risk bivouacking or descending in darkness, which dramatically increases error rates and hazard exposure.
The approach involves significant glacier travel over steep terrain with high altitude exposure; sudden weather changes can trap parties above base camp.
watchOut: Do not underestimate the loose rock on the north ridge; it is a serious objective hazard that has caused many parties to rate this section as the crux.
Simul-climbing in the couloir requires solid snow sense and proper snow anchor placement; weak anchors can result in a team fall.
Crevasse rescue is not optional on this peak; ensure all team members are trained in crevasse rescue before attempting.
The descent takes 6-7 hours and is complex; allow ample daylight and do not summit late.
Snowpack coverage varies dramatically by year; low-snow years can make glacier travel significantly more hazardous and may make the route impassable.
proTips: Simul-climb the couloir with four snow flukes for running belays rather than stopping for each belay; this saves time and reduces exposure to falls.
Bring two 6.9mm ropes for the descent; establish at least one redundant anchor at each rappel station and test it before committing weight.
Start from camp before dawn to maximize daylight and reduce the risk of late-day descent complications.
Carry light, efficient alpine gear; every ounce matters in the 10+ hours of climbing and descent.
Scout the north ridge traverse in good light to identify the safest passage across loose ledges and pinnacles.
If the couloir is snow-free or mostly bare rock, reassess the entire route; loose rock climbing without snow protection is significantly more hazardous.
routes.proNeeds: Solid rock climbing skills up to Class 5 lead climbing with loose rock, proper snow climbing technique with ice axe and crampons, and advanced glacier travel including crevasse rescue. All team members must be comfortable with exposed scrambling on poor rock and simul-climbing in moderately steep snow.
routes.detailedRack: Two 6.9mm dynamic ropes (50m each, for simul-climbing and rappelling). Four to six snow flukes in various sizes for running belays and anchors. A small trad rack: 6 cams (0.3-0.75 inches) and 4-6 nuts for occasional belay anchors on rock. Multiple rap slings (120cm) and cordage for creating additional anchors. Ice screws may be useful if the couloir is heavily iced. A single ice tool per person for anchor placement.
whatToBring: High-calorie food (2,000+ calories for the climb day)
First aid kit with blister treatment and pain relief

pitchDetail.notes: Initial boot-scree ascent up Crooked Thumb Glacier to snow saddle north of peak; transition to roped simul-climbing with snow flukes as slope steepens.
Continuation of northwest couloir; simul-climb with running snow fluke belays; snow quality varies from firm névé to posthole-prone slush depending on conditions.
Exit from couloir to north ridge; transition zone with mixed snow and rock; unpleasant scrambling on exposed, sandy ledges with poor holds.
Traverse north ridge around series of pinnacles on loose Class 4-5 ledges; exposed section with poor rock quality; this is the crux pitch and most dangerous section of the route.
After navigating the ridge traverse, scramble across boulders and semi-loose terrain; class 2-3 scrambling becomes easier as climbers approach summit fin.
Final push to summit; solid Class 4-5 rock ridge on the summit fin; secure holds and better rock quality than lower sections; approximately 100 feet to true summit.

road.name: Ruth Creek Road to Hannegan Trailhead
road.status: Seasonal; may be closed or snow-covered in winter.
road.seasonalGate: Road is typically snow-free and accessible from July through October; check with Glacier Ranger Station for current conditions.
road.driveNote: From the Glacier, WA area, drive up Ruth Creek Road to Hannegan Trailhead (approximately 15 miles from the town of Concrete or 25 miles from Bellingham). The road is rough in places and high-clearance vehicles are recommended. There is a day-use parking area and primitive campsites at the trailhead.

timing.recommendedStart: Depart camp before dawn (4:00-5:00 AM) to maximize daylight for climbing and especially for the complex descent.

access.landManager: National Park Service (North Cascades National Park); Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest (Hannegan Trailhead approach)
access.fees: No entry or parking fees within North Cascades National Park; $5 Northwest Forest Pass for parking at Hannegan Trailhead (required).
access.permit: Backcountry permit required for all overnight stays in North Cascades National Park Complex (includes North Cascades NP, Ross Lake NRA, and Lake Chelan NRA). Permits cost $10 per person plus $6 non-refundable reservation fee. Permits must be obtained in advance at the Wilderness Information Center in Marblemount or through the NPS reservation system.
access.passRequired: Washington State Pass (Discover Pass or Northwest Forest Pass) required for vehicle parking at Hannegan Trailhead.
access.closures: North Cascades National Park may restrict access during avalanche control operations or severe weather. The Hannegan Pass Trail may be closed seasonally due to snow; verify current conditions with the Wilderness Information Center before departing.

climate.forecastZone: National Weather Service Forecast Zone WAZ567 (West Slopes North Cascades)
climate.typical: Highly variable alpine climate with frequent storms and rapidly changing conditions. Average temperatures at elevation (8,100 ft) range from 30-40°F in summer.
climate.spring: April-May: Highly unstable with frequent storms, poor visibility, and significant avalanche danger from warming. Not recommended for this technical route.
climate.summer: June-August: Best climbing window. July-August typically offers the most stable conditions with extended daylight and better snow consolidation. June may have excessive crevassing and poor snow coverage.
climate.fall: September-October: Increasingly unstable weather patterns; storms become more frequent and avalanche danger increases. Climbing window closes rapidly.
climate.winter: November-April: Buried under heavy snow with extreme avalanche danger and poor visibility. Not feasible for alpinists.

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Whatcom County Sheriff's Office (non-emergency): 360-676-6911; Emergency 911 dispatch: 360-778-8900
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades National Park Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount: (360) 854-7245 (standard), (360) 873-4590 (emergency)
emergency.nearestHospital: PeaceHealth St. Joseph Medical Center, Bellingham, WA (2901 Squalicum Parkway, Bellingham, WA 98225, phone (360) 734-5400); helicopter evacuation capability available via AirLift Northwest.
emergency.notes: Cell service is nonexistent in the backcountry; satellite communicator (inReach/SPOT) is mandatory. Rescue response from North Cascades typically involves helicopter evacuation to Bellingham or Sedro-Woolley. Helicopter rescue is extremely expensive; ensure proper insurance and rescue coverage before attempting this peak.

routes.corrections: Elevation discrepancy: given peak elevation is 8,109 feet, but authoritative sources (Wikipedia, PeakBagger, peakery.com) consistently list 8,129 feet (2,478 m). The 8,129 ft elevation is used throughout this route description as it has strong independent corroboration.

routes.overview: A shorter, more direct alpine scramble and climbing route ascending the north ridge and east face directly from the glacier, offering Class 3-4 rock climbing with less overall exposure than the west couloir.
routes.beta: This route was established as a first ascent on July 31, 1963, by members of a mountaineering party exploring the Northern Pickets. The route ascends directly up the east face from the Crooked Thumb Glacier, staying on lower-angle rock and avoiding the higher technical crux of the northwest couloir. The route transitions from easy glacier climbing to Class 3-4 scrambling on the east-facing side of the peak.
routes.fa: Jackson, Jensen, Marts, and Schmechel, July 31, 1963
routes.approach: Follow the same approach as the West Couloir route to reach Crooked Thumb Glacier moraine camp (40 hours from Hannegan Trailhead, 8+ miles, 5,200 feet elevation gain). From camp, boot-scree up Crooked Thumb Glacier to the eastern base of the peak (approximately 1-2 hours).
routes.descent: Rappel the east face using established anchors or cairn-marked stations. Downclimb Class 3-4 terrain to the glacier. Total descent time approximately 3-4 hours.
routes.descentText: Rappel the east-facing slope using 2-3 rappel stations (anchors may be marked with cairns or slings from previous ascents). Downclimb Class 3-4 scrambling terrain on the east face, regaining the glacier at the base. Return to camp via boot-scree down Crooked Thumb Glacier. The descent is significantly faster than the west couloir route and involves less complex routefinding.
routes.itinerary: Day 1-2: Hike Hannegan Pass Trail to Hannegan Pass area. Day 2-3: Traverse Whatcom and Challenger Glaciers to Crooked Thumb Glacier moraine camp. Day 4: Early alpine start (5:00-6:00 AM); ascend east face to summit and descend (7-8 hours round-trip). Day 5: Return to Whatcom Pass area. Day 6-7: Exit via Hannegan Pass Trail.
routes.bail: If the east face proves too loose or rockfall hazard is high, retreat back to the glacier. This route offers more straightforward retreat options than the west couloir due to less exposure.
routes.turnaround: Turn around by mid-afternoon if weather deteriorates or team members are fatigued. The direct nature of this route makes retreat simpler than the west couloir.
routes.bestSeason: Mid-July through August provides the most stable conditions. This route may be feasible earlier in the season (late June) than the west couloir due to lower commitment and shorter duration.
routes.comms: Cell coverage is nonexistent in the backcountry; satellite communication via inReach or SPOT is mandatory.
objHaz: loose Class 3-4 rock on east face
wet snow and postholing on glacier
knownHazards: The east face rock can be loose and fractured; exercise caution and wear a helmet due to rockfall hazard.
Crevasse danger remains significant on the Crooked Thumb Glacier approach; proper rope teams and crevasse rescue training are essential.
Late-season snow conditions may expose additional loose rock and increase rockfall hazard.
The route is more direct than the west couloir but still requires solid alpine scrambling skills and careful route-finding to identify the safest path through the loose rock.
watchOut: This route involves less technical climbing than the west couloir but requires helmet use due to significant rockfall hazard.
Do not assume the route is simpler because it is lower grade; loose rock presents serious hazards and can injure or kill climbers.
Ensure proper glacier approach and crevasse rescue skills; the easier climbing does not eliminate glacier hazards.
Late-day summit attempts are more feasible on this route than the west couloir due to shorter duration, but still avoid descending in poor visibility.
proTips: This route is faster and less exposed than the west couloir, making it preferable for teams with less rock climbing experience.
Scout the ascent route carefully to identify the path of least resistance through the loose rock terrain.
Wear a quality climbing helmet; rockfall is a real hazard on this route.
Leave a fixed rope or markers for the descent if time permits; the east face can be tricky to navigate during descent.
routes.proNeeds: Solid Class 4 scrambling ability on loose rock, glacier travel and crevasse rescue skills, and experience with alpine routefinding. Less technical rock climbing is required than the west couloir, but alpine rock scrambling skills are still essential.
gear: Climbing helmet (essential due to rockfall hazard)
Rock protection (small rack of cams and nuts for occasional anchor backup)
routes.detailedRack: Single 50-60m dynamic rope (9mm) for simul-climbing and rappelling. Small rock rack: 4-6 cams (0.4-0.6 inches) and 3-4 nuts for occasional anchor backup. Multiple rap slings and cordage for creating rappel anchors. Ice axe for snow/scree work and snow anchor placement if needed.
whatToBring: High-calorie food (1,500-2,000 calories for climb day)
First aid kit with blister treatment

pitchDetail.notes: Boot-scree ascent from glacier to base of east face; mix of snow patches and loose scree; approximately 1-2 hours.
Lower section of east face; moderately easy scrambling on loosely consolidated gneiss; route-finding is required to identify the safest path.
Mid-section of east face; more exposed scrambling with increased rockfall hazard; use helmet and move efficiently to minimize exposure time.
Upper section approaching summit; angle decreases as climbers gain elevation; transition to easier scrambling and rocky terrain.

road.name: Ruth Creek Road to Hannegan Trailhead

blurb:
Crooked Thumb Peak is a remote 8,129-foot gneiss alpine peak in the Picket Range of North Cascades National Park, located 0.5 miles south of Mount Challenger. Fred Beckey and Helmy Beckey made the first ascent in 1940. The peak offers exciting alpine rock and snow climbing with routes combining Class 4-5 scrambling, steep snow couloirs, and technical exposure over hanging glaciers, making it one of the more committing peaks in the extremely remote Northern Pickets.</blurb>

hierarchyNote: Elevation discrepancy detected: database lists 7582 ft, but all authoritative sources (SummitPost, Mountain Project, Peakbagger, USGS) consistently document 6,640 feet (2,025 m). South Twin is the higher peak at 7,004+ feet. This is a critical error requiring correction. Hierarchy/location is accurate (Twin Sisters Range, Mount Baker Wilderness, Whatcom County, 9 miles SW of Mount Baker).
blurb: North Twin Sister is a 6,640-foot peak in Washington's Twin Sisters Range, located just southwest of Mount Baker in the North Cascades. The range is composed of dunite (ultramafic olivine rock), creating some of the grippiest scrambling terrain in Washington State with distinctive orange-yellow coloring. First ascended via the South Ridge in 1930, North Twin has become a popular alpine scrambling destination renowned for its exposed ridge traverses and exceptional rock quality.

routes.overview: The most popular route on North Twin Sister, featuring a narrow ridge with sustained class 3-4 scrambling on grippy olivine rock. The route combines technical scrambling with significant exposure and excellent handholds.
routes.bestSeason: July through September offers the most stable weather and minimal snow coverage. Early season (May-June) allows for glissading descents on north-facing snowfields but requires ice axe and crampons. Ice axe may be needed before August. Avoid October onward due to increasing snow and weather instability.
routes.approach: Park near the gate at Clearwater Creek bridge area. Bike approximately 5-6 miles up Forest Road 38 (logging road) with 2,000-2,500 feet of elevation gain to reach Dailey Prairie at approximately 3,500-4,000 feet. At roughly 4,000-4,100 feet, transition from logging roads to traditional forest trail with switchbacks through dense forest. Look for trail markers (cairn, dead tree, dirt mound) to locate the start of the old road spur through clearcut area. Continue upward following the old trail as it transitions to open alpine. The ridge base is reached at approximately 4,600-5,000 feet after bushwhacking and scrambling through transitional terrain.
routes.descent: Most parties downclimb the West Ridge following the ascent route, remaining well under class 3 difficulty with good holds throughout. Alternative: In early season (May-June) when snow is present, a steep glissade descent is possible on the long, steep snow field on the north-facing slope, requiring careful route-finding to intersect the ridge crest at approximately 4,600 feet. Early season descents require ice axe and crampons; late season (August+) requires downclimbing the ridge.
routes.descentText: The West Ridge downclimb is straightforward for most of the descent, retracing your ascent route with handholds readily available on the grippy olivine rock. The ridge remains class 3 terrain when staying on the proper line, though off-route scrambling can push difficulty to class 4 or 5. If descending snowfields, maintain control and be prepared for self-arrest. The forest transition back to logging roads follows the same approach route in reverse.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Bike approach from gate to Dailey Prairie trailhead (1.5-2 hours), hike forest trail to treeline (1.5-2 hours), scramble West Ridge to summit (2-3 hours). Total trailhead to summit: 6-7 hours. Return via downclimb (3-4 hours) or glissade descent (early season, shorter). Total car-to-car: 10-14 hours depending on fitness and conditions.
routes.bail: Bailout from the lower ridge (below 5,500 feet) is straightforward—simply descend the forest trail back to Dailey Prairie and return via logging road. From higher on the ridge (above 6,000 feet), staying on the ridge to the summit is often easier than attempting to traverse off; however, the northeast ridge offers an emergency descent option in some seasons. Avoid traversing into the south/southeast aspects during afternoon as rockfall hazard increases on steeper slopes facing away from the ridge crest.
routes.turnaround: Maintain a firm turnaround time of 2-3 PM at the summit to ensure adequate daylight for descent and navigation through forest terrain before dark. Weather can deteriorate rapidly in afternoons; if conditions are marginal by midday, consider turning back from lower on the ridge. Do not attempt the descent in poor visibility due to route-finding complexity through the forest transition zone.
routes.beta: The West Ridge is sustained class 3 scrambling with numerous exposed sections on a narrow crest; stay alert for route-finding decisions where the crest is obstructed by outcrops. The rule of thumb is to stay left (west) on the lower ridge to maintain easier class 3 terrain; drifting right leads to unnecessary class 4-5 scrambling. Navigation improves as you gain elevation and the ridge becomes more defined. The grippy olivine rock provides excellent friction and positive holds—gardening gloves are recommended for the ultra-grippy surface. Most parties do not rope up, though some choose to set a hand line on the most exposed sections. Helmets are essential due to rockfall hazard and exposure. Route-finding is critical: follow cairns and established trails; at around 6,300 feet, cairned routes may appear to terminate, but an alternate route through a notch ('Katie Gap') bypasses cliff sections ahead.
proTips: Bike the entire approach both directions—the return on the gated logging road is mostly downhill and significantly faster than hiking, cutting total time by 2-3 hours.
The ultra-grippy olivine rock provides excellent handholds even when damp; bring gardening gloves or tape to protect your hands, as the sandpaper-like texture will shred skin quickly.
Route-finding is critical: stay left on the lower ridge and watch for cairn markers. If you hit vertical sections or false summits, backtrack and reassess your line.

Early season (May-June) allows glissading descents but requires ice axe, crampons, and good snow judgment. Later season (July-September) offers stable conditions but requires downclimbing the entire ridge.
Start early (5-6 AM trailhead) to allow ample time for return in daylight; the forest section can be disorienting in poor light.
Bring extra water; there is no reliable water source on the ridge beyond the approach forest.
watchOut: Narrow ridge with significant exposure: falls from higher sections are potentially fatal. Helmets mandatory; consider a rope and harness for less experienced scramblers.
Rockfall hazard on southeast-facing slopes and in the summit area; wear helmets and be alert to other parties above.
Loose rock on the ridge; test holds before committing your full weight, especially on the steeper sections.
Early season (May-June) north-facing snowfields present avalanche hazard. Assess snow stability and be prepared for rapid weather changes.
Route-finding complexity: multiple false summits and obscured crest sections can lead to time loss or off-route scrambling into exposed class 4-5 terrain.
knownHazards: Exposure on narrow ridge crest: several hundred feet of vertical drop-off on both flanks in the upper sections.
Loose rock and rockfall: the olivine is generally solid, but weathered sections and recent rockfall can create unstable blocks.
Early season snow and ice: north-facing slopes retain snow into late June; crevasses are rare but the snow surface can be icy and slick.
Avalanche risk on approach snowfields in winter and spring; avoid in high-risk conditions.
Weather deterioration: afternoon thunderstorms common in summer; rapid wind and visibility change on exposed ridge.
gear: Scrambling boots with good ankle support
Lightweight harness and carabiners (optional; for confident scramblers a rope may be carried for safety)
Minimal technical rock rack (optional; most parties do not carry climbing gear)
routes.detailedRack: No technical climbing rack required for the standard West Ridge scramble. However, experienced alpinists may carry a lightweight rack (small wired nuts and cams, sizes 0.5-2 inches) and a single 30-50m dynamic rope as emergency protection. Most parties successfully complete the route with none of this gear. If bringing a rope, set a hand line on the most exposed sections (approximately 100 feet of steep ridge) to protect less experienced members.
routes.proNeeds: Light alpine protection optional. The route is fundamentally a scramble with no vertical rock climbing. If roping: a 30-meter rope or longer, rock shoes optional (most use scrambling boots), and basic belay/anchor knowledge. The rock quality is generally good for natural anchors (horns, flakes), but building bomber anchors requires care due to the friable olivine.
whatToBring: Mountain bike or sturdy hybrid bike (highly recommended for approach)
Hat and gloves (even in summer, temperature drops with altitude)
Map and compass (topo map of Whatcom County area)
GPS device or phone with offline maps
First aid kit including blister treatment and pain relievers

climate.forecastZone: North Cascades marine-influenced climate; Puget Sound forecast zone applies to marine conditions, with mountain forecasts available via Mountain-Forecast.com and Mountain Weather Forecast (NOAA).
climate.typical: Maritime climate with moderate temperatures and high precipitation. July-August average highs near 60°F (15°C) at elevation with frequent afternoon thunderstorms. Snow-free on exposed ridges July-September, but afternoon weather deterioration is common.
climate.spring: May-June: Highly variable; significant snow coverage on north-facing slopes (snow persists on north ridge until late June average). Cold nights near freezing, afternoons 40-50°F. Ice axe and crampons needed. High avalanche hazard early season.
climate.summer: July-September: Most stable season with dry spells. July-August daytime temperatures 50-60°F at ridge elevation, dropping to 30-40°F at night. September shows increasing cloud cover and chance of early snow at higher elevations. Afternoon thunderstorms increase in frequency mid-summer.
climate.fall: October-November: Rapid snow-in period; active avalanche conditions and poor visibility common. Not recommended for scrambling.
climate.winter: December-March: Heavy snow coverage, avalanche hazard, and whiteout conditions. Technical alpine climbing only; approach logging roads may be impassable.

routes.comms: Cell phone coverage is very limited at elevation and within the Twin Sisters Range due to dense forest cover on approach. In Bellingham, Verizon covers 90.4% of Whatcom County and AT&T covers 86.9%, but coverage deteriorates rapidly in forested areas and at high elevation. No reliable cell service on the ridge itself. Recommend carrying a satellite messenger (Garmin InReach or SPOT device) or scheduling regular check-in calls before entering the backcountry.

routes.overview: The second most popular route on North Twin Sister, a moderate class 2-3 scramble on the northeast aspect. This route is frequently used as an alternate descent from the West Ridge and offers a more mellow alpine experience compared to the exposed main route.
routes.bestSeason: Late July through September when snow coverage on the north-facing slope is minimal. The north ridge is snow-covered on average years until late in the season; early season (May-June) requires ice axe and crampons. August-September provides the most stable and snow-free conditions.
routes.approach: Same as West Ridge approach: bike or hike Forest Road 38 approximately 5-6 miles to Dailey Prairie, then traverse forest and open terrain to reach the ridge base at approximately 4,600-5,000 feet. The North Ridge base is accessed by contouring around the eastern aspects of the mountain rather than directly ascending the West Ridge.
routes.descent: The North Ridge is a popular descent route from the West Ridge. Most parties ascending via the exposed West Ridge will descend the more moderate North Ridge on the return journey, glissading on snow-covered sections early in the season (May-June) or downclimbing the pleasant class 3 terrain in mid-to-late season. The ridge offers a more mellow aesthetic than the West Ridge and is easier to navigate in reverse.
routes.descentText: Descending the North Ridge involves downclimbing relatively straightforward class 2-3 scrambling terrain with good holds on the grippy olivine. The ridge is less narrow and exposed than the West Ridge, making for a more comfortable descent. In early season when snow persists on the north slope, controlled glissading is possible down the steep snow field on the north face, intersecting the ridge at approximately 4,600 feet. Later season descents follow the rock ridge crest with standard downclimbing techniques.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Bike or hike approach from gate to Dailey Prairie (2 hours), forest and alpine approach to ridge base (2 hours), ascend North Ridge to summit (3-4 hours), descend North Ridge (2-3 hours). Total car-to-car: 9-12 hours depending on fitness and conditions.
routes.bail: Bailout from the North Ridge is straightforward at any elevation; simply descend the ridge back toward the base and retrace your approach route. The terrain is not steep and exposure is minimal, allowing for easy retreat if weather deteriorates or party members tire.
routes.turnaround: Maintain a firm turnaround time of 2:00-2:30 PM at the summit to ensure adequate daylight for descent and forest navigation before dark. The North Ridge descent can be slower than the West Ridge if snow requires careful glissading, so start descent early.
routes.beta: The North Ridge is a more straightforward alpine scramble than the West Ridge, with less exposure and more moderate terrain. Class 2-3 scrambling throughout with handholds generally readily available. Route-finding is simpler; stay on the ridge crest and follow the natural line of ascent. The route is typically snow-covered in early season (May-June), requiring careful snow travel and assessment of avalanche hazard on the steep north-facing slope. By late July, most snow has melted and the ridge is dry rock scrambling. The rock quality is excellent throughout with positive holds on the grippy olivine. Most parties do not rope up, but early-season parties should carry ice axe and crampons and be prepared for self-arrest.
proTips: The North Ridge is the classic alternate descent from the West Ridge—if descending from the West Ridge, traversing around the mountain to the North Ridge often saves time and provides a more enjoyable glissade or downclimb.
In early season, the north-facing slope may hold snow through June; if glissading, find the steepest safe line and use your ice axe for self-arrest practice.
Later in the season (August-September), the ridge dries out completely and becomes pleasant, straightforward class 3 terrain.
Route-finding is easier on the North Ridge than the West Ridge; if you get lost, simply heading downward toward lower elevations will intersect the approach forest trail.
watchOut: Early season (May-June) avalanche hazard on the steep north-facing snowfields; assess conditions carefully and avoid if danger is high.
Ice-covered sections on the ridge can be slick; crampons improve security but downclimbing care is essential.
Weather deterioration on the exposed upper ridge, though less dramatic than on the West Ridge.
Loose rock hazard, though generally lower than on the West Ridge.
knownHazards: Snow and avalanche hazard on early-season north-facing slope: the ridge remains snow-covered into late June on average years, with avalanche risk on slopes steeper than 30 degrees.
Ice-slicked rock sections in spring and early summer where meltwater runs across the ridge.
Less dramatic exposure than the West Ridge, but sections still have several hundred feet of drop-off.
Route-finding less clear than the West Ridge, but terrain is more forgiving if off-route.
gear: Climbing helmet (recommended, though hazard is lower than West Ridge)
Scrambling boots with good ankle support
Ice axe (mandatory for early-mid season, June-July)
routes.detailedRack: No technical climbing rack needed. Early-season parties should carry ice axe and crampons for snow travel. No ropes typically required, though a lightweight rope can be useful for belaying less experienced members through steep snow sections.
routes.proNeeds: Light alpine protection. The route is fundamentally a scramble and snow-scramble; no rock climbing. Early-season: ice axe, crampons, and knowledge of self-arrest techniques are essential. A rope can be carried for belaying through exposed snow sections.
whatToBring: Hat and gloves (mandatory in early season, recommended year-round)
GPS device or phone with offline maps
First aid kit including blister treatment and pain relievers

blurb: South Twin Sister (7,004 ft) is the highest peak in the Twin Sisters Range, a distinctive olivine-dunite formation southwest of Mount Baker in Washington's Cascades. The range is geologically unique as the largest exposed olivine body in the Western Hemisphere, offering exceptionally grippy rock and some of the finest scrambling in Washington state. First ascended by J. M. Edson, E. A. Hegg, and P. J. Parris on August 1, 1891, the peak sees fewer ascents than its northern neighbor but attracts alpinists seeking quality scrambles through exposed terrain and glacier approaches.

routes.routeName: South Twin Sister Standard (Dailey Prairie/South Face)
routes.fa: J. M. Edson, E. A. Hegg, P. J. Parris (August 1, 1891)
routes.overview: The standard and most straightforward approach to South Twin Sister via Dailey Prairie and Orsino Creek basin, featuring Class 2-3 scrambling up the south-facing gullies and slopes. This route avoids the technical ridge crest and is the most commonly ascended line, suitable for competent scramblers with alpine experience.
routes.beta: From the Orsino Creek basin, ascend via the lower west ridge gully, traversing onto the south face where gentler gullies provide easier passage than staying on the ridge crest. Multiple route options allow parties to adjust difficulty based on skill level and conditions. The route involves 2-3 hours of scrambling with significant exposure in places but generally manageable for experienced alpinists.
routes.approach: Drive to Middle Fork Road off the Nooksack River near Acme, Washington. Proceed 4.8 miles to a junction leading down to a gated bridge (contact Olivine Corp. at 360-733-3332 for access status). Bike or hike logging roads around Dailey Prairie (6.25 miles). The road system is complex with multiple offshoots; a map is essential. At Orsino Creek (the best water source), leave bikes at the large pipe barrier. Follow a narrow climbers' path that is overgrown but well-marked through dense forest. Cross Orsino Creek and continue to a red talus slope. Ascend through timber and past old cabin ruins to reach the basin below the ridge proper. Total approach: ~11.5 miles, 5,400-6,000 ft elevation gain, 4.6-5.7 hours on foot plus 2.5 hours bike time.
routes.descent: Descend via the upper west ridge gully, reversing the ascent route. Use care on loose terrain; stay to the sides of gullies to minimize rockfall hazard on descent. Glissading is possible early season on long snow slopes. Descend 4-4.5 hours to the bike parking area, then bike back to vehicle. Total descent time: 4-5 hours.
routes.descentText: From the summit, descend south-southeast via the upper west ridge gully, reversing the ascent route. Navigate through exposed scrambling terrain with care, particularly on tired legs. The terrain is loose in places; avoid dislodging rocks onto parties below. When encountering sections with significant exposure, use caution and consider roping if conditions or party composition warrant it. Once on the upper gully system, follow clear fall-line paths downward. In early season (May-June), long sections of consolidated snow allow glissading; stamp steps and control descent speed carefully to avoid uncontrolled slides. Throughout the descent, stay alert for incoming rockfall, particularly from the ridge crest above. Exit the basin onto the forested climbers' path and follow it back down to Orsino Creek (refill water here if needed). Continue down the old road to the bike parking area, then bike back to the vehicle on the logging roads.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Approach via logging roads and foot trail. Depart trailhead at 0500 (5 AM) or earlier. Bike to Orsino Creek (2.5 hours), cache bikes, hike to basin (2 hours). Establish camp in the inter-peak basin or push for summit attempt (2-3 more hours). 14-16 hours total to summit or camp. Day 2 (if camping): Summit push from basin 0600 (2-3 hours climbing, 1 hour summit time). Descend to basin (2 hours). Pack camp and hike/bike out (3-4 hours). 11-15 hours for descent and exit.
routes.bail: From the basin, retreat is straightforward; descend gullies back to the forested climbers' path and follow it down to Orsino Creek, then exit via the logging road system. There are no escape routes from higher on the ridge; commit only when conditions and party fitness are solid. Early retreat from the ridge proper is the safest option if conditions deteriorate.
routes.turnaround: Turn around if summit is not reached by 1200 hours (noon) to ensure safe return to vehicles before dark. On exposed terrain, never proceed into conditions of poor visibility, electrical storms, or significant new snow accumulation. Monitor weather continuously; maritime influence creates rapid weather changes. If rocks or ice are falling, retreat immediately.
routes.bestSeason: May through October. Optimal climbing occurs in July and August when snowpack has consolidated, the talus slope is free of snow, and days are longest (early starts can be made at 0400-0500). June can be excellent but expect lingering snow on talus that requires careful travel. Late May may have significant snow; wait for conditions to improve. September and October offer stable conditions and fewer parties, though weather becomes more variable. Avoid late fall (November) when snow returns and avalanche danger increases.
routes.comms: Cell service is unreliable in the Middle Fork Nooksack River valley and Dailey Prairie area. Expect no coverage below 7,000 ft; satellite communicators (inReach, etc.) are strongly recommended for safety. Mount Baker Ranger District can be reached at (360) 856-5700 for conditions updates or emergencies.
watchOut: Loose rock hazard on descent; a dislodged boulder caused significant thigh bruising on recent ascent
Wet, maritime-influenced snow creates avalanche hazard spring-early summer; assess slope angles above 30°
Exposure on upper ridge sections with long drop-offs to both sides; ropes recommended if unfamiliar with exposure
Route-finding challenges on approach logging roads; carry detailed map or GPS
Bergschrund (if Sisters Glacier approach attempted) may be problematic later in season and could trap climbers in deep crevasses
knownHazards: Loose olivine rock; rock strikes are possible on descent in gullies
Wet avalanche danger from maritime snow influence; avoid mid-morning ascent when wet slab potential peaks
Rockfall from ridge crest onto south face gullies during or immediately after storms
Exposure with 200-300 ft drop-offs on ridge crest; requires scrambling competence and comfort with exposure
Late-season bergschrund (if glacier route taken) can be impassable without proper ice tools or ropes
Route-finding difficulty on complex logging road system in poor visibility
proTips: Bike the logging roads to save 1-2 hours and energy; gear can be cached in a waterproof bag at road's end
Refill water at Orsino Creek on both ascent and descent (2.5L sufficient if you fill there)
Start before dawn (0400-0500 departure) to maximize daylight on exposed terrain and ensure safe return before dark
Avoid the ridge crest on descent; traverse south to gullies for easier downclimbing and to minimize exposure on tired legs
Use trekking poles on the approach to reduce knee strain on the long descent on logging roads; carry them down
The summit register (small PVC tube) exists; sign in for tracking purposes, but expect minimal prior ascent records
Consider a rope for the upper ridge section if unfamiliar with Class 3-4 exposure; most parties climb unroped but should be comfortable with 300+ ft drops
whatToBring: Daypack with rain layer and wind jacket (maritime weather changes rapidly)
2.5-3 liters water capacity or filtration for natural sources (Orsino Creek reliable)
Alpine helmet (mandatory; rockfall risk is real)
Climbing shoes or approach shoes with solid soles (never ascend in full hiking boots)
Rope (60-meter optional, recommended if unfamiliar with exposed scrambling)
Harness, belay device, and sling (if bringing rope)
Compass and detailed topographic map (GPS as backup)
First aid kit (trekking poles become excellent splint supports)
Sunscreen and sun-protective clothing (olivine rock reflects intense sun)
Emergency shelter or bivy sack (in case forced to retreat in darkness)
Toilet paper and waste bag (pack out all waste from wilderness area)
routes.detailedRack: No bolted anchors exist on this scramble route. Bring a light trad rack (small nuts, micro cams) only if you plan to rope on the ridge; most parties climb un-roped. Single 60m dynamic rope with 2-3 slings and a belay device suffice if desired for security on Class 3 sections with exposure.
routes.proNeeds: This route does not require rock climbing protection in the technical sense (no bolted anchors, minimal gear placements). Rope is psychological security for parties unfamiliar with exposure, but competent scramblers climb un-roped. If roping, place anchors on large flakes or horn features; the olivine rock is solid and accepts gear well. Rappelling is rarely necessary; downclimbing is the standard descent method.
gear: Rope and harness (optional if unfamiliar with exposure)

timing.recommendedStart: 0400-0500 hours (4-5 AM) for full daylight on exposed sections and safe return before dark.

climate.typical: Highly variable maritime climate. Early morning often clear; afternoon thunderstorms common (especially June-August). Wind from westerly direction off Pacific. Temperatures range 40-50°F summit, 60°F base (early morning start).
climate.spring: (May) Heavy wet snow, avalanche danger high. Approach roads may still have winter blockages. Best avoided unless experienced in snow travel.
climate.summer: (July-August) Most stable window. Daytime highs 50-55°F summit, sunny mornings. Afternoon clouds and occasional rain showers. Evening fog common. Expect clear summits 60-70% of attempts.
climate.fall: (September-October) Increasingly variable. Early fall (Sept) stable; late fall brings return of snow. Morning frosts and frozen rock early season; afternoon warming. Wind can increase mid-to-late fall.
climate.winter: Not a summer-season climb; winter ascents rare and dangerous due to avalanche terrain and extreme rockfall risk on icy rock. Avoid November-April.

access.landManager: USDA Forest Service (Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest, Mt. Baker District)
access.fees: No entrance fee. Northwest Forest Pass may be required for parking at some Forest Service trailheads. Contact Mt. Baker Ranger District at (360) 856-5700 for current requirements.
access.permit: No climbing permit required. Wilderness area access is free. Some private land (Hampton Family Forests owns the gate area); confirm access status with Olivine Corp. at (360) 733-3332 before trip.
access.passRequired: Potentially, if parking at a designated Forest Service area. Verify with Mt. Baker Ranger District. Private gate (Olivine Corp. operated) may require arrangement.
access.closures: No known seasonal closures. Mount Baker National Forest is open year-round but alpine access is limited by snow and avalanche conditions, effectively closing the peak from November-April. Road washouts can occur; contact ranger district before travel.

road.driveNote: From Acme, WA, take SR-539 east and follow signs to Middle Fork Road. Road quality deteriorates; high-clearance or 4WD vehicle recommended. Approximately 50 minutes from nearest town (Acme). Road can be rough and may require careful navigation. Winter storms can cause washouts.
