routes.corrections: ELEVATION CORRECTION: Database lists elevation as 7,460 ft but authoritative sources (USGS, Peakbagger NAVD 88, Wikipedia, all three primary sources) confirm South Twin Sister elevation as 7,004 feet (2,135 m). Database value is significantly overstated by ~456 ft. Corrected to 7,004 ft based on consensus of reliable sources.

routes.fa: J. M. Edson, E. A. Hegg, P. J. Parris (August 1, 1891)
routes.overview: The classic West Ridge ascent of South Twin Sister, featuring 2+ hours of continuous Class 3-4 scrambling on solid olivine rock. This is the most aesthetically satisfying line in the Twin Sisters, offering exposed ridge traverses, technical foot placements, and exceptional rock quality. Best for climbers with scrambling experience and comfort with significant exposure.
routes.beta: The West Ridge proper stays near or on the ridge crest, offering more exposure and technical scrambling than the southern gully variation. Multiple 'crux' sections require deliberate foot and handholds. The olivine rock (dunite composition) is exceptionally grippy, feeling like 50-grit sandpaper even on steep sections. Two documented 5th-class spots exist on the crest, requiring rope or downclimbing for safer passage. Most parties climb un-roped by downclimbing exposed sections and using hands on steep scrambles.
routes.approach: Same as South Twin Standard route: Drive to gated bridge on Middle Fork Road (contact Olivine Corp. 360-733-3332). Bike logging roads 6.25 miles to Orsino Creek (refill water here). Hike overgrown climbers' path through forest to red talus slope and basin below ridge. Ascend ridge from the basin via the west ridge proper (starting lower is easier). Total approach: ~11-12 miles, ~5,400 ft elevation gain, 5.5-6 hours total approach time (includes 2.5 hour bike portion).
routes.descent: Reverse the ridge descent via downclimbing the Class 3-4 sections. Exercise extreme caution; downclimbing exposed terrain is more dangerous than climbing up. Use careful foot placement and full hand contact. Some parties employ a short rappel (one to two rope lengths) from natural anchors on the ridge crest to bypass the steepest sections, but most downclimb. Once on the upper ridge, descend to the high notch and continue into the south-facing gullies for easier slopes back to the basin. Total descent: 4-5 hours including gully scrambling to the basin.
routes.descentText: Descend the ridge crest or the gullies immediately below (south side). On the technical sections of the ridge, use extreme caution: downclimbing Class 3-4 terrain is riskier than ascending. Face into the rock, use full hand contact, and avoid rushing. Place feet deliberately and test handholds before committing weight. If uncomfortable with exposure on the crest, traverse immediately onto the south-facing terrain where easier scrambling is found. Stay on the right (north) side of gullies to minimize rockfall exposure. Move one party member at a time through the most exposed sections to reduce hazard if someone slips. In early season, snow can make downclimbing treacherous; coil rope on snow slopes and glissade if conditions permit. Once the terrain eases below 6,000 ft, move to the gullies and descend directly to the basin via the easiest fall-line route.
routes.itinerary: Predawn start essential for this route. Depart vehicle at 0430 (4:30 AM). Bike and hike approach (5.5-6 hours including water refill at Orsino Creek). Arrive basin 1030-1100 (10:30-11:00 AM). Ridge scramble 1100-1300 (2 hours). Summit 1300-1320 (20 minutes). Begin descent 1320 (1:20 PM). Downclimb ridge and gullies 1320-1700 (3.5 hours). Bike/hike exit 1700-1900 (2 hours). Vehicle by 1900-2000 (7-8 PM). Total time: 15-18 hours.
routes.bail: From the ridge proper, immediate bail is complex. Downclimbing the crest is mandatory to reach gullies on the south. If weather deteriorates or party member becomes incapacitated, downclimb to the high notch (400 ft below summit) and traverse south immediately into gullies where easier scrambling and more bail-out options exist. There are no escape routes from the ridge crest itself; commit only with solid fitness and weather.
routes.turnaround: Mandatory turnaround at 1100-1130 (11:00-11:30 AM) if summit has not been reached. Daylight on exposed terrain is non-negotiable. Weather deterioration is rapid; abandon summit attempt if clouds form or wind picks up to sustained >25 mph. Do not be on the ridge after 1500 (3 PM); darkness on this terrain is dangerous.
routes.bestSeason: June through October. Peak season is July-August for stable weather and longest daylight. June is acceptable if snow has cleared from the talus approach (call ranger district for conditions update). September offers stable conditions. October is marginal; avoid late October when snow returns and avalanche risk increases. This technically demanding route is not recommended in May (excessive snow) or November-April (extreme conditions).
routes.comms: No cell service in the approach area. Satellite communicator (inReach, Garmin, or similar) is essential given the exposed terrain and long scramble time. Mt. Baker Ranger District: (360) 856-5700 for pre-trip conditions check.
watchOut: Exposure is continuous on the ridge crest; 200-300 ft drop-offs to both sides. Scrambling competence and comfort with altitude exposure are mandatory.
Loose rock is a real hazard. Avoid kicking rocks onto parties below. On descent, a dislodged basketball-sized boulder could trigger serious injury to lower party members.
Class 5 sections exist on the ridge crest. Do not assume all scrambling is climbable un-roped. Scout terrain ahead and be prepared to either down-climb (risky) or rappel (requires rope and anchors).
Weather changes rapidly. Afternoon thunderstorms can develop quickly. If electrical activity begins, descend immediately regardless of progress.
Traffic of loose rock increases on descent. Move efficiently and communicate when dislodging rocks. Helmets are mandatory.
Late-season bergschrund hazard if glacier approach is attempted (not standard route but possible option).
knownHazards: Continuous exposure on ridge crest; sustained Class 3-4 scrambling at altitude for 2+ hours is physically demanding
Drop-offs of 200-300 feet on both sides of ridge; one misstep could be fatal for unroped climbers
Olivine rock, while grippy, is prone to loose pieces; rockfall from above or dislodged rock on descent
Afternoon thunderstorms common June-August; electrical storm risk on an exposed ridge
Wet olivine rock in rain is still grippy but requires deliberate movement; ice-covered rock later in season becomes dangerous
Bergschrund on Sisters Glacier (if alternative glacier approach attempted) can be an impassable trap in late season
proTips: This route is best for climbers with scrambling experience on peaks like Middle Teton or North Cascades passes (Class 3-4 comfort is needed).
Start before 0430 (4:30 AM) to ensure maximum daylight on the ridge. Lingering on the crest after 1500 (3 PM) is risky in maritime weather zone.
The olivine rock is incredibly grippy; trust it. Even steep (60°+) sections have excellent friction. Confidence on the rock reduces fatigue and risk.
Consider carrying a 60-meter rope and harness even if you climb un-roped. It adds only 4-5 lbs and provides options for a rappel if party member becomes anxious or incapacitated.
Trekking poles stored in pack for descent only; carrying them on the ridge adds weight and limits hand use on technical sections.
Scout the downclimb route ahead of time while ascending; visibility and route-finding are more difficult when descending, especially on tired legs and in changing light.
A climbing helmet is not optional; this is not a hiking route where helmets are precaution. On a 2+ hour ridge scramble with rockfall potential, helmets save lives.
Use a group of 2-3 for optimal safety; solo climbing this route is not recommended given exposure. Larger groups should move in two subgroups to minimize rockfall exposure.
whatToBring: Climbing shoes or sticky approach shoes (not hiking boots)
Daypack 25-30L with rain jacket and wind layer
60m dynamic climbing rope (optional if familiar with Class 3-4 exposure; suggested for first-timers)
Harness and belay device if bringing rope
Climbing slings x2 and carabiners x4 for anchors or rappel setup
Compass and detailed topographic map of Twin Sisters range
First aid kit with pain management (e.g., ibuprofen)
Sunscreen and sunglasses (olivine rock reflects intense sun)
Toilet paper and waste disposal bag
routes.detailedRack: Light alpine trad rack if rope is carried: small nuts (sizes 1-3), micro cams (0.5-1.5"), slings, and a belay device. Anchors are rarely bombed, but horn features and large flakes on the ridge accept gear well given the solid olivine composition. Most commonly, parties will build a rappel anchor on a large flake or horn near the high notch if they choose to rappel one or two pitches. A 60-meter rope allows one rappel of ~80-90 feet if rigged with intermediate anchors.
routes.proNeeds: Rope is optional for competent scramblers but provides security on Class 3-4 terrain with significant exposure. If roping, set up intermediate anchors on horn features or large flakes. The olivine rock is solid; protect placements hold well. Most parties climb un-roped and downclimb exposed sections, which requires high confidence and competence. A rappel rope is more useful than a belay setup on this scramble route. Rescue evacuation from the ridge proper is extremely difficult and time-consuming; commitment to safe climbing is paramount.

timing.recommendedStart: 0430-0500 hours (4:30-5:00 AM) for maximum daylight on exposed ridge sections.

climate.typical: Highly variable maritime climate with rapid changes. Early morning often clear (7-9 AM), afternoon thunderstorms common (12-4 PM). Westerly winds off the Pacific. Summit temperatures 40-50°F even in summer; base 60°F. Plan for rain even if forecast is clear.
climate.spring: (May-early June) High avalanche danger, heavy wet snow on approach talus. Snow on ridge crest makes scrambling dangerous. Not recommended unless climber has advanced snow travel skills.
climate.summer: (July-August) Most stable window; best month is July. Early morning clear; expect afternoon clouds by 1-2 PM. Occasional showers. Winds light to moderate. Temperatures 45-55°F summit. Summit success rate ~60-70%.
climate.fall: (September-October) Improving stability early fall (Sept), increasing variability late fall (Oct). September offers excellent conditions. October mountain freezing level drops; frost on morning rock complicates scrambling.
climate.winter: Not a safe climbing window. November-April: avalanche terrain is active, rockfall is extreme (frost-thaw cycles loosen every rock), lightning risk is elevated. Avoid entirely.

access.landManager: USDA Forest Service (Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest, Mt. Baker District)
access.fees: No climbing fee. Forest Service parking may require a Northwest Forest Pass (available at ranger stations or online).
access.permit: No permit required for this climb. Mount Baker Wilderness area is open to the public without permits.
access.passRequired: Possibly, depending on parking location. Confirm with Mt. Baker Ranger District (360) 856-5700. The gated private road (Olivine Corp.) may require pre-arrangement.
access.closures: No official seasonal closures, but alpine access is limited by snow and avalanche conditions November-May. Route is effectively closed by conditions rather than formal closure.

routes.corrections: Elevation corrected from database value of 7,460 ft to accurate 7,004 ft per USGS and Peakbagger NAVD 88. This route is the more technical version of the South Twin summit. First ascent date (August 1, 1891) applies to peak, not specifically to this ridge line, but is the only documented first ascent of South Twin Sister.

hierarchyNote: CRITICAL: No "Mount Brunswick" exists at 8,645 feet in Washington state. All authoritative sources (Peakbagger, Mountain Project, SummitPost, Wikipedia) document only Brunswick Mountain in British Columbia, Canada at 1,788m (5,866 ft). No Washington peak with this name or elevation exists. The routes wa_brunswick_standard and wa_brunswick_north_approach are not documented in any climbing database. This record requires manual verification and correction - peak may be misidentified, mislocated, or have incorrect elevation data.

UNABLE TO VERIFY: Despite exhaustive search of authoritative sources (Mountain Project, SummitPost, PeakBagger, Cascade Alpine Guide, Climber.org, AllTrails, The Mountaineers, climbing forums, and peak databases), no peak named "Cabin Creek Peak" at 8,316 feet in Washington could be found. Searches revealed a Cabin Creek Peak in Idaho (9,968 ft, Tango Peaks), Cabin Mountain in Washington (4,813 ft), and Castle Peak in Washington (8,306-8,343 ft), but no Washington peak matching the given name and elevation. This peak may need verification against authoritative USGS or Peakbagger direct database access, or the data may refer to a peak under an alternate name.
